**Definition:** A system in which large quantities of a **standardized** product are manufactured continuously on a **production line**, with each operation performing a small, repetitive task on every unit as it moves through the line.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Volume:** High — thousands to millions of identical units.
- **Variety:** Very low — typically one or a few product variants.
- **Workforce:** Semi-skilled / unskilled; tasks are highly specialized.
- **Layout:** **Product layout** (assembly line), machines arranged in sequence of operations.
- **Capital Investment:** High — special-purpose machines, transfer lines, automated equipment.
- **Lead Time:** Short for the line (hours).
- **Inventory:** Finished goods buffer stock to meet demand fluctuations.
- **Examples:** Passenger cars (Maruti Swift line), smartphones, packaged biscuits, electric bulbs, ballpoint pens, bottled water.

**The Line Balancing Equation:**

The **cycle time (CT)** of a mass-production line is the maximum time allowed at each workstation:

$$
CT \;=\; \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Daily Demand}}
$$

The **theoretical minimum number of workstations** is:

$$
N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}}{CT}
$$

Where $t_i$ is the standard time for task $i$, and $n$ is the total number of tasks.

The **line efficiency (η)** is:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{i}}{N_{\text{actual}} \cdot CT} \times 100 \,\%
$$

The **balance delay (BD)** is:

$$
BD \;=\; 1 - \eta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality Check:** In automotive final assembly (e.g., Hyundai's Chennai plant), cycle times are typically 60–90 seconds per vehicle, with 30–50 workstations and line efficiencies of 85–95%.

**Advantages of Mass Production:**
1. Very low unit cost (economies of scale).
2. Shorter production lead time per unit.
3. Less skilled labor required per workstation.
4. Consistent and uniform quality.

**Disadvantages of Mass Production:**
1. Very high capital investment.
2. Inflexible — design changes are extremely expensive.
3. Boring, repetitive work → low employee morale.
4. Breakdown of a single station halts the entire line.

### 2.4 Continuous Production

**Definition:** A system in which production proceeds **without interruption**, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, for months or years, transforming raw materials into a continuous flow of product. The output is typically measured in **kilograms, liters, cubic meters, or moles** rather than discrete units.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Volume:** Extremely high.
- **Variety:** Almost zero — the process is locked to one chemistry or one formulation.
- **Workforce:** Highly automated; small number of process operators and control-room staff.
- **Layout:** **Process layout** with continuous flow through pipes, reactors, conveyors.
- **Capital Investment:** Enormous.
- **Lead Time:** Determined by the *residence time* of material in the process; production rate is fixed.
- **Inventory:** Buffers are in the form of in-process storage tanks, silos, or intermediate holding vessels.
- **Examples:** Oil refineries, ammonia plants, cement plants, steel mills (blast furnace), paper mills, electricity generation, LNG plants.

**The Continuous Flow Equation:**

The mass output rate of a continuous process is given by the fundamental flow equation:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \rho \cdot A \cdot v
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}$ = Mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid/material (kg/m³)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- $v$ = Linear velocity (m/s)

For a chemical reactor of volume $V$ operating at a space time $\tau$:

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{V}{\dot{V}} \;=\; \frac{V \cdot C_{A0}}{F_{A0}}
$$

Where $\dot{V}$ is the volumetric flow rate, $C_{A0}$ is inlet concentration, and $F_{A0}$ is the inlet molar flow rate.

**Advantages of Continuous Production:**
1. Lowest possible unit cost.
2. Highly automated → minimal manual labor.
3. Stable, predictable product quality.
4. No downtime losses for changeovers.

**Disadvantages of Continuous Production:**
1. Astronomical capital investment.
2. Extremely inflexible — designed for one product.
3. High cost of shutdown and startup.
4. A breakdown can be catastrophic (safety, environmental, financial).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Job Production | Batch Production | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Very Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Variety** | Very High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| **Capital Investment** | Low | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Unit Cost** | Very High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| **Lead Time / Unit** | Very Long | Moderate | Short | Fixed by process |
| **Skill Level** | High | Moderate | Low | Specialized |
| **Layout** | Fixed-position | Process / Cellular | Product (Line) | Process (Pipeline) |
| **Inventory Type** | Raw material only | WIP + Finished | Finished goods | In-process buffer |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | High | Low | Lowest |
| **Examples** | Ship, prototype | Bakery, pharma | Cars, phones | Refinery, cement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Batch Quantity (EBQ)

This is the single most-asked derivation in the IEPM module. The EBQ is derived by minimizing the total annual inventory cost, which has two components: **(i) Annual Setup Cost** and **(ii) Annual Holding Cost**.

**Step 1: Set up the variables and assumptions.**

Let:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = Batch size (units/batch)
- $S$ = Setup cost incurred once per batch (₹/setup)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $C$ = Unit purchase/production cost (₹/unit)

**Step 2: Express the Annual Setup Cost.**

The number of setups per year is $\frac{D}{Q}$. Therefore, the annual setup cost is:

$$
\text{Setup Cost} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q} \times S
$$

**Step 3: Express the Annual Holding Cost.**

For a batch that is consumed uniformly, the average inventory is $\frac{Q}{2}$ (assuming instantaneous replenishment and no safety stock). Therefore:

$$
\text{Holding Cost} \;=\; \frac{Q}{2} \times H
$$

**Step 4: Write the Total Annual Cost (excluding the constant $D \times C$ term).**

$$
TC(Q) \;=\; \frac{D S}{Q} \;+\; \frac{H Q}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ and equate to zero for the minimum.**

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} \;=\; -\frac{D S}{Q^{2}} \;+\; \frac{H}{2} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $Q$.**

$$
\frac{H}{2} \;=\; \frac{D S}{Q^{2}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad Q^{2} \;=\; \frac{2 D S}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}\;}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute $Q^{*}$ back into $TC(Q)$ to get the minimum total cost.**

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \frac{D S}{\sqrt{2 D S / H}} \;+\; \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D^{2} S^{2} H}{2 D S}} \;+\; \sqrt{\frac{H^{2} D S}{2 H}}
$$

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}} \;+\; \sqrt{\frac{D S H}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{2 D S H}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Conditions (must be stated in the KTU answer):**
> (1) Demand rate is constant and known.
> (2) Replenishment is instantaneous (lead time = 0 or replenishment is very fast).
> (3) No stock-outs are permitted.
> (4) Setup cost and holding cost are constant and independent of $Q$.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Production System

A frequently asked 14-mark question is *"Given certain conditions, which production system is most suitable?"* The following decision matrix provides a structured framework:

| Condition / Parameter | Recommended Production System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Demand volume < 100 units/year, customer-specific | **Job Production** | Low volume, high variety; customization is paramount. |
| Demand 100 – 10,000 units/year, 5–50 variants | **Batch Production** | Moderate volume & variety; EBQ logic applies. |
| Demand > 100,000 units/year, 1–5 variants | **Mass Production** | High volume, low variety; assembly-line economy. |
| Demand measured in kg/hr, 24×7 operation, single product | **Continuous Production** | Process locked to one chemistry; non-stop flow. |
| Heavy/immovable product (ship, bridge) | **Job Production** | Fixed-position layout is mandatory. |
| Product is perishable (milk, bread) | **Continuous or Mass** | Long lead times are unacceptable. |
| Frequent design changes expected | **Job or Batch** | Mass/Continuous are inflexible. |
| Capital is constrained | **Job or Batch** | Mass/Continuous need huge CAPEX. |
| Product must meet tight tolerances at scale | **Mass or Continuous** | Standardization yields consistency. |

### 3.3 Worked Example — EBQ Calculation (KTU Standard Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]:**
> A company requires **12,000 units/year** of a component. The setup cost per batch is **₹450** and the holding cost is **₹6 per unit per year**. Determine (a) the Economic Batch Quantity, (b) the number of setups per year, and (c) the minimum total annual inventory cost.

**Given:**
- $D = 12{,}000$ units/year
- $S = \text{₹}450$/setup
- $H = \text{₹}6$/unit/year

**Solution:**

**(a) EBQ calculation:**

$$
Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 450}{6}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{10{,}800{,}000}{6}} \;=\; \sqrt{1{,}800{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q^{*} \;=\; 1341.64 \;\approx\; 1342 \text{ units per batch}\;}
$$

**(b) Number of setups per year:**

$$
N_{\text{setups}} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{12{,}000}{1342} \;\approx\; 8.94 \;\approx\; 9 \text{ setups/year}
$$

**(c) Minimum total annual inventory cost:**

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{2 D S H} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 450 \times 6}
$$

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{64{,}800{,}000} \;=\; 8050.78
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\min} \;\approx\; \text{₹}8{,}051 \text{ per year}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Optimality:** The setup cost at $Q^{*}$ is $\frac{D S}{Q^{*}} = \frac{12{,}000 \times 450}{1342} = \text{₹}4{,}024$. The holding cost at $Q^{*}$ is $\frac{H Q^{*}}{2} = \frac{6 \times 1342}{2} = \text{₹}4{,}026$. Both are equal to $\text{₹}\sqrt{D S H / 2} \approx \text{₹}4{,}025$, and their sum is $\text{₹}8{,}050 \approx TC_{\min}$. ✓ **Optimality confirmed.**

### 3.4 Line Balancing Worked Example (Mass Production Module)

> **Problem:** An assembly line has 8 tasks with the following times (in seconds): 40, 30, 25, 35, 20, 45, 28, 32. If the daily demand is 400 units and the available time is 480 minutes/day, find (a) cycle time, (b) theoretical minimum number of workstations, and (c) line efficiency assuming the balance is achieved optimally.

**Solution:**

**(a) Cycle time:**

$$
CT \;=\; \frac{480 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min}}{400 \text{ units}} \;=\; \frac{28{,}800}{400} \;=\; 72 \text{ s}
$$

**(b) Theoretical minimum number of workstations:**

Total task time:

$$
\sum t_i \;=\; 40 + 30 + 25 + 35 + 20 + 45 + 28 + 32 \;=\; 255 \text{ s}
$$

$$
N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\sum t_i}{CT} \;=\; \frac{255}{72} \;=\; 3.54 \;\Longrightarrow\; 4 \text{ workstations}
$$

**(c) Line efficiency at theoretical optimum:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{255}{4 \times 72} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{255}{288} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 88.54\%
$$

The balance delay is $100\% - 88.54\% = 11.46\%$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Product-Process Matrix (Hayes & Wheelwright Framework)

This is the most-tested visual framework in KTU Module 3. The matrix cross-references the **product life cycle stage** with the **process type** to reveal the most cost-effective production system choice.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STAGE 1: PRODUCT INTRODUCTION"] --> A1["Low Volume, High Variety"]
    A1 --> A2["JOB PRODUCTION"]
    A1 --> A3["Project-based workshops"]

    B["STAGE 2: PRODUCT GROWTH"] --> B1["Rising Volume, Reducing Variety"]
    B1 --> B2["BATCH PRODUCTION"]
    B1 --> B3["Disconnected line flow / Cells"]

    C["STAGE 3: PRODUCT MATURITY"] --> C1["High Volume, Standardized"]
    C1 --> C2["MASS PRODUCTION"]
    C1 --> C3["Connected assembly line"]

    D["STAGE 4: PRODUCT DECLINE"] --> D1["Falling Volume"]
    D1 --> D2["Return to BATCH or JOB"]
    D1 --> D3["Repurpose machinery"]

    A2 -.->|"Volume rises"| B1
    B2 -.->|"Standardization sets in"| C1
    C2 -.->|"Demand wanes"| D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Move from the *top-left* to the *bottom-right* of the matrix as a product moves from introduction → growth → maturity. A *mismatch* (e.g., using job production for a mature product) is a strategic error that KTU examiners frequently test.

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Choosing a Production System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START: Define Product and Market"] --> S1["What is the annual demand volume?"]
    S1 -->|"< 100 units"| S2["Is each unit unique and customized?"]
    S1 -->|"100 - 10,000 units"| S3["Are there multiple variants or models?"]
    S1 -->|"> 10,000 units"| S4["Is the product flow continuous non-stop?"]

    S2 -->|"Yes"| S5["JOB PRODUCTION: Fixed-Position Layout"]
    S2 -->|"No"| S3

    S3 -->|"Yes, periodic changeovers"| S6["BATCH PRODUCTION: Cellular / Process Layout"]
    S3 -->|"No, standardized"| S4

    S4 -->|"Yes, 24x7 flow process"| S7["CONTINUOUS PRODUCTION: Process Layout"]
    S4 -->|"No, discrete units on a line"| S8["MASS PRODUCTION: Product Line Layout"]

    S5 --> S9["Examples: Ships, custom tooling"]
    S6 --> S10["Examples: Bakery, pharmaceuticals, machined parts"]
    S7 --> S11["Examples: Oil refinery, cement, ammonia, LNG"]
    S8 --> S12["Examples: Automobiles, smartphones, FMCG packaged goods"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Production System

This block-level diagram maps the **interactions** between the four classical production systems and the four key resources (Men, Machines, Materials, Methods — the **4M** framework of Industrial Engineering).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RESOURCES["4M RESOURCES"]
        M1["MEN - Workforce"]
        M2["MACHINES - Equipment"]
        M3["MATERIALS - Inputs"]
        M4["METHODS - Process Plans"]
    end

    subgraph SYSTEMS["PRODUCTION SYSTEMS"]
        P1["JOB PRODUCTION"]
        P2["BATCH PRODUCTION"]
        P3["MASS PRODUCTION"]
        P4["CONTINUOUS PRODUCTION"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT METRICS"]
        O1["Volume Q"]
        O2["Unit Cost"]
        O3["Lead Time"]
        O4["Flexibility Index"]
    end

    M1 --> P1
    M1 --> P2
    M1 --> P3
    M1 --> P4
    M2 --> P1
    M2 --> P2
    M2 --> P3
    M2 --> P4
    M3 --> P1
    M3 --> P2
    M3 --> P3
    M3 --> P4
    M4 --> P1
    M4 --> P2
    M4 --> P3
    M4 --> P4

    P1 -->|"Low Q, High Flexibility"| O1
    P1 -->|"Highest Unit Cost"| O2
    P1 -->|"Longest Lead Time"| O3
    P1 -->|"Max Flexibility"| O4

    P4 -->|"Highest Q, Lowest Variety"| O1
    P4 -->|"Lowest Unit Cost"| O2
    P4 -->|"Process-Determined"| O3
    P4 -->|"Min Flexibility"| O4
```

### 4.4 Cost Behavior Across Production Systems (Conceptual Graph)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph X["PRODUCTION VOLUME (units per year)"]
        direction LR
        X1["Job: ~10"] --> X2["Batch: ~10,000"] --> X3["Mass: ~1,000,000"] --> X4["Continuous: Tons per day"]
    end

    subgraph Y["UNIT COST BEHAVIOR"]
        direction LR
        Y1["Very High per Unit"] --> Y2["Moderate per Unit"] --> Y3["Low per Unit"] --> Y4["Lowest per Unit"]
    end

    X1 -.-> Y1
    X2 -.-> Y2
    X3 -.-> Y3
    X4 -.-> Y4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Whenever the question includes a graph or chart in the syllabus, KTU examiners expect the student to **draw and label** it in the answer script. A neat ASCII or hand-drawn version of the above cost-volume trade-off always earns full method marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between Job Production and Batch Production.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Job Production involves manufacturing *one unit* (or a very small number) at a time, with each product being **unique and customer-specific**. Examples include custom-built ships and prototypes. Batch Production, in contrast, involves manufacturing a *group of identical units* in a single setup, after which the equipment is reset to produce the next batch of a different variant. Examples include pharmaceutical tablets and bakery products.

**Key Distinctions (for the 3rd mark):**
- **Volume:** Job = 1 (very low); Batch = moderate (50 to thousands).
- **Variety:** Job = very high; Batch = moderate.
- **Changeover:** Job has no changeover; Batch incurs periodic setup/changeover time.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*What is Continuous Production? Give two examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Continuous Production is a production system in which goods are manufactured **without interruption, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week**, for extended periods (months to years). The output is typically measured in *mass or volume units* (kg, m³, liters) rather than discrete units. The process flow is locked to a single product or product family, and the production rate is governed by the residence time in the process equipment.

**Two Examples (for the remaining 2 marks):**
1. **Oil refinery** — crude oil is continuously fractionated into petrol, diesel, kerosene, etc.
2. **Cement plant** — limestone, clay, and gypsum are continuously processed through a rotary kiln to produce clinker and then cement.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the characteristics, advantages, disadvantages, and suitable layout for **Job Production** and **Batch Production** systems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing company has an annual demand of **24,000 units** for a component. The setup cost per batch is **₹800**, and the holding cost is **₹10 per unit per year**. Determine:
   (i) The Economic Batch Quantity.
   (ii) The number of batches per year.
   (iii) The minimum total annual inventory cost.
   (iv) The time between two consecutive setups, assuming 300 working days per year. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Characteristics, Advantages, Disadvantages, and Layouts (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Job Production | Batch Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume & Variety** | Very low volume, very high variety; each product is unique. | Moderate volume, moderate variety; runs of similar items. |
| **Workforce** | Highly skilled craftsmen, designers, specialists. | Semi-skilled operators; can be trained on a family of machines. |
| **Layout Type** | **Fixed-position layout** (ship, bridge) OR **Process/Functional layout** for smaller jobs. | **Process layout** with batch scheduling OR **Group Technology / Cellular layout**. |
| **Capital Investment** | Low; general-purpose machines and hand tools. | Moderate; some special jigs and fixtures. |
| **Lead Time** | Very long (weeks–months). | Moderate (days–weeks). |
| **Inventory** | Minimal raw material; zero finished goods. | WIP between operations; raw material and finished goods stock. |
| **Examples** | Shipbuilding, dam construction, prototypes, custom garments. | Bakery products, pharmaceutical tablets, machined components. |

**Advantages of Job Production:** (i) High customization, (ii) Easy design modification, (iii) Low capital investment, (iv) High skill development for workers.

**Disadvantages of Job Production:** (i) High unit cost, (ii) Complex production planning, (iii) Long lead times, (iv) Underutilization of machines and labor.

**Advantages of Batch Production:** (i) Better machine utilization than job, (ii) Lower unit cost via learning curve, (iii) Moderate flexibility.

**Disadvantages of Batch Production:** (i) Changeover/downtime losses, (ii) Higher WIP, (iii) Larger lead time than mass production.

---

**(b) EBQ Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 24{,}000$ units/year, $S = \text{₹}800$/setup, $H = \text{₹}10$/unit/year.

**(i) EBQ:**

$$
Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 800}{10}} \;=\; \sqrt{3{,}840{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q^{*} \;\approx\; 1959.59 \;\approx\; 1960 \text{ units per batch}\;}
$$

**(ii) Number of batches per year:**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}} \;=\; \frac{24{,}000}{1960} \;\approx\; 12.24 \;\approx\; 13 \text{ batches/year}
$$

**(iii) Minimum total annual inventory cost:**

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{2 D S H} \;=\; \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 800 \times 10} \;=\; \sqrt{384{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;TC_{\min} \;\approx\; \text{₹}19{,}596 \text{ per year}\;}
$$

**(iv) Time between setups (cycle time for production planning):**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Working days/year}}{N} \;=\; \frac{300}{12.24}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T_{\text{cycle}} \;\approx\; 24.51 \text{ days between consecutive setups}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> (1) Do **not forget to state the assumptions** of the EBQ model — constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stock-outs. Examiners explicitly allocate 1–2 marks for assumptions.
> (2) When the question asks for "minimum total cost," compute the *inventory-only* cost (setup + holding). Do not multiply by the unit production cost unless explicitly asked.
> (3) Always **carry the units** through the calculation; losing unit marks is a common KTU error.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **Mass Production** and **Continuous Production** systems under the following heads: volume, variety, layout, capital investment, lead time, workforce skill, inventory, and examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mass-production assembly line has **6 tasks** with standard times of **45, 30, 25, 20, 35, and 25 seconds** respectively. The daily demand is **300 units** and the plant operates **8 hours per day** with **one shift**. Determine:
   (i) The cycle time.
   (ii) The theoretical minimum number of workstations.
   (iii) The line efficiency if the line is balanced optimally.
   (iv) The balance delay. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Mass vs. Continuous Production (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Mass Production | Continuous Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | High (thousands to millions of discrete units). | Extremely high (output measured in kg, m³, or tons). |
| **Variety** | Low (1–5 standardized variants). | Almost zero (single chemistry or formulation). |
| **Layout** | **Product layout** (assembly line) — machines arranged in process sequence. | **Process layout** with continuous flow through pipes, reactors, conveyors. |
| **Capital Investment** | High — special-purpose machines, transfer lines, automation. | Very high — entire plants cost millions to billions. |
| **Lead Time** | Short per unit (hours) — fixed by line cycle time. | Fixed by the *residence time* of material in the process. |
| **Workforce Skill** | Semi-skilled / unskilled; tasks specialized and repetitive. | Highly trained process operators; small workforce relative to output. |
| **Inventory** | Finished goods buffer stock to meet demand swings. | In-process buffer tanks, silos, intermediate holding vessels. |
| **Examples** | Automobiles (Maruti Swift line), smartphones, biscuits, electric bulbs. | Oil refinery, ammonia plant, cement, steel, LNG, paper. |

**Distinguishing Phrase (for the final mark):** Mass production deals with **discrete units** on a line; continuous production deals with an **unbroken flow of material** in a process. Mass production can be shut down between shifts; continuous production is intended to run 24×7 for years.

---

**(b) Line Balancing Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** 6 tasks with times $t_1 = 45, t_2 = 30, t_3 = 25, t_4 = 20, t_5 = 35, t_6 = 25$ seconds. Daily demand = 300 units. Available time = 8 hours/day = 28,800 s/day.

**(i) Cycle time:**

$$
CT \;=\; \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Daily Demand}} \;=\; \frac{8 \times 3600}{300} \;=\; \frac{28{,}800}{300} \;=\; 96 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical minimum number of workstations:**

$$
\sum t_i \;=\; 45 + 30 + 25 + 20 + 35 + 25 \;=\; 180 \text{ s}
$$

$$
N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\sum t_i}{CT} \;=\; \frac{180}{96} \;=\; 1.875 \;\Longrightarrow\; 2 \text{ workstations}
$$

**(iii) Line efficiency at theoretical optimum:**

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{\text{actual}} \times CT} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{180}{2 \times 96} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta \;=\; \frac{180}{192} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 93.75\%\;}
$$

**(iv) Balance delay:**

$$
BD \;=\; 1 - \eta \;=\; 1 - 0.9375
$$

$$
\boxed{\;BD \;=\; 0.0625 \text{ or } 6.25\%\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Line Balancing):**
> (1) Always convert **hours to seconds** (multiply by 3600) before calculating cycle time. The most common error is using minutes, leading to a 60× wrong answer.
> (2) $N_{\min}$ must **always be rounded UP** to the next integer. Writing 1.875 directly is incorrect — the line physically needs 2 workstations.
> (3) Line efficiency > 100% is **impossible**; if you compute such a value, you have made a unit error. Re-check the calculation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 3:**

- ✅ **Four classical production systems:** Job → Batch → Mass → Continuous, in that order.
- ✅ **Inverse relationship:** As volume increases, variety decreases.
- ✅ **Job Production:** Lowest volume, highest variety, longest lead time, **fixed-position layout** for large projects.
- ✅ **Batch Production:** Uses the **EBQ formula** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$; incurs setup and holding cost trade-off.
- ✅ **EBQ Assumptions:** Constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no shortages, constant $S$ and $H$.
- ✅ **Mass Production:** **Product (line) layout**, cycle time $CT = \text{Available Time} / \text{Daily Demand}$.
- ✅ **Line Efficiency:** $\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{\text{actual}} \times CT} \times 100\%$. Always $\leq 100\%$.
- ✅ **Continuous Production:** **24×7 operation**, output measured in mass/volume units, fixed by **residence time** and **space time**.
- ✅ **Product-Process Matrix (Hayes & Wheelwright):** Aligns the production system with the stage of the product life cycle.
- ✅ **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Job for very-low-volume customized; Batch for moderate volume/variety; Mass for high-volume standardized; Continuous for process-flow bulk.
- ✅ **Memorize two real-world examples** for *each* type — this is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question in the KTU board exam.
- ✅ **Carry units throughout calculations** to avoid losing method marks.
- ✅ **Always state the assumptions** when deriving or applying the EBQ model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging Trends in Production Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emerging Trends in Production Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Emerging Trends in Production Management** refer to the contemporary technological, organizational, and strategic transformations that are reshaping the way manufacturing and service enterprises plan, organize, direct, and control their production operations. These trends integrate **cyber-physical systems**, **digital technologies**, **sustainable practices**, and **intelligent automation** into traditional production management frameworks to achieve higher efficiency, flexibility, customization, and sustainability.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic encompasses the convergence of the **Fourth Industrial Revolution (Industry 4.0)** with classical production management functions — Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, and Controlling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Module 3)**
> Emerging trends in production management cover the paradigm shift from mass production to **smart, connected, data-driven, and sustainable production ecosystems** — characterized by real-time decision-making, decentralized control, and human-machine collaboration.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traditional factory** as a large orchestra where every musician (machine) plays from a fixed sheet of music (production schedule), the conductor (manager) controls everything from the front, and any mistake in one section can disrupt the entire performance.

Now imagine a **modern smart factory** as a **jazz ensemble with AI headphones**:
- Each musician (smart machine) listens to the overall rhythm in real-time
- They **self-adjust** their tempo and notes based on sensors and data
- The conductor (manager) now acts as a **collaborative guide** rather than a strict controller
- If one musician falters, the others **adapt dynamically** without stopping the show
- The audience (customer) can even request **personalized variations** of the song

This is exactly what emerging trends like **IoT, AI, Digital Twins, and Cyber-Physical Systems** do — they transform rigid, hierarchical production into a **flexible, intelligent, and responsive network**.

---

## 1.3 Key Emerging Trends (Overview)

| # | Trend | Core Idea |
|---|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing** | Cyber-physical production systems |
| 2 | **Internet of Things (IoT)** | Connected machines exchanging real-time data |
| 3 | **Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning** | Self-learning production optimization |
| 4 | **Digital Twins** | Virtual replicas of physical processes |
| 5 | **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing)** | Layer-by-layer part fabrication |
| 6 | **Cloud Manufacturing** | On-demand manufacturing resources via cloud |
| 7 | **Big Data Analytics** | Data-driven decision-making |
| 8 | **Sustainable / Green Manufacturing** | Eco-friendly, zero-waste production |
| 9 | **Robotics & Collaborative Robots (Cobots)** | Human-robot collaboration |
| 10 | **Augmented Reality (AR) / Virtual Reality (VR)** | Immersive training & assembly |
| 11 | **Blockchain in Production** | Transparent, tamper-proof supply chain |
| 12 | **Predictive Maintenance** | Failure prediction using sensor data |
| 13 | **Lean-Agile Manufacturing** | Waste elimination + flexibility |
| 14 | **Mass Customization** | Personalized products at mass-production cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** The **Industry 4.0 design principles** as defined by the German Plattform Industrie 4.0 are: **Interconnection, Information Transparency, Technical Assistance, and Decentralized Decisions**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters (Industry Perspective)

- The global **smart manufacturing market** is projected to exceed **USD 700 billion by 2030**.
- Manufacturers adopting Industry 4.0 report **20–30%** gains in productivity and **15–25%** reduction in downtime.
- The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes this topic to prepare engineers for **Industry 5.0** (human-centric, sustainable, resilient).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Industry 4.0 — The Foundational Framework

Industry 4.0 represents the **Fourth Industrial Revolution** and is the umbrella under which most emerging trends operate.

### The Four Design Principles of Industry 4.0

1. **Interconnection** — Machines, devices, sensors, and people connect and communicate via IoT.
2. **Information Transparency** — Systems create a virtual copy of the physical world through sensor data.
3. **Technical Assistance** — Systems support humans in decision-making and physically assist in tasks.
4. **Decentralized Decisions** — Cyber-physical systems make decisions on their own; humans intervene only at exceptions.

### The Why & How

- **Why:** Traditional production is rigid, siloed, and reactive. Industry 4.0 makes it **connected, transparent, predictive, and adaptive**.
- **How:** Through a stack of technologies — IoT (data collection), Cloud/Edge computing (data storage & processing), AI/ML (intelligence), and Digital Twins (simulation).

---

## 2.2 Internet of Things (IoT) in Production

IoT is a network of **physical devices embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity** that enables them to collect, exchange, and act on data.

**The "Why":** Traditional machines operate in isolation — managers only know about problems *after* they occur. IoT provides **real-time visibility** into machine health, energy use, and throughput.

**The "How":**
1. **Sensors** capture data (temperature, vibration, pressure, cycle time).
2. **Connectivity** (Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa) transmits data.
3. **Platform** (Cloud/Edge) processes data.
4. **Action** — alerts, automated adjustments, or maintenance triggers.

---

## 2.3 Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning in Production

- **AI** is the broader concept of machines performing tasks that typically require human intelligence.
- **ML** is a subset of AI where systems learn from data without being explicitly programmed.

**Production Applications:**
- Demand forecasting
- Quality defect detection (computer vision)
- Process parameter optimization
- Predictive maintenance
- Dynamic scheduling

---

## 2.4 Digital Twins

A **Digital Twin** is a dynamic, virtual replica of a physical product, process, or system that uses real-time sensor data to mirror its real-world counterpart.

**Why it matters:** Engineers can run **"what-if" simulations** on the digital twin before making changes in the physical world — reducing risk, cost, and time-to-market.

---

## 2.5 Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing)

Builds objects **layer-by-layer** from digital 3D models, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing (cutting/milling).

**Production Impact:**
- Eliminates tooling costs for prototypes
- Enables complex geometries impossible with traditional methods
- Supports **mass customization**
- Reduces inventory through **on-demand printing**

---

## 2.6 Cloud Manufacturing (CMfg)

A **service-oriented manufacturing model** where manufacturing resources (machines, software, capabilities) are encapsulated as cloud services and accessed on-demand over the internet.

---

## 2.7 Big Data Analytics in Production

Production environments generate **massive volumes** of data (terabytes/day). The **5 V's** of Big Data:
- **Volume** — scale of data
- **Velocity** — speed of data generation
- **Variety** — different data types
- **Veracity** — data quality/trustworthiness
- **Value** — actionable insight extracted

---

## 2.8 Sustainable / Green Manufacturing

Production that **minimizes negative environmental impact** while conserving energy and natural resources.

**Key Metrics:**
- **Carbon Footprint** (kg CO₂ equivalent per unit)
- **Material Utilization Rate**
- **Energy Intensity** (kWh per unit)
- **Waste Recycling Rate**

---

## 2.9 Lean-Agile Manufacturing

| Aspect | Lean | Agile |
|--------|------|-------|
| **Focus** | Waste elimination | Flexibility & responsiveness |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System | Software development |
| **Strength** | Efficiency | Adaptability |
| **Combined** | **Lean-Agile** = efficient AND flexible production |

---

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|------------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | A = Availability, P = Performance, Q = Quality (all as decimals) | Measures effectiveness of manufacturing equipment |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{Run\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time}$ | Run Time in hours | Equipment uptime ratio |
| **Performance** | $P = \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{Run\ Time}$ | Time in seconds/minutes | Speed efficiency |
| **Quality** | $Q = \dfrac{Good\ Count}{Total\ Count}$ | Unitless ratio | Good output ratio |
| **Productivity** | $\eta = \dfrac{Output}{Input}$ | Output/Input in matching units | Resource efficiency |
| **Material Utilization Rate** | $MUR = \dfrac{Material\ in\ Final\ Product}{Total\ Material\ Used} \times 100$ | Percentage | Green manufacturing KPI |
| **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{Total\ Energy\ Consumed\ (kWh)}{Units\ Produced}$ | kWh per unit | Sustainability metric |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{Units\ Passing\ First\ Inspection}{Total\ Units\ Inspected}$ | Decimal/percentage | Quality metric |
| **Predictive Maintenance Savings** | $S_{PM} = C_{breakdown} - C_{predictive}$ | Cost in currency | Maintenance cost reduction |
| **Industry 4.0 Maturity Index** | $M = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score (1–5) | Adoption assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Point:** OEE of **85%** is considered **World-Class**. A typical Indian manufacturing plant operates at **60–70%**. Improving OEE by even 5% can dramatically boost profitability.

---

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Tesla, BMW):** Digital twins for entire assembly lines; AI-driven quality inspection.
- **Aerospace (GE, Airbus):** 3D-printed titanium components, reducing weight by 40%.
- **Pharma:** IoT-enabled cold chain monitoring.
- **Electronics (Foxconn):** Collaborative robots (cobots) working alongside humans.
- **FMCG (Hindustan Unilever):** Cloud-based production planning across plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Framework: The Six Big Losses (OEE Foundation)

OEE analysis is the **gateway** to most smart manufacturing interventions. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this as a core analytical tool. Let us derive the OEE framework step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define Planned Production Time (PPT)

$$PPT = Total\ Available\ Time - Planned\ Downtime$$

*Reasoning:* We only count the time the plant *should* be running. Breaks, scheduled maintenance, and shift changes are excluded because they are planned.

### Step 2: Calculate Run Time (RT)

$$RT = PPT - Unplanned\ Downtime$$

*Reasoning:* Subtract the actual time equipment was *not* running due to breakdowns, changeovers, or material shortages.

### Step 3: Calculate Availability (A)

$$A = \dfrac{RT}{PPT}$$

*Reasoning:* Availability captures the **"Availability Loss"** — equipment failures and setup/changeover losses.

### Step 4: Calculate Performance (P)

$$P = \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{RT}$$

*Reasoning:* Performance captures **"Speed Losses"** — idling, minor stops, and reduced operating speed. Note that P is capped at 100% for OEE calculation (when actual speed exceeds ideal, cap at 100%).

### Step 5: Calculate Quality (Q)

$$Q = \dfrac{Good\ Count}{Total\ Count}$$

*Reasoning:* Quality captures **"Quality Losses"** — defects and rework that produce scrap.

### Step 6: Combine into OEE

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\[6pt]
&= \left(\dfrac{RT}{PPT}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{RT}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{Good\ Count}{Total\ Count}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Good\ Count}{PPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Reasoning:* This elegant simplification shows that **OEE = (Ideal time to produce good parts) / (Planned production time)**. The middle terms cancel out, making OEE a pure measure of manufacturing excellence.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: OEE Calculation

**Problem:** A machine has the following data for one shift:
- Total shift time = **8 hours** = **480 minutes**
- Breaks = **30 minutes**
- Unplanned downtime = **45 minutes**
- Total parts produced = **420 units**
- Good parts = **378 units**
- Ideal cycle time = **0.8 minutes/unit**

**Step 1: Planned Production Time**

$$PPT = 480 - 30 = 450\ minutes$$

**Step 2: Run Time**

$$RT = 450 - 45 = 405\ minutes$$

**Step 3: Availability**

$$
A = \dfrac{RT}{PPT} = \dfrac{405}{450} = 0.9000 = 90.00\%
$$

**Step 4: Performance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{RT} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.8 \times 420}{405} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{336}{405} = 0.8296 = 82.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Quality**

$$
Q = \dfrac{Good\ Count}{Total\ Count} = \dfrac{378}{420} = 0.9000 = 90.00\%
$$

**Step 6: OEE**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE &= A \times P \times Q \\[6pt]
&= 0.9000 \times 0.8296 \times 0.9000 \\[6pt]
&= 0.6719 = 67.19\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An OEE of **67.19%** is *typical* for Indian industry but far below the world-class benchmark of **85%**. The plant has significant room for improvement, particularly in **Performance**.

---

## 3.3 Six Big Losses — Mapping to OEE Components

| OEE Component | Loss Category | Specific Losses | Mitigation via Emerging Tech |
|---------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| **Availability** | Equipment Failure | Breakdowns, tool failures | **Predictive Maintenance** (AI/ML on sensor data) |
| **Availability** | Setup & Changeover | Long changeovers | **SMED** + IoT-enabled quick changeover |
| **Performance** | Idling & Minor Stops | Small stops, operator pauses | **Cobots** + real-time monitoring |
| **Performance** | Reduced Speed | Machine running below ideal | **AI-based process optimization** |
| **Quality** | Defects | Rework, scrap | **Computer vision** quality inspection |
| **Quality** | Startup Rejects | Defects at production start | **Digital twin** for ramp-up simulation |

---

## 3.4 Industry 4.0 Maturity Model (Analytical Matrix)

| Maturity Level | Name | Characteristics | Technology Stack |
|----------------|------|-----------------|------------------|
| Level 1 | **Computerization** | Basic automation, PLCs | SCADA, PLCs |
| Level 2 | **Connectivity** | Machines networked, data collection | IoT, M2M communication |
| Level 3 | **Visibility** | Real-time dashboards, transparency | MES, cloud platforms |
| Level 4 | **Transparency** | Contextualized information, root-cause analysis | Big Data, analytics |
| Level 5 | **Predictive** | Forecasting failures and quality issues | AI/ML, Digital Twins |
| Level 6 | **Adaptive** | Self-optimizing systems | Autonomous CPS, closed-loop control |

**Mathematical Representation of Maturity Score:**

$$
M = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot L_i}{6}
$$

where $L_i$ is the achieved level (1–6) and $w_i$ is the strategic weight for that dimension.

---

## 3.5 ROI Analysis for an Industry 4.0 Investment

**Step 1: Identify Costs**

$$C_{total} = C_{hardware} + C_{software} + C_{training} + C_{integration}$$

**Step 2: Identify Annual Benefits**

$$
B_{annual} = B_{downtime\ reduction} + B_{quality\ improvement} + B_{labor\ saving} + B_{energy\ saving}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Payback Period**

$$
Payback\ Period = \dfrac{C_{total}}{B_{annual}}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate ROI (3-year horizon)**

$$
ROI = \dfrac{3 \times B_{annual} - C_{total}}{C_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5: NPV (if discount rate $r$ is given)**

$$
NPV = -C_{total} + \sum_{t=1}^{3} \dfrac{B_{annual}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

This framework is **frequently asked** in KTU exams when discussing justification of Industry 4.0 investments.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Industry 4.0 Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Physical Layer: Machines, Sensors, Actuators, Robots] --> B1[Connectivity Layer: IoT, 5G, Industrial WiFi]
    B1 --> C1[Edge Layer: Edge Computing, Local Data Processing]
    C1 --> D1[Cloud Layer: Data Storage, Big Data Platform]
    D1 --> E1[Intelligence Layer: AI, ML, Analytics Engines]
    E1 --> F1[Application Layer: Digital Twin, MES, ERP, Dashboards]
    F1 --> G1[Decision Layer: Human Operator, Autonomous Control]
    G1 --> A1
    E1 --> H1[Services to Customers and Suppliers]
    F1 --> H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows the **closed-loop data flow** in a smart factory. Data flows *upward* (from physical to decision), while control actions flow *downward* (from decision to physical). The Intelligence Layer is the **brain** of Industry 4.0.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Industry 4.0. List its four design principles. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Model Answer:**

**Industry 4.0** refers to the ongoing transformation of traditional industrial processes through the integration of cyber-physical systems, the Internet of Things, cloud computing, cognitive computing, and artificial intelligence. It represents the **Fourth Industrial Revolution**, following mechanization (1.0), mass production (2.0), and automation/computers (3.0).

**The four design principles are:**
1. **Interconnection** — Connection between machines, devices, sensors, and people.
2. **Information Transparency** — Creating a virtual copy of the physical world through sensor data.
3. **Technical Assistance** — Systems support humans in decision-making and physically assist in tasks.
4. **Decentralized Decisions** — Cyber-physical systems make autonomous decisions.

---

### Question 2: What is a Digital Twin? Mention two of its production applications. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]

**Model Answer:**

A **Digital Twin** is a dynamic, real-time virtual replica of a physical product, process, or system that mirrors its real-world behavior using data from IoT sensors.

**Two production applications:**
1. **Process Simulation** — Running "what-if" scenarios on the virtual model to optimize parameters before physical implementation.
2. **Predictive Maintenance** — Monitoring real-time sensor data on the twin to predict component failures in the physical asset.
3. *(Bonus)* Product lifecycle management and remote monitoring of field-deployed equipment.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write generic answers like "Industry 4.0 is good for manufacturing." KTU examiners award marks for **specific examples, numerical data, and structured frameworks**.
> - In 14-mark questions, **always** include: (i) a definition, (ii) at least 2 examples, (iii) advantages with justification, (iv) challenges, and (v) a neat diagram/table.
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by skipping the **formula or quantitative aspect** in questions that have an analytical component.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Internet of Things (IoT) in Production Management**. Discuss its architecture with a neat diagram and enumerate **five benefits** of IoT adoption in a manufacturing plant. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The **Internet of Things (IoT)** in production management refers to a network of interconnected physical devices — sensors, actuators, machines, and controllers — embedded with electronics, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect, exchange, and act upon production data in real time without human-to-human or human-to-computer intervention.

**Architecture of IoT in Production (4 Layers):**

| Layer | Function | Example |
|-------|----------|---------|
| **1. Perception / Sensor Layer** | Captures physical data (temperature, vibration, pressure, position) | Temperature sensors on a CNC machine |
| **2. Network / Connectivity Layer** | Transmits data via wireless/wired protocols | Wi-Fi, 5G, MQTT, Modbus |
| **3. Processing / Edge-Cloud Layer** | Stores, processes, and analyses data | AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Edge gateways |
| **4. Application / Service Layer** | Delivers actionable insights to users | Dashboards, alerts, automated controls |

**Five Benefits of IoT in Manufacturing:**

1. **Real-time Equipment Monitoring** — Continuous tracking of machine health reduces unplanned downtime by up to **50%**.
2. **Predictive Maintenance** — Sensor data fed to AI models predicts failures *before* they occur, reducing maintenance costs by **20–30%**.
3. **Improved Quality Control** — In-line sensors detect deviations instantly, reducing defect rates.
4. **Energy Optimization** — Real-time energy monitoring identifies wasteful processes; potential savings of **10–15%**.
5. **Enhanced Supply Chain Visibility** — IoT-enabled tracking of materials from supplier to finished goods dispatch.

**Conclusion:** IoT forms the **data backbone** of Industry 4.0, enabling the transition from reactive to predictive and prescriptive production management.

---

**Q. (b)** A CNC machine operates for **2 shifts of 8 hours each** daily. The shift has **two 20-minute breaks**. Unplanned downtime is **1.5 hours/day**. The machine produces **800 parts/day** with **720 good parts**. The ideal cycle time is **40 seconds**. Calculate the **OEE** and identify the **biggest loss category**. Suggest a specific Industry 4.0 intervention. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Total available time = $2 \times 8 = 16\ hours = 960\ minutes$
- Planned breaks = $2 \times 20 = 40\ minutes$
- Unplanned downtime = $1.5\ hours = 90\ minutes$
- Total parts = **800**, Good parts = **720**
- Ideal cycle time = **40 seconds = 40/60 minutes ≈ 0.6667 minutes/part**

**Step 1: Planned Production Time (PPT)**

$$PPT = 960 - 40 = 920\ minutes$$

**Step 2: Run Time (RT)**

$$RT = 920 - 90 = 830\ minutes$$

**Step 3: Availability (A)**

$$
A = \dfrac{RT}{PPT} = \dfrac{830}{920} = 0.9022 = 90.22\%
$$

**Step 4: Performance (P)**

$$
P = \dfrac{Ideal\ Cycle\ Time \times Total\ Count}{RT} = \dfrac{0.6667 \times 800}{830} = \dfrac{533.33}{830} = 0.6426 = 64.26\%
$$

**Step 5: Quality (Q)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{Good\ Count}{Total\ Count} = \dfrac{720}{800} = 0.9000 = 90.00\%
$$

**Step 6: OEE**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.9022 \times 0.6426 \times 0.9000 = 0.5218 = 52.18\%
$$

**Analysis — Biggest Loss Category:**
- **Availability: 90.22%** (Loss = 9.78%)
- **Performance: 64.26%** (Loss = 35.74%) ← **BIGGEST**
- **Quality: 90.00%** (Loss = 10.00%)

The **Performance loss** is dominant — the machine is running but **35.74% slower than its ideal speed**, indicating **Idling, Minor Stops, or Reduced Speed**.

**Industry 4.0 Intervention:**
- Deploy **AI-based Machine Learning algorithms** on IoT sensor data (spindle speed, feed rate, vibration) to identify sub-optimal operating parameters in real time.
- Implement **Edge-computed anomaly detection** that automatically adjusts feed and speed to ideal values.
- Use a **Digital Twin** to simulate and eliminate speed-loss events.

**Expected Outcome:** Performance improvement to **90%+** can raise OEE to approximately **75%**, a substantial productivity gain.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Discuss the role of **Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML)** in modern production management. Explain any **four production applications** with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**
- **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is the branch of computer science that enables machines to perform tasks that typically require human intelligence — reasoning, learning, perception, and decision-making.
- **Machine Learning (ML)** is a subset of AI that uses algorithms to learn patterns from data and make predictions or decisions **without being explicitly programmed**.

**Why AI/ML in Production?**
Modern manufacturing generates massive datasets (machine parameters, quality records, supply chain events). Humans cannot analyze this in real time. AI/ML provides the **cognitive layer** of Industry 4.0.

**Four Production Applications:**

1. **Predictive Maintenance**
   - *Example:* General Electric uses ML on jet engine sensor data (vibration, temperature) to predict component failures **days in advance**, reducing unscheduled downtime by **30%**.

2. **Computer Vision-based Quality Inspection**
   - *Example:* BMW uses AI-powered cameras to detect paint defects and surface irregularities on car bodies with **99.9% accuracy**, far exceeding human inspection.

3. **Demand Forecasting & Production Planning**
   - *Example:* Amazon uses ML models to forecast demand at SKU-level, optimizing inventory and reducing stockouts by **20%**.

4. **Process Parameter Optimization**
   - *Example:* In semiconductor manufacturing, ML adjusts etching parameters in real time to maximize yield, improving First Pass Yield by **5–10%**.

**Conclusion:** AI/ML transforms production management from **descriptive** (what happened?) to **predictive** (what will happen?) and **prescriptive** (what should we do?).

---

**Q. (b)** Compare **Lean Manufacturing** and **Agile Manufacturing**. Discuss the concept of **Mass Customization** and explain how emerging technologies enable it. Use a suitable example. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table — Lean vs. Agile Manufacturing:**

| Parameter | Lean Manufacturing | Agile Manufacturing |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Eliminate waste (Muda) | Respond quickly to change |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System (TPS) | Software development / dynamic markets |
| **Focus** | Efficiency, cost reduction | Flexibility, speed-to-market |
| **Production Type** | High volume, low variety | Low volume, high variety / custom |
| **Inventory** | Minimal (JIT) | Buffer / strategic |
| **Lead Time** | Reduced via flow | Reduced via parallelization |
| **Best For** | Stable, predictable demand | Volatile, customized demand |
| **Limitations** | Brittle to demand changes | Higher cost per unit |

**Mass Customization:**
**Mass Customization** is a production strategy that delivers **personalized products** to customers at **near mass-production cost and efficiency**. It blends the **low cost of mass production** with the **flexibility of custom manufacturing**.

**How Emerging Technologies Enable Mass Customization:**

1. **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing):** Allows production of unique geometries per customer *without* retooling. *Example:* **Nike By You** lets customers design their own shoes, 3D-printed to spec.
2. **IoT & Data Analytics:** Captures individual customer preferences at scale for data-driven design.
3. **Flexible Robotics:** Cobots can be reprogrammed in minutes to assemble different variants on the same line.
4. **Cloud Manufacturing:** On-demand access to distributed production capacity for custom orders.
5. **Digital Twins:** Simulate customer-specific designs virtually before physical production, reducing trial cost.

**Example: Tesla's Configurable Cars**
Tesla's website lets a customer configure a Model 3 with custom color, wheels, interior, and software features. The same production line builds **thousands of unique configurations daily** using **AI-driven scheduling**, **IoT-enabled stations**, and **flexible robotics** — achieving mass customization at scale.

**Conclusion:** Emerging technologies dissolve the historic trade-off between **customization** and **scale**, enabling the **"Lot Size of 1"** paradigm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Emerging Trends in Production Management**

- [ ] **Industry 4.0** = Cyber-Physical Systems + IoT + AI + Cloud. Four design principles: **Interconnection, Information Transparency, Technical Assistance, Decentralized Decisions**.
- [ ] **IoT in Production** has a **4-layer architecture**: Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- [ ] **Digital Twin** = Real-time virtual replica of a physical asset/process.
- [ ] **Additive Manufacturing** (3D Printing) = Layer-by-layer; enables **mass customization**.
- [ ] **Cloud Manufacturing** = Manufacturing-as-a-Service via cloud platforms.
- [ ] **Big Data** has **5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- [ ] **AI/ML** drives: Predictive Maintenance, Computer Vision QC, Demand Forecasting, Process Optimization.
- [ ] **Cobots** = Collaborative Robots that work *with* humans (not isolated like traditional industrial robots).
- [ ] **AR/VR** is used in **training, assembly assistance, and maintenance** guidance.
- [ ] **Blockchain** provides **tamper-proof traceability** in supply chains.
- [ ] **Sustainable Manufacturing** KPIs: Carbon Footprint, Material Utilization Rate, Energy Intensity.
- [ ] **Lean vs. Agile:** Lean = waste elimination (stable demand); Agile = flexibility (volatile demand). Modern plants need **Lean-Agile hybrid**.
- [ ] **Industry 5.0** (next frontier) = **Human-centric**, sustainable, and resilient — emphasizing the *human* element beyond Industry 4.0's automation focus.
- [ ] **OEE Formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$. World-class benchmark = **85%**.
- [ ] **Six Big Losses:** Equipment Failure, Setup, Idling/Minor Stops, Reduced Speed, Defects, Startup Rejects.
- [ ] **Maturity Levels of Industry 4.0:** Computerization → Connectivity → Visibility → Transparency → Predictive → Adaptive.
- [ ] **Mass Customization** is enabled by Additive Manufacturing, IoT, AI, and Flexible Automation.
- [ ] **Cybersecurity in OT (Operational Technology)** is an **emerging critical concern** with increased connectivity.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions on this topic, structure your answer as **Definition → Architecture/Diagram → 4+ Applications with Examples → Comparison Table → Industry 4.0 Intervention → Conclusion**. This is the **highest-scoring template** for KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Management: Functions of Inventory, Inventory Problem Classification, Selective Inventory Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inventory Management: Functions of Inventory, Inventory Problem Classification, Selective Inventory Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Inventory?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Formal):**
> *Inventory* refers to the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, components, and other supplies held by an organization at a given point in time to meet future demand, smooth production, and decouple successive operations in the supply chain.

In the language of **Industrial Engineering**, inventory represents **idle resources** awaiting further processing or consumption. Although inventory is essentially an asset on the balance sheet, it simultaneously represents **locked-up working capital** and a **source of hidden costs** (storage, insurance, depreciation, obsolescence, and pilferage).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Reservoir Analogy"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Think of inventory as a **water reservoir** behind a dam.
> - The **dam** is your warehouse.
> - **Inflow** = incoming materials (orders, production output).
> - **Outflow** = customer demand, consumption in production.
> - The **water level** = on-hand inventory.
> - **Overflow** = excess inventory → waste, holding cost, obsolescence.
> - **Dry dam** = stockout → lost sales, production stoppage.
>
> The job of the inventory manager is to keep the water level **just right** — high enough to prevent drought, low enough to prevent flooding.

---

## 1.3 Functions of Inventory — The "WHY" of Holding Stock

Every organization holds inventory to fulfill **one or more strategic, operational, or financial functions**. KTU examiners frequently test this as a **3-mark short answer** or as a **7-mark part question**. Below are the **eight principal functions of inventory**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 8 Functions of Inventory (Master List)**

| # | Function | Engineering Purpose |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Meeting Demand** | Satisfy customer orders without delay |
| 2 | **Decoupling Operations** | Allow independent working of successive stages |
| 3 | **Buffering Uncertainty** | Absorb fluctuations in demand and supply |
| 4 | **Economies of Scale** | Leverage quantity discounts & lower per-unit ordering costs |
| 5 | **Pipeline / Transit Stock** | Cover goods in movement between locations |
| 6 | **Hedging Against Price Changes** | Buy when prices are low (speculative motive) |
| 7 | **Smoothing Production** | Maintain level production despite seasonal demand |
| 8 | **Avoiding Stockouts & Lost Sales** | Protect service level and goodwill |

### 1.4 Detailed Explanation of Each Function

### (i) Meeting Demand
The most fundamental function. Since **customer demand is rarely perfectly predictable**, finished-goods inventory ensures that orders can be fulfilled immediately. Without it, every sale would require a fresh production run — economically infeasible.

> **Example:** An automobile dealer keeps a stock of cars because customers will not wait 6 weeks for a fresh order.

### (ii) Decoupling Operations
Different stages of production (e.g., forging → machining → assembly) often operate at **different rates**. Inventory held *between* stages acts as a **buffer**, allowing each stage to run at its own optimal speed without waiting for the previous stage.

> **Example:** A printing press does not stop when the binding section has a minor delay — WIP inventory cushions the disruption.

### (iii) Buffering Uncertainty (Safety Stock Function)
Both **demand** and **supply lead times** vary randomly. Safety inventory protects against:
- Demand surges
- Supplier delays
- Quality rejections
- Transport disruptions

### (iv) Economies of Scale
Ordering in bulk reduces:
- Per-unit ordering cost
- Per-unit transportation cost
- Per-unit production setup cost (per EOQ logic)

Trade-off: Larger order quantities → higher **carrying cost**. Inventory theory balances these.

### (v) Pipeline / In-Transit Inventory
Goods moving from a distant supplier take days or weeks in transit. The shipment *itself* is an asset — **pipeline inventory**. It cannot be used but must be financed.

> **Example:** Crude oil in an ocean tanker from the Middle East to India.

### (vi) Hedging Against Price Changes
When prices are expected to **rise** (e.g., due to commodity inflation, tariffs, or seasonal trends), firms stockpile in advance.

> **Example:** Steel mills buying iron ore in bulk before an expected price hike.

### (vii) Smoothing Production (Level Production Strategy)
Demand is often **seasonal** (e.g., fans in summer, heaters in winter), but production is ideally **level** (constant workforce, no overtime, no layoffs). Inventory is built up in slack months and drawn down in peak months.

### (viii) Avoiding Stockouts
Stockouts cause:
- Lost sales
- Customer dissatisfaction
- Production line shutdowns (if critical spares are missing)
- Backorder costs

Inventory serves as an **insurance policy** against these consequences.

---

## 1.5 Inventory Problem Classification — Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:**
> Not all inventory problems are the same. KTU expects students to **classify** these problems along well-defined axes so that the **appropriate model** (EOQ, Economic Lot Size, Safety Stock, etc.) can be applied.

The classification rests on **four axes**:

1. **Demand Pattern** → Deterministic vs Probabilistic
2. **Lead Time Pattern** → Deterministic vs Probabilistic
3. **Review Mechanism** → Continuous Review (Q-system) vs Periodic Review (P-system)
4. **Number of Items / Echelons** → Single-Item vs Multi-Item; Single-Echelon vs Multi-Echelon

### 1.6 Selective Inventory Control — Conceptual Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> *Selective Inventory Control* is the practice of **classifying inventory items into logical groups** based on their **value, criticality, usage rate, or scarcity**, so that managerial attention, control effort, and resources can be **focused where they matter most** (typically, on the small percentage of items that account for the large percentage of value consumed — the **Pareto Principle**, 80/20 rule).

Common techniques covered in the KTU syllabus:
- **ABC Analysis** (Always Better Control) — based on Annual Consumption Value
- **VED Analysis** — Vital, Essential, Desirable (criticality-based)
- **FSN Analysis** — Fast, Slow, Non-moving (usage-rate-based)
- **SDE Analysis** — Scarce, Difficult, Easy (procurement-based)
- **HML Analysis** — High, Medium, Low (unit-price-based)
- **GOLF Analysis** — Government, Ordinary, Local, Foreign (source-based)
- **SOS Analysis** — Season, Off-Season (demand-pattern-based)

These techniques are typically applied in **combination** (e.g., ABC + VED, ABC + FSN) to give a multi-dimensional view.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Functions of Inventory — The Logic Tree

Below is a **structured logical decomposition** of why inventory exists in any industrial system:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inventory Function Logic Tree**

- **1. Demand-Side Functions**
    - Meet unpredictable customer orders
    - Cover seasonal demand spikes
    - Provide immediate availability (service level)
- **2. Supply-Side Functions**
    - Buffer against unreliable suppliers
    - Cover long procurement lead times
    - Allow bulk procurement discounts
- **3. Operations-Side Functions**
    - Decouple stages with different cycle times
    - Enable smooth, level production runs
    - Reduce setup frequency (large batch sizes)
- **4. Financial-Side Functions**
    - Hedge against future price inflation
    - Pipeline stock acts as moving collateral
- **5. Strategic-Side Functions**
    - Maintain safety stock against disruptions
    - Support Just-in-Time buffers
    - Provide flexibility in make-to-stock vs make-to-order decisions

## 2.2 Inventory Problem Classification — The Master Taxonomy

### Axis 1: Demand Pattern

| Class | Description | Real-World Example | Typical Model |
|-------|-------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Deterministic** | Demand is known with certainty (constant or varying deterministically) | Hospital consuming exactly 100 gloves/day | **EOQ Model**, **EPQ Model** |
| **Probabilistic (Stochastic)** | Demand follows a probability distribution (e.g., Normal, Poisson) | Retail store's daily sales of a SKU | **Newsboy Model**, **Safety Stock Models** |

### Axis 2: Lead Time Pattern

| Class | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Deterministic Lead Time** | Lead time is known and constant |
| **Probabilistic Lead Time** | Lead time varies — supplier is unreliable, transport delays occur |

### Axis 3: Review Mechanism

| Class | Description | Trigger | Common Name |
|-------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| **Continuous Review (Q-System)** | Inventory is monitored **every transaction**; reorder when level hits reorder point | Reorder Point (ROP) | **Q-Model / Reorder Point System** |
| **Periodic Review (P-System)** | Inventory is reviewed at **fixed intervals** (e.g., weekly); order up to a target level | Review Period (T) | **P-Model / Periodic Review System** |

### Axis 4: Number of Items and Echelons

- **Single-Item, Single-Echelon** → Simplest EOQ
- **Multi-Item, Single-Echelon** → Joint ordering with constraints
- **Single-Item, Multi-Echelon** → Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP)
- **Multi-Item, Multi-Echelon** → Material Requirements Planning (MRP) networks

## 2.3 Selective Inventory Control — Theoretical Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 ABC Analysis (Pareto / Always Better Control)

ABC Analysis is the **foundation** of selective inventory control. It is rooted in **Vilfredo Pareto's 80/20 principle**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):**
> Approximately **80% of the total consumption value** of inventory comes from only **20% of the items**, while the remaining **80% of items** account for only **20% of the value**.

### Procedure for ABC Classification:

1. List **all inventory items**.
2. Compute **Annual Consumption Value** for each item:

$$\text{Annual Consumption Value} = \text{Annual Usage (units)} \times \text{Unit Cost (₹)}$$

3. Rank items in **descending order** of consumption value.
4. Compute **cumulative percentage** of value and **cumulative percentage** of items.
5. Apply **classification thresholds** (typical industrial cutoffs):

| Class | % of Items | % of Value | Control Strategy |
|-------|------------|------------|------------------|
| **A** | 10 – 20% | 70 – 80% | **Tight control** — frequent review, accurate records, close follow-up, low safety stock |
| **B** | 20 – 30% | 15 – 25% | **Moderate control** — periodic review, normal record-keeping |
| **C** | 50 – 70% | 5 – 10% | **Loose control** — bulk orders, simple records, large safety stock OK |

### 2.3.2 VED Analysis (Criticality Classification)

Used mainly for **spare parts inventory** in maintenance contexts.

| Category | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **V — Vital** | Stockout causes complete production stoppage; very expensive to substitute | Aircraft engine spare, transformer core |
| **E — Essential** | Stockout severely impairs operations but workarounds exist | Pump impeller, gearbox |
| **D — Desirable** | Stockout causes minor inconvenience only | Office stationery, floor mats |

### 2.3.3 FSN Analysis (Movement Classification)

| Category | Meaning | Action |
|----------|---------|--------|
| **F — Fast Moving** | High issue rate | Tight control, continuous review |
| **S — Slow Moving** | Moderate issue rate | Periodic review |
| **N — Non-Moving** | No issues for >12 months | Dispose, write off, or liquidate |

### 2.3.4 SDE Analysis (Procurement Difficulty)

| Category | Meaning | Action |
|----------|---------|--------|
| **S — Scarce** | Long lead time, limited suppliers | High safety stock |
| **D — Difficult** | Moderate procurement complexity | Medium safety stock |
| **E — Easy** | Readily available in market | Low safety stock |

### 2.3.5 HML Analysis (Unit Price Classification)

| Category | Unit Price Range | Control Focus |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| **H — High** | ₹ > some threshold | Security, theft prevention |
| **M — Medium** | Mid-range | Standard control |
| **L — Low** | Cheap items | Bulk orders, low priority |

### 2.3.6 GOLF Analysis (Source of Supply)

| Category | Source | Strategic Implication |
|----------|--------|------------------------|
| **G — Government** | Public sector | Long lead times, bureaucratic |
| **O — Ordinary** | Local manufacturer | Readily available |
| **L — Local** | Regional sources | Quick delivery |
| **F — Foreign** | Imports | Currency risk, customs, long lead times |

### 2.3.7 SOS Analysis (Seasonality)

| Category | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| **S — Season** | Demand only in specific seasons (e.g., woollens in winter) |
| **O — Off-Season** | No current demand; held for next season |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Print This for Quick Revision**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Annual Consumption Value | $ACV = U \times C$ | $U$ = Annual usage (units); $C$ = Unit cost (₹/unit); $ACV$ in ₹ | Foundation of ABC |
| 2 | Cumulative % of Value | $CV_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} ACV_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} ACV_j} \times 100$ | $N$ = total items | Sum reaches 100% |
| 3 | Cumulative % of Items | $CI_i = \frac{i}{N} \times 100$ | — | Plot vs $CV$ for curve |
| 4 | Inventory Turnover Ratio | $ITR = \frac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Dimensionless; typical range: 4–12 | Higher = better liquidity |
| 5 | Average Inventory (Q-model) | $\bar{I} = \frac{Q}{2} + SS$ | $Q$ = order qty; $SS$ = safety stock | Steady-state average |
| 6 | Safety Stock (Std Dev method) | $SS = Z \times \sigma_d \times \sqrt{L}$ | $Z$ = service-level z-score; $\sigma_d$ = daily demand std dev; $L$ = lead time (days) | Used in probabilistic demand |
| 7 | Reorder Point (deterministic) | $ROP = d \times L$ | $d$ = avg daily demand; $L$ = lead time | Triggers new order |
| 8 | Reorder Point (probabilistic) | $ROP = \bar{d} \times \bar{L} + Z \times \sigma_{DL}$ | $\sigma_{DL}$ = std dev of demand during lead time | With safety stock built-in |
| 9 | Annual Carrying Cost | $H = I_C \times C \times \bar{I}$ | $I_C$ = carrying cost rate (%/yr); $C$ = unit cost | Annualized |
| 10 | ABC Threshold Rule | If $CV_i \leq 80\%$, classify as **A**; if $\leq 95\%$, **B**; else **C** | — | Standard industrial cutoffs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use the vertical pipe `\vert` directly inside markdown table cells — it breaks the table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** ABC + VED matrices are routinely used in **maintenance stores** to decide spares stocking.
- **Retail Chains (e.g., Lulu Mall, Big Bazaar):** ABC drives **shelf placement and replenishment frequency**.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** HML + VED combination prevents theft of high-value items.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** FSN + SOS handles expiry and seasonal demand.
- **E-Commerce Warehouses (e.g., Amazon):** Continuous use of ABC for SKU prioritization in robotic fulfillment centers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of ABC Classification Logic — A Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
> A manufacturing company uses **8 items** (P1 through P8). Annual usage and unit cost data are given. Apply ABC analysis and classify items.

### 3.1.1 Given Data Table

| Item Code | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| P1 | 4,000 | 50 |
| P2 | 3,000 | 10 |
| P3 | 2,500 | 80 |
| P4 | 1,500 | 25 |
| P5 | 1,000 | 200 |
| P6 | 800 | 60 |
| P7 | 600 | 5 |
| P8 | 400 | 150 |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV)

Using $ACV = U \times C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACV_{P1} &= 4000 \times 50 = 200{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P2} &= 3000 \times 10 = 30{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P3} &= 2500 \times 80 = 200{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P4} &= 1500 \times 25 = 37{,}500 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P5} &= 1000 \times 200 = 200{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P6} &= 800 \times 60 = 48{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P7} &= 600 \times 5 = 3{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
ACV_{P8} &= 400 \times 150 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Rank in Descending Order of ACV

Sorting from highest to lowest ACV:

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) |
|------|------|---------|
| 1 | P1 | 200,000 |
| 2 | P3 | 200,000 |
| 3 | P5 | 200,000 |
| 4 | P8 | 60,000 |
| 5 | P6 | 48,000 |
| 6 | P4 | 37,500 |
| 7 | P2 | 30,000 |
| 8 | P7 | 3,000 |

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Cumulative Values and Percentages

**Total ACV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total ACV} &= 200{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 48{,}000 + 37{,}500 + 30{,}000 + 3{,}000 \\
&= 778{,}500 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cumulative Value and Cumulative Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank 1 (P1): Cum Value} &= 200{,}000; \quad \% = \frac{200{,}000}{778{,}500} \times 100 = 25.69\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 2 (P3): Cum Value} &= 400{,}000; \quad \% = 51.38\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 3 (P5): Cum Value} &= 600{,}000; \quad \% = 77.07\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 4 (P8): Cum Value} &= 660{,}000; \quad \% = 84.78\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 5 (P6): Cum Value} &= 708{,}000; \quad \% = 90.95\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 6 (P4): Cum Value} &= 745{,}500; \quad \% = 95.76\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 7 (P2): Cum Value} &= 775{,}500; \quad \% = 99.61\% \\[4pt]
\text{Rank 8 (P7): Cum Value} &= 778{,}500; \quad \% = 100.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cumulative % of Items:**

$$
CI_i = \frac{i}{N} \times 100 = \frac{i}{8} \times 100
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank 1: } & 12.50\% \\
\text{Rank 2: } & 25.00\% \\
\text{Rank 3: } & 37.50\% \\
\text{Rank 4: } & 50.00\% \\
\text{Rank 5: } & 62.50\% \\
\text{Rank 6: } & 75.00\% \\
\text{Rank 7: } & 87.50\% \\
\text{Rank 8: } & 100.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply ABC Cutoffs

Using **standard thresholds** (A ≤ 80%, B ≤ 95%, C = remainder):

| Rank | Item | Cum % Value | Cum % Items | Class |
|------|------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | P1 | 25.69% | 12.50% | **A** |
| 2 | P3 | 51.38% | 25.00% | **A** |
| 3 | P5 | 77.07% | 37.50% | **A** |
| 4 | P8 | 84.78% | 50.00% | **B** |
| 5 | P6 | 90.95% | 62.50% | **B** |
| 6 | P4 | 95.76% | 75.00% | **B** |
| 7 | P2 | 99.61% | 87.50% | **C** |
| 8 | P7 | 100.00% | 100.00% | **C** |

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Control Policy by Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Decision Matrix:**

| Class | Items | % of Items | % of Value | Recommended Control |
|-------|-------|------------|------------|---------------------|
| **A** | P1, P3, P5 | 37.5% | 77.07% | Tight control, perpetual records, frequent review, low safety stock, accurate forecasting |
| **B** | P8, P6, P4 | 37.5% | 18.69% | Moderate control, periodic review, normal record-keeping |
| **C** | P2, P7 | 25.0% | 4.24% | Loose control, bulk orders, simple two-bin system, high safety stock OK |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Selective Control Technique

| Technique | Primary Basis | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|------------|
| **ABC** | Annual consumption value | All industries, general inventory | Ignores criticality & lead time |
| **VED** | Criticality / consequence of stockout | Spare parts in maintenance, hospital pharmacy | Subjective judgments |
| **FSN** | Usage velocity (issue rate) | Slow-moving / obsolete identification | Needs long historical data |
| **SDE** | Procurement difficulty | Items with unreliable supply | Ignores value |
| **HML** | Unit price | Theft-prone items | Ignores volume |
| **GOLF** | Source geography | Import-dependent inventory | Subjective |
| **SOS** | Seasonality | FMCG, apparel, agro-products | Useful only for seasonal items |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** In real practice, **ABC + VED** is the most commonly tested combination in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Always be ready to draw a **3×3 ABC–VED matrix** and explain each of the 9 cells' strategy.

### 3.3 ABC–VED Cross-Matrix (Analytical Strategy)

| | **V (Vital)** | **E (Essential)** | **D (Desirable)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Highest priority — strict control, low safety stock, multiple suppliers | High priority — periodic review | Moderate — bulk orders |
| **B** | High priority — strict safety stock | Moderate | Low priority |
| **C** | Moderate — keep buffer | Low — reorder when needed | Lowest — bulk order, two-bin system |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    B --> B2[Cover Seasonal Demand]
    B --> B3[Provide Service Level]

    C --> C2[Cover Long Lead Times]
    C --> C3[Enable Bulk Discounts]
    D --> D1[Decouple Production Stages]
    D --> D2[Enable Smooth Production]
    D --> D3[Reduce Setup Frequency]
