## 4.2 Inventory Problem Classification — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventory Problem]:::root --> B{Demand Pattern Known?}
    B -- Yes --> B1[Deterministic Models]
    B -- No --> B2[Probabilistic Models]
    B1 --> B1a{Lead Time Known?}
    B1a -- Yes --> B1a1[EOQ or EPQ Model]
    B1a -- No --> B1a2[EOQ with Variable Lead Time]
    B2 --> B2a{Review Mechanism?}
    B2a -- Continuous --> B2a1[Q System with Safety Stock]
    B2a -- Periodic --> B2a2[P System Order Up To Level]
    A --> C{Number of Items}
    C -- Single --> C1[Classical EOQ]
    C -- Multiple --> C2[Joint Replenishment]
    A --> D{Number of Echelons}
    D -- Single --> D1[Single Stage Model]
    D -- Multi --> D2[Distribution Network Model]
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 ABC Analysis — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: List All Inventory Items] --> S2[Step 2: Compute Annual Consumption Value]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Rank Items in Descending Order]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Cumulative Percentages]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply Threshold 80 95 100]
    S5 --> S6A[Cumulative Value less than or equal 80 percent CLASS A]
    S5 --> S6B[Cumulative Value less than or equal 95 percent CLASS B]
    S5 --> S6C[Remaining CLASS C]
    S6A --> F1[Tight Control Perpetual Records]
    S6B --> F2[Moderate Control Periodic Review]
    S6C --> F3[Loose Control Bulk Orders]
    classDef step fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef classA fill:#c6e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    classDef classB fill:#ffe699,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef classC fill:#f4b084,stroke:#823b0e,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 step
    class S6A,F1 classA
    class S6B,F2 classB
    class S6C,F3 classC
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define inventory. List any four functions of inventory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Inventory* is the stock of raw materials, components, work-in-progress, finished goods, and other supplies held by an organization to support production, satisfy customer demand, and decouple operations.
>
> **Four Functions of Inventory:**
> 1. **Meeting Demand** — Fulfilling customer orders without delay.
> 2. **Decoupling Operations** — Allowing independent operation of successive production stages.
> 3. **Buffering Against Uncertainty** — Absorbing fluctuations in demand and lead time.
> 4. **Economies of Scale** — Leveraging lower per-unit costs through bulk procurement or production.

*(Any 4 valid functions accepted; 0.5 mark for definition + 0.625 mark each for functions)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is selective inventory control? Name any two techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Selective Inventory Control* is the practice of classifying inventory items into categories based on their value, criticality, usage rate, or procurement difficulty, so that managerial control efforts are focused on the most important items.
>
> **Two Techniques:**
> 1. **ABC Analysis** (Pareto / Always Better Control) — based on Annual Consumption Value.
> 2. **VED Analysis** — Vital, Essential, Desirable — based on the criticality of a stockout.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for techniques)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE norms, each Part-B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. The two alternative questions provided below (Question A and Question B) are independent problem statements — students answer either one.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice Option 1**

#### (a) Discuss the various functions of inventory in a manufacturing organization with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Inventory serves multiple strategic, operational, and financial roles in a manufacturing setup. The **eight major functions** are:

**1. Meeting Demand (1 mark):**
Inventory of finished goods allows the firm to fulfil customer orders instantly. *Example:* A refrigerator manufacturer keeps stock ready because buyers prefer immediate delivery.

**2. Decoupling Operations (1 mark):**
WIP inventory between stages (e.g., press shop, welding, paint shop) lets each stage operate at its own optimal rate. *Example:* If the paint shop slows down, machining does not stop because the WIP buffer holds components.

**3. Buffering Uncertainty (1 mark):**
Safety stock covers random demand surges and supplier delays. *Example:* A pharma company stocks 3 weeks of paracetamol to cover monsoon-driven demand spikes.

**4. Economies of Scale (1 mark):**
Ordering raw material in bulk lowers per-unit ordering and transport cost. *Example:* Ordering 10 tonnes of steel instead of 1 tonne saves transportation cost per kg.

**5. Pipeline / Transit Stock (1 mark):**
Goods in transit (e.g., coal on a ship from Australia to India) are inventory assets.

**6. Hedging Against Price Changes (1 mark):**
Buying aluminium at ₹200/kg when prices are forecast to rise to ₹260/kg.

**7. Smoothing Production (0.5 mark):**
A ceiling fan manufacturer produces at a constant rate throughout the year, building inventory in winter and depleting it in summer.

**8. Avoiding Stockouts (0.5 mark):**
Critical spares inventory prevents machine downtime.

*(Mark split: 7 functions × 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

#### (b) Explain ABC analysis of inventory with the steps involved. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
ABC Analysis (Always Better Control) classifies inventory items into three categories — A, B, C — based on their **Annual Consumption Value** (Annual Usage × Unit Cost), following the **Pareto 80/20 principle**.

**Steps (3 marks):**
1. List all inventory items.
2. Compute $ACV = \text{Annual Usage} \times \text{Unit Cost}$ for each item.
3. Rank items in descending order of ACV.
4. Compute cumulative percentage of ACV and cumulative percentage of item count.
5. Apply thresholds: **A** items = top 10–20% of items contributing 70–80% of value; **B** items = next 20–30% contributing 15–25%; **C** items = remaining 50–70% contributing 5–10%.
6. Develop class-specific control policies.

**Classification Summary (1 mark):**

| Class | % Items | % Value | Control |
|-------|---------|---------|---------|
| A | 10–20% | 70–80% | Tight |
| B | 20–30% | 15–25% | Moderate |
| C | 50–70% | 5–10% | Loose |

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Focuses managerial attention on critical items.
- Reduces carrying costs without compromising service.
- Helps in budgeting and forecasting.
- Reduces clerical effort on low-value items.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Based on annual value only; ignores criticality (use VED for that).
- Past data may not reflect future consumption patterns.
- Subjective cutoffs (80/95/100%) may not suit every organization.
- Assumes stable consumption; sudden shifts can mislead the classification.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice Option 2**

#### (a) Classify the inventory problems based on demand pattern and lead time. Explain the relevant models briefly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Inventory problems can be classified along **two main dimensions**: demand pattern and lead time pattern.

**1. Classification by Demand Pattern (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Example | Model |
|------|-------------|---------|-------|
| **Deterministic Demand** | Demand is known and constant (or varies deterministically) | Hospital consumes 100 syringes/day | **EOQ Model** (Economic Order Quantity) |
| **Probabilistic / Stochastic Demand** | Demand is random with a known probability distribution | Retail store daily sales of soap | **Safety Stock / Newsboy / Probabilistic ROP Model** |

**Deterministic Model — EOQ (1 mark):**
$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$
where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.

**Probabilistic Model — Reorder Point with Safety Stock (1 mark):**
$$ROP = \bar{d}\bar{L} + Z\sigma_{DL}$$
where $\bar{d}\bar{L}$ = expected demand during lead time, $Z$ = service-level z-score, $\sigma_{DL}$ = standard deviation of demand during lead time.

**2. Classification by Lead Time Pattern (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Treatment |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Deterministic Lead Time** | Lead time is fixed and known | Use $ROP = d \times L$ directly |
| **Probabilistic Lead Time** | Lead time varies (supplier unreliability, transport variability) | Use $ROP = \bar{d}\bar{L} + Z\sqrt{\bar{L}\sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^2\sigma_L^2}$ |

**Combined Classification Matrix (essential for full marks):**

| | **Deterministic Lead Time** | **Probabilistic Lead Time** |
|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic Demand** | Basic EOQ / EPQ | EOQ with safety stock |
| **Probabilistic Demand** | ROP model with SS | Full stochastic model |

---

#### (b) Explain the various selective inventory control techniques used in industries. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Selective inventory control groups items so that **different control policies** are applied to different groups based on their strategic importance.

**1. ABC Analysis — Value Based (1.5 marks):**
Classifies items based on Annual Consumption Value into A (tight control), B (moderate), C (loose). Based on Pareto 80/20 principle.

**2. VED Analysis — Criticality Based (1.5 marks):**
Classifies spares into:
- **V — Vital:** Stockout stops plant completely.
- **E — Essential:** Stockout severely impairs production but workarounds exist.
- **D — Desirable:** Stockout causes only minor inconvenience.

**3. FSN Analysis — Movement Based (1 mark):**
Classifies items into **F**ast, **S**low, **N**on-moving based on issue rate. Non-moving items are disposed of to free locked capital.

**4. SDE Analysis — Procurement Difficulty (0.5 mark):**
**S**carce, **D**ifficult, **E**asy. Determines safety stock levels — scarce items need higher buffers.

**5. HML Analysis — Unit Price (0.5 mark):**
**H**igh, **M**edium, **L**ow-priced items. Controls theft-prone high-value items.

**6. GOLF Analysis — Source (0.5 mark):**
**G**overnment, **O**rdinary, **L**ocal, **F**oreign sources. Critical for import-dependent inventory and currency risk.

**7. SOS Analysis — Seasonality (0.5 mark):**
**S**eason vs **O**ff-season items. Common in FMCG, apparel, agro-products.

**Combined Use (1 mark):**
In practice, **ABC + VED**, **ABC + FSN**, and **ABC + SDE** are combined as 2D matrices to develop sophisticated multi-criteria control policies. For example, an item that is **A-V** (high value, vital) gets the strictest possible control — perpetual inventory, multiple suppliers, low safety stock; whereas **C-D** items (low value, desirable) get bulk orders and minimum control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Functions of Inventory" with "Types of Inventory."** Functions are *purposes* (e.g., meeting demand); Types are *physical categories* (raw material, WIP, finished goods). Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are mixed up.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the formula $ACV = U \times C$ in ABC.** Always write the formula explicitly before the numerical table.
>
> 3. **Not specifying the cutoffs (80/95/100).** A bare classification without thresholds is incomplete — 1 mark lost.
>
> 4. **Single-technique blind spot.** Selective inventory control is **not just ABC**. Examiners expect at least 4–5 techniques. Mentioning only ABC and missing VED/FSN costs up to 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing "Deterministic Demand" with "Constant Demand."** Deterministic means *known* (could be time-varying); constant is a special case.
>
> 6. **Skipping the ABC–VED cross-matrix.** When asked about combined techniques, drawing the 3×3 matrix is mandatory for full marks.
>
> 7. **In numerical ABC problems, not sorting in descending order.** Unsorted tables are marked incorrect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- **Inventory** is the stock of materials held to support operations; it is both an **asset** and a **cost center**.
- The **8 functions** of inventory are: meeting demand, decoupling operations, buffering uncertainty, economies of scale, pipeline stock, hedging prices, smoothing production, and avoiding stockouts.
- **Inventory problems** are classified along **4 axes**: demand pattern, lead time pattern, review mechanism (Q vs P), and number of items/echelons.
- **Deterministic demand** → EOQ / EPQ models. **Probabilistic demand** → Safety stock + ROP models.
- **Continuous review (Q-system)** triggers an order when stock falls to ROP; **Periodic review (P-system)** orders up to a target level at fixed intervals.
- **ABC Analysis** (foundation of selective control): $ACV = U \times C$; typical thresholds are **80/95/100%**.
  - **A items** (10–20% of SKUs, 70–80% of value) → **tight control**.
  - **B items** (20–30% of SKUs, 15–25% of value) → **moderate control**.
  - **C items** (50–70% of SKUs, 5–10% of value) → **loose control**.
- **VED** classifies by criticality (Vital/Essential/Desirable) — common in spares maintenance.
- **FSN** classifies by usage velocity (Fast/Slow/Non-moving).
- **SDE** classifies by procurement difficulty (Scarce/Difficult/Easy).
- **HML** classifies by unit price (High/Medium/Low) — useful for theft prevention.
- **GOLF** classifies by source (Government/Ordinary/Local/Foreign).
- **SOS** classifies by seasonality (Season/Off-season).
- In practice, **ABC + VED** cross-matrices drive real-world inventory control decisions.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** is the philosophical foundation of selective inventory control.
- Always show **cumulative percentages** in numerical ABC problems — partial credit is denied otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Review and Periodic Review Inventory Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Continuous Review and Periodic Review Inventory Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Inventory Control Systems** are the structured policies and procedures used by organizations to determine when to place replenishment orders and how much to order, so that the costs of holding, ordering, and stockouts are optimally balanced against the cost of meeting customer demand.

Within this broad domain, the KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly focuses on two dominant replenishment philosophies:

1. **Continuous Review System (CRS)** — also called the **Q-System** or **Reorder Point (ROP) System**. The inventory level of the item is monitored **continuously** (after every transaction, in real time), and a fixed quantity **Q** is ordered whenever the on-hand stock falls to a pre-computed level called the **Reorder Point (R)**.
2. **Periodic Review System (PRS)** — also called the **P-System** or **Fixed Order Interval System**. The inventory is reviewed only at **discrete, fixed time intervals** of period **T** (e.g., once a week, once a month), and the quantity ordered varies so as to raise the stock up to a pre-determined **Order-Up-To Level (S)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – High-Yield Concept:** The fundamental difference is **WHEN** the system reacts (continuously vs. periodically) and **HOW MUCH** is ordered (fixed Q vs. variable to top up to S).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy 1 — The Refrigerator (Continuous Review):**
Imagine a refrigerator in your home. Every time you open the door, you glance at the milk level. The *moment* the milk drops below a fixed line (say, 1/4 of the carton), you immediately place an order with the local dairy for **exactly 2 litres**, because that is your standard family pack. You are monitoring the level **continuously**, and the **order size is fixed**. This is a Continuous Review / Q-System.

**Analogy 2 — The Office Pantry (Periodic Review):**
Now imagine the office pantry is checked **only on Mondays**. The pantry in-charge opens the storeroom on every Monday morning, looks at the remaining tea-bags, sugar, and biscuits, and orders a quantity large enough to bring the stock up to a target ceiling (say, 100 tea-bags). The **order timing is fixed (weekly)** but the **order quantity varies** based on consumption. This is a Periodic Review / P-System.

**Analogy 3 — Blood Pressure Monitor vs. Annual Health Checkup:**
A BP monitor that takes readings every second and alerts you the moment pressure crosses a threshold is a **Continuous Review** device. An annual master health checkup that you undergo every 12 months regardless of how you feel is a **Periodic Review** process. One reacts to *instantaneous* signals; the other reacts to a *scheduled snapshot*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU exams:** Most numerical problems in Module 3 differ only in the safety-stock formula and the average-inventory formula between the two systems. Confusing the two is the single largest source of mark loss reported by KTU 2024 Scheme paper evaluators.

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standard Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | Annual demand | units/year |
| **d** | Daily (average) demand | units/day |
| **Q** | Order quantity (fixed in Q-System) | units/order |
| **T** | Review period (fixed in P-System) | days/weeks |
| **L** | Lead time | days |
| **R** | Reorder Point | units |
| **S** | Order-Up-To Level | units |
| **SS** | Safety Stock | units |
| **H** | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹/unit-year |
| **S<sub>o</sub>** | Ordering cost per order | ₹/order |
| **C** | Unit purchase cost | ₹/unit |
| **z** | Standard Normal service-factor (Z-score) | dimensionless |
| **σ** | Standard deviation of demand | units |
| **α** | Stockout probability (1 − service level) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always use consistent units. If demand is given as monthly, convert to annual **before** applying EOQ, and convert lead time to the same time base as the demand standard deviation. A common error is mixing daily demand with monthly lead time.

### 1.4 Where Each System is Used in Real Engineering Industries

| Industry Context | Preferred System | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Fast-moving FMCG SKUs in supermarkets | **Q-System (CRS)** | Tight control, low stockout tolerance, real-time POS data available. |
| Perishable grocery items (bread, milk) | **P-System (PRS)** | Items delivered daily by supplier on a fixed route, so review is naturally periodic. |
| Spare parts in a heavy-engineering plant | **Q-System (CRS)** | High criticality, low volume, high unit cost → continuous monitoring pays off. |
| Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) chains | **P-System (PRS)** | Supplier visits customer on a fixed schedule (milk-run). |
| Automobile sub-assemblies on JIT line | **Q-System (CRS)** with Kanban | Reorder triggered by visual signal (= reaching ROP). |
| Pharmacy / hospital bulk-drug stock | **P-System (PRS)** | Daily or weekly physical counting is feasible; continuous electronic tracking may be unavailable. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Continuous Review System (Q-System) — Operational Logic

The Q-System operates on a simple loop. The inventory position is updated **after every demand event**, and the rule is:

> *"Whenever Inventory Position ≤ R, place an order of size Q. Otherwise, do nothing."*

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Set the order quantity Q** to the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) that minimises the sum of ordering and holding cost:
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S_{o}}{H}}$$

2. **Compute the Reorder Point R** so that the order arrives **just in time** to cover the demand that will occur during the lead time L, plus a buffer for variability:
$$R = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$$

3. **Compute the Safety Stock SS** as the buffer that protects against two independent sources of variability: fluctuating demand during lead time, and fluctuating lead time for constant demand.

   - *Case 1 — Variable demand, constant lead time:*
$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$$
   - *Case 2 — Constant demand, variable lead time:*
$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{L} \cdot \bar{d}$$
   - *Case 3 — Variable demand AND variable lead time (general case):*
$$SS = z \cdot \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

4. **Track the inventory position** continuously:
$$\text{Inventory Position} = \text{On-Hand} + \text{On-Order} - \text{Backorders}$$

5. **Trigger rule:** if Inventory Position ≤ R, place an order of size Q; this order will arrive after L time-units.

### 2.2 Periodic Review System (P-System) — Operational Logic

The P-System operates on a different loop. The inventory is reviewed only at fixed intervals T, and the order placed is whatever quantity is needed to refill the stock to a ceiling S.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Choose the review period T** (often equal to the time it takes to consume one EOQ, so that the average order size equals Q*):
$$T = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \sqrt{\frac{2 S_{o}}{D H}}$$

2. **Compute the Order-Up-To Level S** (also called the "target level" or "base stock") so that the stock after ordering covers the demand during one full review period **plus** the subsequent lead time, plus a safety buffer:
$$S = \bar{d} \cdot (T + L) + SS_{P}$$

3. **Compute the Safety Stock for the P-System SS<sub>P</sub>** — the buffer now protects against variability over a longer horizon, **(T + L)**, instead of just L:
$$SS_{P} = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{T + L}$$
$$SS_{P} = z \cdot \sqrt{(T + L) \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

4. **At each review instant** (every T time units), count the current inventory I<sub>t</sub> and place a variable order:
$$Q_{\text{order}}(t) = S - I_{t}$$

5. **Trigger rule:** the order arrives after L time-units, and the next review is exactly T time-units later.

### 2.3 The Crucial Safety-Stock Insight (Why P-System Needs More Safety Stock)

In the Q-System, safety stock covers demand variability over a window of length **L** (lead time).
In the P-System, a stockout can occur *anywhere* within the next **T + L** window — either immediately after the review (if demand is high and lead time is long) or near the next review (if demand is high and lead time is short).

Because the protected horizon is **longer** in the P-System, the safety stock is **larger** for an equivalent service level:

$$\frac{SS_{P}}{SS_{Q}} = \sqrt{\frac{T + L}{L}} \;\geq\; 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – High-Yield Result:** If both systems use the same service level (same z), the Periodic Review system always requires **more safety stock** than the Continuous Review system. This is the most-tested comparative fact in KTU Module-3 problems.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Continuous Review (Q-System) | Periodic Review (P-System) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Order Quantity | $Q$ (fixed) = $\sqrt{2 D S_{o} / H}$ | $Q_{t} = S - I_{t}$ (variable) |
| 2 | Re-Order Trigger | When On-Hand $\leq R$ | Every $T$ time units |
| 3 | Reorder Point / Target Level | $R = \bar{d} L + SS_{Q}$ | $S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P}$ |
| 4 | Safety Stock (variable demand, constant L) | $SS_{Q} = z \cdot \sigma_{d}\sqrt{L}$ | $SS_{P} = z \cdot \sigma_{d}\sqrt{T + L}$ |
| 5 | Safety Stock (general case) | $SS_{Q} = z \cdot \sqrt{L \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ | $SS_{P} = z \cdot \sqrt{(T + L) \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ |
| 6 | Average Inventory | $\bar{I}_{Q} = \dfrac{Q}{2} + SS_{Q}$ | $\bar{I}_{P} = \dfrac{\bar{d} T}{2} + SS_{P}$ |
| 7 | Maximum Inventory | $Q + SS_{Q}$ | $S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P}$ |
| 8 | Number of Orders / Year | $N = D / Q$ | $N = 1 / T$ |
| 9 | Time Between Orders | $Q / D$ | $T$ (fixed) |
| 10 | Annual Holding Cost | $H \cdot \bar{I}_{Q}$ | $H \cdot \bar{I}_{P}$ |
| 11 | Annual Ordering Cost | $(D / Q) \cdot S_{o}$ | $(1 / T) \cdot S_{o}$ |
| 12 | Annual Purchase Cost | $C \cdot D$ | $C \cdot D$ |
| 13 | Total Relevant Cost (TRC) | $\dfrac{D}{Q} S_{o} + H \cdot \bar{I}_{Q}$ | $\dfrac{1}{T} S_{o} + H \cdot \bar{I}_{P}$ |
| 14 | Service Level Relationship | Type 1 = $P(\text{stockout})$; Type 2 = $P(\text{demand met from stock})$ | Same definitions; same $z$-table usage |

### 2.5 Service Level — Two Industry Definitions

- **Type-1 Service Level (Cycle-Service level, CSL):** Probability of **not** stocking out during a replenishment cycle.
$$CSL = P(\text{Lead-Time Demand} \leq R) = 1 - \alpha$$
The z-value is read from standard normal table: $z = Z_{1 - \alpha}$.

- **Type-2 Service Level (Fill Rate, β):** Fraction of total demand that is **satisfied immediately from stock** (not backordered).
$$\beta = 1 - \frac{E[\text{shortage per cycle}]}{Q}$$
Solving for SS in Type-2 requires the standard normal **loss function** $L(z)$:
$$SS = \sigma_{LT} \cdot (k - \frac{Q}{Q + \sigma_{LT}} \cdot L(z) \cdot \text{...})$$
   (Full loss-function derivations are beyond Module 3; KTU Module 3 mostly tests Type-1 service level.)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 default assumption:** Unless the problem explicitly says "fill rate" or "Type-2", the z-value in Module 3 problems corresponds to **Type-1 (cycle) service level** read directly from the standard normal CDF.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Q-System with ROP** is the workhorse of **MRP-II / ERP systems** in discrete manufacturing, where each issue/Receipt transaction is posted to the inventory file in real time.
- **P-System** is the foundation of **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** and **Joint Replenishment** in retail and pharma supply chains.
- Both systems converge to **identical average cost** when $T = Q/D$ and the safety stock is computed at the same service level — this is the basis of the "Hybrid System" or "Periodic-Review with EOQ" model taught in advanced modules.
- The Q-vs-P choice directly drives the **warehouse picking policy**: Q-System → bin-level picking; P-System → full-aisle periodic count.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of EOQ (used inside the Q-System)

The relevant annual cost (ignoring the constant purchase cost $C \cdot D$) is:
$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} S_{o}}_{\text{ordering cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} H}_{\text{holding cost (cycle stock)}}$$

**Step 1.** Differentiate TC with respect to Q and set to zero:
$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D S_{o}}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Solve for Q:
$$\frac{D S_{o}}{Q^{2}} = \frac{H}{2}$$
$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 D S_{o}}{H}$$
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S_{o}}{H}}$$

**Step 3.** Verify second-order condition:
$$\frac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 D S_{o}}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad (\text{minimum, since } D, S_{o}, Q > 0)$$

**Step 4.** Substituting back gives minimum total relevant cost:
$$TC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D S_{o} H}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Safety Stock for Q-System (General Case)

Let $X$ = demand during lead time, with $X \sim N(\mu_{LT}, \sigma_{LT}^{2})$.
$$\mu_{LT} = \bar{d} \cdot L \qquad (\text{or } L \cdot \bar{d} \text{ if } L \text{ is in same units as } d)$$

The general formula for the lead-time standard deviation, when demand during individual days is independent with variance $\sigma_{d}^{2}$ and lead time has variance $\sigma_{L}^{2}$:
$$\sigma_{LT}^{2} = L \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}$$
$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

The safety stock for a Type-1 service level $1 - \alpha$:
$$SS = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LT} = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sqrt{L \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the P-System Order-Up-To Level

At the moment of review at time $t$, the available stock is $I_{t}$. An order of size $(S - I_{t})$ is placed and arrives after $L$ time units. The next review is $T$ later. The stock at the next review will be:
$$I_{t+T} = I_{t} + (S - I_{t}) - \text{Demand over } (T + L) = S - D_{T+L}$$

For zero stockout (with safety stock $SS_{P}$):
$$S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P}$$

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Step-by-Step — Q-System)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 2024 module question):**
A component used in a refrigerator assembly line has annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S_{o} = ₹ 250$ per order. Holding cost $H = ₹ 5$ per unit per year. Lead time $L = 6$ days. Daily demand is normally distributed with $\bar{d} = 40$ units/day and $\sigma_{d} = 8$ units/day. The management wants a 95 % Type-1 service level. Find $Q^{*}$, $R$, $SS_{Q}$, average inventory, and annual total relevant cost.

**Step 1 — Convert annual demand to daily:**
Already given: $\bar{d} = D / 300 = 12{,}000/300 = 40$ units/day (assuming 300 working days/year). ✓

**Step 2 — Compute EOQ:**
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S_{o}}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 250}{5}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \approx 1095.4$$
$$Q^{*} \approx 1096 \text{ units per order}$$

**Step 3 — Find z for 95 % service level:**
From standard normal table, $P(Z \leq 1.645) = 0.95$, so $z = 1.645$.

**Step 4 — Compute lead-time standard deviation** (constant L, variable demand):
$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{d}\sqrt{L} = 8 \times \sqrt{6} = 8 \times 2.4495 = 19.596$$

**Step 5 — Safety Stock:**
$$SS_{Q} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.645 \times 19.596 = 32.24 \approx 33 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Reorder Point:**
$$R = \bar{d} L + SS_{Q} = 40 \times 6 + 33 = 240 + 33 = 273 \text{ units}$$

**Step 7 — Average Inventory:**
$$\bar{I}_{Q} = \frac{Q^{*}}{2} + SS_{Q} = \frac{1096}{2} + 33 = 548 + 33 = 581 \text{ units}$$

**Step 8 — Annual Total Relevant Cost:**
$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} S_{o} = \frac{12{,}000}{1096} \times 250 = 10.949 \times 250 = ₹ 2{,}737.2$$
$$\text{Holding Cost} = H \cdot \bar{I}_{Q} = 5 \times 581 = ₹ 2{,}905$$
$$TC_{\min} = 2{,}737.2 + 2{,}905 = ₹ 5{,}642.2 \text{ per year}$$

### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Step-by-Step — P-System)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 2024 module question — same data, switched to P-System):**
A pharmaceutical company stocks a drug. Annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units, $S_{o} = ₹ 250$, $H = ₹ 5$, $L = 6$ days, $\bar{d} = 40$, $\sigma_{d} = 8$. Review period $T = 1$ month = 25 working days. Service level = 95 %. Find $S$, $SS_{P}$, and average inventory.

**Step 1 — Review period in working days:**
$T = 25$ days.

**Step 2 — Compute z for 95 %:**
$z = 1.645$ (same as before).

**Step 3 — Standard deviation over (T + L) window:**
$$\sigma_{T+L} = \sigma_{d}\sqrt{T + L} = 8 \times \sqrt{25 + 6} = 8 \times \sqrt{31} = 8 \times 5.5678 = 44.542$$

**Step 4 — Safety Stock:**
$$SS_{P} = z \cdot \sigma_{T+L} = 1.645 \times 44.542 = 73.27 \approx 74 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Order-Up-To Level S:**
$$S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P} = 40 \times 31 + 74 = 1{,}240 + 74 = 1{,}314 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Average Inventory:**
$$\bar{I}_{P} = \frac{\bar{d} T}{2} + SS_{P} = \frac{40 \times 25}{2} + 74 = 500 + 74 = 574 \text{ units}$$

**Step 7 — Comparative Insight:**
For the same service level, $SS_{P} = 74 > SS_{Q} = 33$. The P-System carries more safety stock (≈ 2.24 × the Q-System) but slightly **less cycle stock** (500 vs 548) because the review interval $T$ is shorter than $Q^{*}/D = 1096/12000 \times 300 = 27.4$ days in this case. Net average inventory is comparable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion sanity check:** $T = 25$ days in the P-system is **not** the same as $Q^{*}/D = 27.4$ days. The textbook equivalency $T = Q/D$ only holds at the EOQ optimum; here $T$ was imposed externally, so the average cycle stock is $\bar{d}T/2 = 500$, not $Q/2 = 548$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Comparison Axis | Continuous Review (Q-System) | Periodic Review (P-System) |
|---|---|---|
| **Time of review** | Continuous (after every transaction) | Periodic (every T) |
| **Order quantity** | Fixed (Q) | Variable ($S - I_{t}$) |
| **Trigger variable** | Inventory level (R) | Calendar clock (T) |
| **Safety-stock horizon** | Lead time L | Review period + Lead time = T + L |
| **Safety stock at same SL** | Lower (Q-system advantage) | Higher (≈ $\sqrt{(T+L)/L}$ × Q-system SS) |
| **Average inventory** | $Q/2 + SS$ | $\bar{d} T / 2 + SS$ |
| **Information system requirement** | Real-time, transaction-by-transaction | Periodic snapshot (less data-intensive) |
| **Administrative convenience** | Higher (continuous tracking) | Lower (periodic counting) |
| **Stockout vulnerability window** | Narrow (between R and arrival) | Wide (entire T + L window) |
| **Typical use** | High-value, critical, A-class items | Grouped review, vendor delivery routes, C-class items |
| **Cost components** | $DC + (D/Q)S_{o} + H(Q/2 + SS)$ | $DC + (1/T)S_{o} + H(\bar{d} T/2 + SS)$ |
| **Supplier interaction** | Random, may cluster | Synchronised, route-friendly |
| **Effect of stockout cost** | Sensitive — small SS increases | Less sensitive (larger SS absorbs) |
| **Number of SKUs that can be reviewed** | One trigger per SKU, separate | Can review many SKUs together (joint replenishment) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time — Q-System (Saw-Tooth with Reorder Point)

The classic **saw-tooth** inventory profile, where each cycle starts at $Q + SS$ and depletes at rate $\bar{d}$ until it hits R, at which point an order of Q units is released and arrives L time-units later (just in time).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start of Cycle: Stock = Q + SS] -->|Demand depletes at rate d-bar| B[Stock falls linearly]
    B -->|Reaches Reorder Point R| C[Trigger: Place Order of Q units]
    C -->|After Lead Time L| D[Order Arrives: Stock jumps to Q + SS]
    D -->|Next cycle begins| A
```

**Key visual markers on the chart:**

- **Vertical jump at order arrival** = magnitude **Q**
- **Slope of the descending line** = $-\bar{d}$
- **Time between jumps** = $Q / D$ (cycle time)
- **Buffer above R during L** = safety stock $SS_{Q}$
- **Buffer between R and 0** = expected demand during L = $\bar{d}L$

### 4.2 Inventory Level vs. Time — P-System (Variable Order Sizes, Fixed T)

In the P-System, the order size differs from cycle to cycle (because consumption between reviews varies), but the **review instants** are equally spaced at intervals T.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Review at t=0: Stock = I₀, Order = S − I₀] -->|Demand over L| B[Order arrives: Stock = I₀ + S − I₀ − dL = S − dL]
    B -->|Demand over remaining T| C[Next Review at t=T: Stock = I_T, Order = S − I_T]
    C -->|Demand over L| D[Order arrives: Stock = S − dL]
    D -->|Demand over remaining T| E[Review at t=2T: Stock = I_{2T}, Order = S − I_{2T}]
    E -->|Cycle continues| C
```

**Key visual markers on the chart:**

- **Vertical jumps at order arrival** = different magnitudes every cycle (variable Q)
- **Review instants at t = 0, T, 2T, 3T, ...** = regular, equally spaced
- **Slope of descending lines** = $-\bar{d}$ (deterministic portion)
- **Buffer above zero** = safety stock $SS_{P}$ (now protects T + L window)

### 4.3 Decision-Flow Block Diagram — Choosing and Operating a Q-System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Collect annual demand D, ordering cost S₀, holding cost H] --> B[Compute Q* = sqrt of 2DS over H]
    B --> C[Estimate lead time L and demand std dev sigma-d]
    C --> D[Choose service level 1 minus alpha → read z]
    D --> E[Compute sigma-LT = sigma-d × sqrt of L]
    E --> F[SS-Q = z × sigma-LT]
    F --> G[R = d-bar × L + SS-Q]
    G --> H[Real-time: Track Inventory Position IP]
    H --> I{IP ≤ R?}
    I -->|No| H
    I -->|Yes| J[Release Order of Q units]
    J --> K[Order arrives after L time units]
    K --> L[Update IP = IP + Q]
    L --> H
```

### 4.4 Decision-Flow Block Diagram — Choosing and Operating a P-System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Collect annual demand D, ordering cost S₀, holding cost H] --> B[Choose review period T external constraint or T = Q over D]
    B --> C[Estimate lead time L and demand std dev sigma-d]
    C --> D[Choose service level 1 minus alpha → read z]
    D --> E[Compute sigma-TplusL = sigma-d × sqrt of T + L]
    E --> F[SS-P = z × sigma-TplusL]
    F --> G[S = d-bar × T + L + SS-P]
    G --> H[Clock triggers review every T time units]
    H --> I[Count current on-hand inventory I-t]
    I --> J[Compute Q-order = S minus I-t]
    J --> K[Release order of Q-order units]
    K --> L[Order arrives after L time units]
    L --> H
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture — Information Flow Inside an ERP

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SENSING
        POS[Point of Sale Scanner] --> INVDB[(Inventory Database)]
        WH[Warehouse Issue Slip] --> INVDB
        RCPT[Goods Receipt Note] --> INVDB
    end
    INVDB --> CRSCORE[Q-System Reorder Point Engine]
    INVDB --> PRSCORE[P-System Order-Up-To Engine]
    CRSCORE -->|Trigger when IP ≤ R| PO1[Purchase Order Q units]
    PRSCORE -->|Trigger every T| PO2[Purchase Order S minus I-t]
    PO1 --> SUPPLIER[Supplier]
    PO2 --> SUPPLIER
    SUPPLIER --> RCPT
```

### 4.6 Joint-Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inventory Item with Independent Demand] --> B{Decide Policy}
    B -->|Continuous Monitoring Possible, High Unit Cost| C[Q-System]
    B -->|Grouped Replenishment Feasible, Periodic Count| D[P-System]
    C --> E[Q fixed, R fixed, SS = z × sigma-d × sqrt L]
    D --> F[T fixed, S fixed, SS = z × sigma-d × sqrt T plus L]
    E --> G[Lower SS, Higher Info Cost]
    F --> H[Higher SS, Lower Info Cost]
    G --> I[Same Service Level]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short-Answer / Definition)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between a Continuous Review System and a Periodic Review System in inventory management.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Continuous Review System (Q-system)** monitors the inventory level after every transaction and places a **fixed order quantity Q** whenever the inventory position falls to or below a pre-determined **Reorder Point R**. A **Periodic Review System (P-system)** reviews the inventory only at **fixed intervals T** and places a **variable order quantity** to raise the stock up to a target level **S**. The Q-system protects against demand variability over the lead time L, while the P-system protects over the longer window (T + L), and therefore requires more safety stock for the same service level. *(1 mark for definition of Q, 1 mark for definition of P, 1 mark for the safety-stock distinction.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is safety stock? Why does a Periodic Review System require a higher safety stock than a Continuous Review System for the same service level?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Safety stock (SS)** is the extra inventory held as a buffer against fluctuations in demand and/or lead time, to achieve a target customer-service level. In a Continuous Review system, an order is triggered the moment the stock falls to R, so SS must only cover demand variability over the **lead time L**. In a Periodic Review system, the next replenishment could be as far away as **T + L** time units into the future, so SS must cover demand variability over the **longer window (T + L)**. Since variability (standard deviation) scales with the square root of the horizon, $SS_{P}/SS_{Q} = \sqrt{(T+L)/L} \geq 1$, so the P-system always requires more safety stock for the same service level. *(1 mark for SS definition, 1 mark for L vs T+L horizon, 1 mark for sqrt ratio justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between two full questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Continuous Review Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

A manufacturing firm uses 18,000 units/year of a raw material. Ordering cost is ₹ 200 per order. Holding cost is ₹ 4 per unit per year. Lead time is constant at 8 days. Daily demand is normally distributed with mean 60 units and standard deviation 10 units. The firm wants a 97.5 % Type-1 service level.

**(a) [7 marks]** Compute the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), the Reorder Point (R), the safety stock (SS), and the average inventory for a Continuous Review System.

**(b) [7 marks]** If management insists on switching to a Periodic Review System with review period T = 1 month (≈ 25 working days), recompute the safety stock and the order-up-to level S. Comment on the change in safety stock and inventory.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Continuous Review (Q-System):**

*Step 1 — Verify daily demand matches annual demand:*
$\bar{d} = 60$ units/day, working days/year = $18{,}000 / 60 = 300$ days ✓

*Step 2 — EOQ:*
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}800{,}000} \approx 1341.64$$
$$Q^{*} \approx 1342 \text{ units}$$

*Step 3 — z for 97.5 % service level:*
From standard normal table, $P(Z \leq 1.96) = 0.975$, so $z = 1.96$.

*Step 4 — Lead-time standard deviation (constant L, variable demand):*
$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{d} \sqrt{L} = 10 \times \sqrt{8} = 10 \times 2.8284 = 28.284$$

*Step 5 — Safety Stock:*
$$SS_{Q} = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.96 \times 28.284 = 55.44 \approx 56 \text{ units}$$

*Step 6 — Reorder Point:*
$$R = \bar{d} L + SS_{Q} = 60 \times 8 + 56 = 480 + 56 = 536 \text{ units}$$

*Step 7 — Average Inventory:*
$$\bar{I}_{Q} = \frac{Q^{*}}{2} + SS_{Q} = \frac{1342}{2} + 56 = 671 + 56 = 727 \text{ units}$$

**(b) Periodic Review (P-System):**

*Step 1 — z remains 1.96 (same service level).*

*Step 2 — Standard deviation over T + L window:*
$$\sigma_{T+L} = \sigma_{d} \sqrt{T + L} = 10 \times \sqrt{25 + 8} = 10 \times \sqrt{33} = 10 \times 5.7446 = 57.446$$

*Step 3 — Safety Stock:*
$$SS_{P} = z \cdot \sigma_{T+L} = 1.96 \times 57.446 = 112.59 \approx 113 \text{ units}$$

*Step 4 — Order-Up-To Level:*
$$S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P} = 60 \times 33 + 113 = 1980 + 113 = 2093 \text{ units}$$

*Step 5 — Average Inventory under P-system:*
$$\bar{I}_{P} = \frac{\bar{d} T}{2} + SS_{P} = \frac{60 \times 25}{2} + 113 = 750 + 113 = 863 \text{ units}$$

*Step 6 — Comparison comment:*
$SS_{P}/SS_{Q} = 113/56 = 2.02$, which closely matches the theoretical $\sqrt{(T+L)/L} = \sqrt{33/8} = \sqrt{4.125} = 2.031$. The P-system carries about 136 extra units in safety stock and 23 extra units in cycle stock, increasing average inventory from 727 to 863 units. The trade-off is administrative simplicity — only one review per month vs. continuous monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sub-part (a):** Most students lose 2 marks here by computing EOQ correctly but **forgetting the safety-stock component** in the Reorder Point, writing $R = 480$ instead of $R = 536$. Always re-check: $R$ = mean lead-time demand + safety stock. A second common error is using $D$ in place of $\bar{d}$ while keeping $L$ in days, mixing annual and daily units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sub-part (b):** Students often write $SS_{P} = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ (copying the Q-system formula). That is **wrong** — under P-system the protection window is **T + L**, not L. Marks are deducted if this distinction is missed. Also, the *Order-Up-To Level* is $S$, not the order quantity — the order quantity at any review is $S - I_{t}$ and varies cycle-to-cycle.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Periodic Review Focus + Hybrid Comparison**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

A hospital pharmacy stocks a critical drug. The drug has the following characteristics:

- Annual demand $D = 24{,}000$ vials
- Ordering cost $S_{o} = ₹ 300$ per order
- Holding cost $H = ₹ 6$ per vial per year
- Lead time $L = 10$ days (assume 300 working days/year)
- Daily demand $\bar{d} = 80$ vials, $\sigma_{d} = 12$ vials (normally distributed)
- Type-1 service level required: 98 % → $z = 2.05$

**(a) [7 marks]** Design a Periodic Review System with review period $T = 20$ days. Find the Order-Up-To Level $S$, the safety stock, and the average inventory. What is the average order quantity?

**(b) [7 marks]** Now design an equivalent Continuous Review System. Compute its EOQ, Reorder Point, safety stock, and compare the average inventory between the two systems. Discuss which system is preferable if (i) the pharmacy has barcode-based real-time IT infrastructure, and (ii) the pharmacy does not have such IT and physically counts stock only on the review day.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Periodic Review (P-System) with T = 20 days:**

*Step 1 — Service-level z:* Given $z = 2.05$.

*Step 2 — Standard deviation over (T + L) = 30 days:*
$$\sigma_{T+L} = 12 \times \sqrt{30} = 12 \times 5.4772 = 65.73$$

*Step 3 — Safety Stock:*
$$SS_{P} = 2.05 \times 65.73 = 134.74 \approx 135 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 4 — Order-Up-To Level:*
$$S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P} = 80 \times 30 + 135 = 2400 + 135 = 2535 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 5 — Average Inventory:*
$$\bar{I}_{P} = \frac{\bar{d} T}{2} + SS_{P} = \frac{80 \times 20}{2} + 135 = 800 + 135 = 935 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 6 — Average Order Quantity:*
Expected demand between two reviews = $\bar{d} \times T = 80 \times 20 = 1600$ vials.
So $\bar{Q}_{P} = 1600$ vials per order.

**(b) Continuous Review (Q-System) equivalent:**

*Step 1 — EOQ:*
$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 300}{6}} = \sqrt{2{,}400{,}000} \approx 1549.19$$
$$Q^{*} \approx 1550 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 2 — z remains 2.05.*

*Step 3 — Lead-time standard deviation:*
$$\sigma_{LT} = 12 \times \sqrt{10} = 12 \times 3.1623 = 37.95$$

*Step 4 — Safety Stock:*
$$SS_{Q} = 2.05 \times 37.95 = 77.79 \approx 78 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 5 — Reorder Point:*
$$R = 80 \times 10 + 78 = 800 + 78 = 878 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 6 — Average Inventory:*
$$\bar{I}_{Q} = \frac{1550}{2} + 78 = 775 + 78 = 853 \text{ vials}$$

*Step 7 — Comparison summary:*

| Metric | Q-System | P-System |
|---|---|---|
| Safety Stock | 78 | 135 |
| Cycle Stock | 775 | 800 |
| Average Inventory | 853 | 935 |
| Order Quantity | 1550 (fixed) | 1600 average (variable) |
| Review Frequency | Continuous | Every 20 days |

*Step 8 — Qualitative recommendation:*
- **(i) With barcode / real-time IT infrastructure:** The Continuous Review system is preferable. It carries **82 fewer vials in average inventory** (935 − 853 = 82), which at $H = ₹ 6$/vial/year saves ₹ 492/year in holding cost. The real-time data makes continuous monitoring feasible.
- **(ii) Without IT, with only physical counting on the review day:** The Periodic Review system is preferable. Although it carries 82 extra vials (≈ ₹ 492/year extra holding), it eliminates the need for real-time tracking infrastructure and reduces administrative labour. The saving in administrative/IT cost typically outweighs the extra holding cost for moderate-volume SKUs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sub-part (b):** The most common error is writing the Reorder Point as $R = \bar{d} L$ alone (forgetting safety stock) and then comparing it with the Order-Up-To Level $S = \bar{d}(T+L) + SS$. These two quantities serve *different* purposes — $R$ is the **trigger level**, while $S$ is the **target refill level**. They are not directly comparable. Always compute and compare **average inventory** or **annual total cost**, not the trigger and target levels.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Continuous vs Periodic Review**

- **Two systems:** Q-System (continuous review, fixed Q) and P-System (periodic review, fixed T, variable order).
- **Trigger:** Q-system uses inventory-level threshold R; P-system uses clock-based interval T.
- **Refill target:** Q-system uses fixed Q; P-system uses fixed S (Order-Up-To Level).
- **Safety stock horizon:** Q-system protects over **L** time units; P-system protects over **T + L** time units.
- **Safety-stock formula:**
  - Q-system: $SS_{Q} = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \sqrt{L}$ (constant L) or $z \sqrt{L \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ (general).
  - P-system: $SS_{P} = z \cdot \sigma_{d} \sqrt{T + L}$ (constant L) or $z \sqrt{(T+L) \sigma_{d}^{2} + \bar{d}^{2} \sigma_{L}^{2}}$ (general).
- **Key ratio:** $SS_{P} / SS_{Q} = \sqrt{(T+L)/L} \geq 1$ → **P-system always needs more safety stock** at the same service level.
- **EOQ (used in Q-system and as benchmark for T in P-system):** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2 D S_{o} / H}$.
- **Equivalent review period:** $T = Q^{*} / D = \sqrt{2 S_{o} / (D H)}$ (when P-system is to mimic Q-system at optimum).
- **Reorder Point:** $R = \bar{d} L + SS_{Q}$.
- **Order-Up-To Level:** $S = \bar{d}(T + L) + SS_{P}$.
- **Average Inventory:**
  - Q-system: $\bar{I}_{Q} = Q/2 + SS_{Q}$.
  - P-system: $\bar{I}_{P} = \bar{d} T / 2 + SS_{P}$.
- **Annual Total Relevant Cost:**
  - Q-system: $TC_{Q} = (D/Q) S_{o} + H \bar{I}_{Q}$.
  - P-system: $TC_{P} = (1/T) S_{o} + H \bar{I}_{P}$.
- **Service level defaults:** KTU Module 3 almost always uses **Type-1 (cycle) service level** → read $z$ directly from standard normal table.
- **Common z-values to memorise:** 90 % → 1.28; 95 % → 1.645; 97.5 % → 1.96; 98 % → 2.05; 99 % → 2.33; 99.9 % → 3.09.
- **Unit consistency:** Always keep $\bar{d}$ and $L$ in the same time base. Convert annual demand to daily before computing $R$ or $S$.
- **Variability source:** Demand variability ($\sigma_{d}$), lead-time variability ($\sigma_{L}$), or both. Choose the correct formula variant.
- **Joint replenishment:** P-system naturally groups multiple SKUs at one review instant → reduces combined ordering cost.
- **Hybrid heuristic:** When $T = Q^{*}/D$ and same service level, average inventory under both systems is approximately equal — a frequently-tested equivalence.
- **Application heuristic:**
  - A-class high-value items → Q-system.
  - C-class low-value, group-buy items → P-system.
  - VMI / vendor route deliveries → P-system.
  - JIT / Kanban / real-time data → Q-system.
- **KTU-typical computation flow:** (1) Verify unit base, (2) Compute EOQ and T-equivalent, (3) Read z from service level, (4) Compute $\sigma_{LT}$ or $\sigma_{T+L}$, (5) Compute SS, (6) Compute R or S, (7) Compute average inventory and total cost.
- **Most-mark-losing errors:** (a) Mixing annual and daily units, (b) using L instead of T + L under P-system, (c) writing R = $\bar{d}L$ without SS, (d) confusing R with S as if they were comparable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Models, Quantity Discount, Economic Order Quantity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Models, Quantity Discount, Economic Order Quantity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deterministic Inventory Models — Formal Definition

A **Deterministic Inventory Model** is a class of inventory control models in which the demand rate, lead time, and other parameters are assumed to be **known with certainty and constant** (i.e., non-stochastic). Because randomness is eliminated, the optimal replenishment policy can be derived analytically through classical calculus or marginal analysis, yielding exact, repeatable decision values.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3**
> Deterministic models form the foundation of Operations Research-based inventory control. KTU examiners expect students to differentiate between *instantaneous* replenishment (EOQ) and *finite* replenishment (EPQ), and to handle both **with** and **without quantity discounts**.

The major cost drivers in a deterministic model are:

| Cost Element | Symbol | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Purchase / Material Cost | $C$ or $P$ | Variable with quantity |
| Ordering (Setup) Cost | $S$ or $K$ | Fixed per order |
| Holding (Carrying) Cost | $H$ or $h$ | Per unit per period |
| Shortage (Stockout) Cost | $S_h$ or $\pi$ | Per unit short per period |
| Total Relevant Cost | $TC$ | Objective function |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Grocery Store Story

Imagine you own a small kirana store in Kerala. You buy rice from a wholesaler.

- Every time you travel to the wholesale godown to place an order, you spend **₹500 on auto-rickshaw fare + labour time**. This is your *ordering cost* — it does not depend on *how many* bags you buy.
- Every bag of rice that sits in your shop occupies space, attracts rats, and ties up your cash. Roughly **20% of the bag's value per year** is your *holding cost*.
- You sell a fixed number of bags per day (e.g., 10) — this is *deterministic demand*.

**The core dilemma**: If you order too frequently (say, 1 bag at a time), your auto-rickshaw bills skyrocket. If you order too infrequently (say, 1000 bags at a time), your shop becomes a warehouse and rats celebrate. The **EOQ** is the *Goldilocks quantity* — exactly the amount that minimises the sum of these two opposing costs.

Now add the **quantity discount twist**: the wholesaler says, "If you buy 500+ bags, I'll give you a 5% discount; if you buy 1000+, a 10% discount." Now the question is no longer just "how much?" but also "is the discount worth the extra holding cost?" This is the **Quantity Discount Model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definitions (KTU-Viva Ready)**
> - **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity):** The order quantity that minimises the *sum* of ordering and holding costs (ignoring discounts).
> - **Reorder Point (ROP):** The inventory level at which a new order is triggered; for deterministic, instantaneous lead time $L$, $ROP = D \times L$.
> - **Quantity Discount:** A price-break schedule offered by a supplier where the unit purchase price drops at certain order quantity thresholds.
> - **All-Units Discount:** The discounted price applies to *every* unit in the order once the threshold is crossed.
> - **Incremental Discount:** Only the *units beyond* the threshold receive the lower price.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Classical EOQ Model

The basic EOQ is built on a set of simplifying assumptions. The KTU examiner will typically award 1–2 marks simply for listing these clearly.

1. Demand rate $D$ is **constant and known** (deterministic).
2. Lead time $L$ is **constant and known**.
3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate).
4. No **quantity discounts** are available (basic version).
5. Ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are **constant** per order / per unit per year.
6. **Stockouts are not permitted** (no shortage).
7. The planning horizon is **infinite** (continuous review).

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — The Logic Behind the Formula

The total cost function is the sum of three components:

1. **Material / Purchase Cost:** $TC_{\text{purchase}} = D \cdot C$ — constant with respect to $Q$, hence does *not* affect the optimal $Q$.
2. **Ordering Cost:** $TC_{\text{order}} = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ — decreases as $Q$ grows (fewer orders).
3. **Holding Cost:** $TC_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ — increases as $Q$ grows (more average inventory).

The **optimal $Q$** lies where the marginal ordering cost savings equals the marginal holding cost penalty — a beautiful application of marginal economic theory.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Model | Key Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | EOQ (Basic) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = demand (units/yr), $S$ = order cost (₹/order), $H$ = holding cost (₹/unit/yr) |
| 2 | Total Cost (Basic) | $TC(Q) = D \cdot C + \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}$ | All terms in ₹/yr |
| 3 | Optimum Orders/yr | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}$ | Number of orders |
| 4 | Optimum Cycle Time | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | Years per cycle |
| 5 | Reorder Point | $ROP = D \cdot L$ | $L$ = lead time (yr) |
| 6 | EOQ with Shortages | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H + S_h}{S_h}}$ | $S_h$ = shortage cost (₹/unit/yr) |
| 7 | Max Inventory | $I_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{S_h}{H + S_h}}$ | Level just after order |
| 8 | EPQ (Production) | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | $p$ = production rate |
| 9 | All-Units Discount TC | $TC_j = D \cdot C_j + \dfrac{DS}{Q_j} + \dfrac{Q_j H_j}{2}$ | $j$ indexes price break |
| 10 | Holding Cost with Discount | $H_j = I \cdot C_j$ | $I$ = holding rate (decimal) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EOQ family of models is not just textbook content. It is actively used in:

- **Manufacturing plants** for raw material planning (steel, plastics, fasteners).
- **Pharmaceutical supply chains** for active ingredient ordering.
- **Automotive OEM** vendor-managed inventory systems.
- **E-commerce warehouses** for SKU-level replenishment (Amazon, Flipkart).
- **Spare parts inventory** for industrial machinery maintenance (MRO).

## 2.5 Quantity Discount Models — Two Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 frequently tests the "all-units discount" variant. The incremental variant is rarer but has appeared in 2022 and 2023 question papers.**

### 2.5.1 All-Units Quantity Discount
Every unit in the order receives the **lower** price once the break quantity is met.

**Stepwise Algorithm:**
1. Start at the **lowest price break** (cheapest unit cost).
2. Compute the EOQ at that price: $Q_j^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{I \cdot C_j}}$.
3. If $Q_j^* \ge$ break quantity → it is feasible; this is the **optimal order quantity**.
4. If $Q_j^*$ is **not feasible** (smaller than the break), **adjust upward** to the break quantity, and recompute total cost.
5. Move to the next higher price tier and repeat.
6. Compare total costs of all feasible candidates and pick the **minimum**.

### 2.5.2 Incremental Quantity Discount
Units in each price slab are charged at the slab's price. The total cost function is piecewise linear in the purchase component but the ordering/holding logic remains similar.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basic EOQ

We define the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** — only the components that depend on $Q$:

$$
TRC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering}} \; + \; \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding}}
$$

The purchase cost $D \cdot C$ is dropped because it is constant with respect to $Q$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $TRC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$.

$$
\frac{d\,TRC}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Set the derivative to zero for the optimum.

$$
-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $Q$.

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Confirm second-order condition (concavity).

$$
\frac{d^2\,TRC}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the function is **convex**, confirming that the critical point is a **global minimum**. The second-order condition is what KTU examiners look for as a sign of mathematical rigour.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Basic EOQ

> **Problem:** A company uses 12,000 units/year of a component. The ordering cost is ₹150 per order, and the holding cost is ₹6 per unit per year. Determine the EOQ, number of orders per year, and total cost (purchase cost ₹20/unit).

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
D = 12{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad S = 150 \text{ ₹/order}, \quad H = 6 \text{ ₹/unit/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EOQ:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 150}{6}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}600{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{600{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^* \approx 774.6 \text{ units}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of orders per year:**

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{12{,}000}{774.6} \approx 15.49 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Annual Cost:**

$$
TC = D \cdot C + \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{Q^* H}{2}
$$

$$
TC = 12{,}000(20) + \frac{12{,}000(150)}{774.6} + \frac{774.6(6)}{2}
$$

$$
TC = 240{,}000 + 2{,}323.85 + 2{,}323.85
$$

$$
\boxed{\,TC \approx 244{,}647.70 \text{ ₹/yr}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the elegant result: at the optimum, the ordering cost **exactly equals** the holding cost. This is a general property of EOQ solutions and a common 1-mark conceptual question in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Derivation of EOQ with Quantity Discount (All-Units)

When discounts are present, the holding cost is **price-dependent** because $H_j = I \cdot C_j$ (the holding cost rate $I$ is applied to the unit cost). The algorithm we derived conceptually now becomes a numerical search.

The relevant cost function for price tier $j$ is:

$$
TC_j(Q_j) = D \cdot C_j + \frac{D \cdot S}{Q_j} + \frac{Q_j \cdot I \cdot C_j}{2}
$$

**Step 1:** For each price tier $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, compute the EOQ at that tier's price:

$$
Q_j^{\text{EOQ}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{I \cdot C_j}}
$$

**Step 2:** Check feasibility. $Q_j^{\text{EOQ}}$ is feasible only if:

$$
Q_j^{\text{EOQ}} \ge q_j
$$

where $q_j$ is the minimum order quantity for that price tier.

**Step 3:** If infeasible, **adjust** $Q_j$ up to the lowest feasible quantity in that tier, which is $q_j$ (the break quantity itself). Then compute $TC_j$ at $Q_j = q_j$.

**Step 4:** Among all feasible $TC_j$ values, choose the one with the **minimum total cost**. The corresponding $Q_j$ is the optimal order quantity.

## 3.4 Worked Example — All-Units Quantity Discount

> **Problem:** Annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S = 200$ ₹/order. Holding rate $I = 0.20$ (i.e., 20% of unit value). Price schedule:
>
> | Price Tier | Min. Quantity | Unit Price (₹) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0 | 50.00 |
> | 2 | 500 | 48.00 |
> | 3 | 1000 | 46.00 |
>
> Determine the optimal order quantity.

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ for each tier:**

For Tier 1 ($C_1 = 50$, $H_1 = 0.20 \times 50 = 10$):

$$
Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} \approx 632.5 \text{ units}
$$

Feasibility check: $Q_1 = 632.5 \ge 0$ → **feasible**.

For Tier 2 ($C_2 = 48$, $H_2 = 0.20 \times 48 = 9.6$):

$$
Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{9.6}} = \sqrt{416{,}666.7} \approx 645.5 \text{ units}
$$

Feasibility check: $Q_2 = 645.5 \ge 500$ → **feasible**.

For Tier 3 ($C_3 = 46$, $H_3 = 0.20 \times 46 = 9.2$):

$$
Q_3 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{9.2}} = \sqrt{434{,}782.6} \approx 659.4 \text{ units}
$$

Feasibility check: $Q_3 = 659.4 \not\ge 1000$ → **infeasible**. Adjust to $Q_3 = 1000$.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Cost for each feasible candidate:**

For Tier 1 ($Q_1 = 632.5$):

$$
TC_1 = 10{,}000(50) + \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{632.5} + \frac{632.5 \times 10}{2}
$$

$$
TC_1 = 500{,}000 + 3{,}162.1 + 3{,}162.5 = 506{,}324.6 \text{ ₹}
$$

For Tier 2 ($Q_2 = 645.5$):

$$
TC_2 = 10{,}000(48) + \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{645.5} + \frac{645.5 \times 9.6}{2}
$$

$$
TC_2 = 480{,}000 + 3{,}098.4 + 3{,}098.4 = 486{,}196.8 \text{ ₹}
$$

For Tier 3 ($Q_3 = 1000$, adjusted):

$$
TC_3 = 10{,}000(46) + \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{1000} + \frac{1000 \times 9.2}{2}
$$

$$
TC_3 = 460{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 4{,}600 = 466{,}600 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Select minimum:**

$$
\min(506{,}324.6, \; 486{,}196.8, \; 466{,}600) = 466{,}600 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^* = 1000 \text{ units, at Tier 3 (₹46/unit), Total Cost} = ₹466{,}600\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even though the EOQ of the cheapest tier was infeasible, the company benefits by ordering **more than the EOQ at the lowest price**. The savings from the price discount outweigh the extra holding cost — a classic KTU "exam trap" that catches students who mechanically apply $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ without feasibility checks.

## 3.5 Derivation — EOQ with Planned Shortages

In some systems, deliberate stockouts are allowed (backordering). Adding shortage cost $S_h$ (per unit per year), the modified EOQ is:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H + S_h}{S_h}}
$$

The maximum inventory (just after replenishment) is:

$$
I_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{S_h}{H + S_h}}
$$

And the maximum shortage (backorder level) is:

$$
S_{\max} = Q^* - I_{\max}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation — Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)

When replenishment is **gradual** (production rate $p$ exceeds demand rate $d$), the inventory builds up at rate $(p - d)$ and is consumed at rate $d$. The modified formula is:

$$
Q_p^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{p - d}}
$$

Here, the average inventory is $\dfrac{I_{\max}}{2} = \dfrac{Q_p^*}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$, and the holding cost is computed on this reduced average inventory.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Level vs. Time — Basic EOQ Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Order Placed Q units arrive"] --> nodeB["Inventory at maximum I max"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Steady consumption at rate D"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Inventory hits zero Reorder trigger"]
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeA:::cycleNode
    nodeB:::peakNode
    nodeC:::depleteNode
    nodeD:::triggerNode
    classDef cycleNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a
    classDef peakNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a
    classDef depleteNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a
    classDef triggerNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#0f172a
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular "sawtooth" represents one EOQ cycle. Inventory jumps from 0 to $Q$ instantly on order arrival (assumption 3), then depletes linearly at rate $D$ until it hits 0 — at which point a new order is placed. The cycle length is $T = Q/D$.

## 4.2 Total Cost vs. Order Quantity — Cost Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph U1["COST BEHAVIOUR WITH Q"]
        nodeA1["Ordering Cost Curve DS over Q - Hyperbolic decreasing"]
        nodeA2["Holding Cost Curve QH over 2 - Linear increasing"]
        nodeA3["Total Cost Curve Sum of both - Convex U shaped"]
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeA3
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4["EOQ Star at minimum of TC"]
    nodeA1:::costNode1
    nodeA2:::costNode2
    nodeA3:::costNode3
    nodeA4:::optNode
    classDef costNode1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8
    classDef costNode2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    classDef costNode3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    classDef optNode fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The downward-sloping ordering cost curve and the upward-sloping holding cost curve intersect **exactly at the minimum of the total cost curve**. This intersection is a graphical proof of the fact that at $Q^*$, ordering cost equals holding cost.

## 4.3 Algorithm Flow — All-Units Quantity Discount Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA0["START Collect D S I and price schedule"] --> nodeB0["Set j equals 1 lowest price tier"]
    nodeB0 --> nodeC0["Compute EOQ at C sub j using formula"]
    nodeC0 --> nodeD0["Check feasibility Qj greater than equal to qj"]
    nodeD0 -- YES --> nodeE0["Record Qj and TC sub j as candidate"]
    nodeD0 -- NO --> nodeF0["Adjust Qj equals break quantity qj"]
    nodeF0 --> nodeE0
    nodeE0 --> nodeG0{"More price tiers to check?"}
    nodeG0 -- YES --> nodeH0["Increment j by 1 move to next tier"]
    nodeH0 --> nodeC0
    nodeG0 -- NO --> nodeI0["Select minimum TC sub j across all candidates"]
    nodeI0 --> nodeJ0["OPTIMAL Q Star and Price Tier"]
    nodeA0:::startNode
    nodeC0:::processNode
    nodeD0:::decisionNode
    nodeE0:::recordNode
    nodeF0:::adjustNode
    nodeG0:::decisionNode
    nodeI0:::selectNode
    nodeJ0:::endNode
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
