# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the assumptions of the classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model.**

**Model Answer:**

The classical EOQ model is built on the following assumptions (any 6 valid points earn full 3 marks):

1. The demand rate $D$ is **constant and deterministic** over time.
2. The lead time $L$ is **known and constant**.
3. Replenishment is **instantaneous** — the entire order quantity arrives in a single lot.
4. The ordering cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are **fixed and known**.
5. **No stockouts are permitted** (i.e., the model is for the no-shortage case).
6. The unit purchase price is **constant** — quantity discounts are not considered in the basic version.
7. The planning horizon is **infinite** (long-run steady-state operation).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between "all-units" and "incremental" quantity discount models.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | All-Units Discount | Incremental Discount |
|---|---|---|
| Price applied to | **Every unit** in the order once the break is reached | **Only the units** in that particular price slab |
| Unit cost function | Step function (jumps down at breaks) | Piecewise linear (each slab has its own price) |
| Purchase cost | $D \cdot C_j$ for chosen tier | $\sum_j (\text{units}_j \times C_j)$ |
| Typical use | Bulk chemical/raw material supply | Government rate contracts, electricity tariffs |
| KTU exam frequency | **Higher** | Moderate |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Rule:** Each Part B question offers internal choice between Question A and Question B on a *different but related* sub-topic.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

The annual demand for a raw material in a manufacturing plant is **8,000 units**. The ordering cost is **₹250 per order**, and the holding cost is **₹4 per unit per year**. The lead time is **15 days** (assume 360-day year). The supplier offers the following all-units quantity discount schedule:

| Tier | Min. Quantity | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 20.00 |
| 2 | 400 | 19.00 |
| 3 | 800 | 18.50 |

**(a) Compute the basic EOQ and total annual inventory cost assuming a constant unit price of ₹20. Also determine the reorder point.** (7 marks)

**(b) Determine the optimal order quantity under the quantity discount schedule. Justify your answer with a feasibility check.** (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Given:** $D = 8000$ units/yr, $S = ₹250$/order, $H = ₹4$/unit/yr, $C = ₹20$, $L = 15$ days.

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 250}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^* = 1000 \text{ units}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of orders per year:**

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8000}{1000} = 8 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Total annual cost:**

$$
TC = D \cdot C + \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{Q^* H}{2}
$$

$$
TC = 8000(20) + \frac{8000 \times 250}{1000} + \frac{1000 \times 4}{2}
$$

$$
TC = 160{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 164{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Reorder Point:**

Daily demand $d = D / 360 = 8000 / 360 = 22.22$ units/day.

$$
ROP = d \times L = 22.22 \times 15 \approx 333.3 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,ROP \approx 334 \text{ units}\,}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ at each price tier** (using $H_j = I \cdot C_j$ with the implied holding rate $I = H/C_1 = 4/20 = 0.20$):

Tier 1 ($C_1 = 20$, $H_1 = 4$):

$$
Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 250}{4}} = 1000 \text{ units} \quad (\text{feasible}, \ge 0)
$$

Tier 2 ($C_2 = 19$, $H_2 = 0.20 \times 19 = 3.8$):

$$
Q_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 250}{3.8}} = \sqrt{1{,}052{,}631.6} \approx 1026.1 \text{ units} \quad (\text{feasible}, \ge 400)
$$

Tier 3 ($C_3 = 18.5$, $H_3 = 0.20 \times 18.5 = 3.7$):

$$
Q_3 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 250}{3.7}} = \sqrt{1{,}081{,}081.1} \approx 1039.8 \text{ units}
$$

Feasibility check: $1039.8 \not\ge 800$? Actually $1039.8 \ge 800$ → **feasible**.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Cost for each tier:**

**Tier 1** ($Q = 1000$):

$$
TC_1 = 8000(20) + \frac{8000 \times 250}{1000} + \frac{1000 \times 4}{2} = 160{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 164{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Tier 2** ($Q = 1026.1$):

$$
TC_2 = 8000(19) + \frac{8000 \times 250}{1026.1} + \frac{1026.1 \times 3.8}{2}
$$

$$
TC_2 = 152{,}000 + 1{,}949.4 + 1{,}949.6 = 155{,}899 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Tier 3** ($Q = 1039.8$):

$$
TC_3 = 8000(18.5) + \frac{8000 \times 250}{1039.8} + \frac{1039.8 \times 3.7}{2}
$$

$$
TC_3 = 148{,}000 + 1{,}923.5 + 1{,}923.6 = 151{,}847.1 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Select minimum:**

$$
\min(164{,}000,\; 155{,}899,\; 151{,}847.1) = 151{,}847.1
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q^* = 1039.8 \text{ units, at Tier 3 (₹18.50/unit), Total Cost} = ₹151{,}847.10\,}
$$

**Annual savings vs. Tier 1:** $164{,}000 - 151{,}847.10 = ₹12{,}152.90$ — a substantial benefit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A company uses a component at the rate of **36,000 units per year**. The ordering cost is **₹400 per order**, and the holding cost is **20% of the unit cost per year**. The unit cost of the component is **₹50**. Backordering is permitted at a cost of **₹5 per unit per year**.

**(a) Determine the EOQ, the maximum inventory, and the maximum backorder level. Also compute the total annual cost.** (7 marks)

**(b) If the company is offered a price of ₹48 per unit on orders of 2000 or more, should the company accept the offer? Justify with total cost analysis.** (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Given:** $D = 36{,}000$ units/yr, $S = ₹400$/order, $C = ₹50$, $I = 0.20$, $H = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10$/unit/yr, $S_h = ₹5$/unit/yr.

**Step 1 — Compute EOQ with shortages:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400}{10}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{10 + 5}{5}}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{2{,}880{,}000} \cdot \sqrt{3} = 1697.06 \times 1.7321 \approx 2939.4 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum inventory:**

$$
I_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400}{10}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{5}{10 + 5}}
$$

$$
I_{\max} = 1697.06 \times \sqrt{1/3} = 1697.06 \times 0.5774 \approx 979.8 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum backorder level:**

$$
S_{\max} = Q^* - I_{\max} = 2939.4 - 979.8 \approx 1959.6 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Total cost:**

$$
TC = D \cdot C + \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{I_{\max}^2 \cdot H}{2 Q^*} + \frac{S_{\max}^2 \cdot S_h}{2 Q^*}
$$

$$
TC = 36{,}000(50) + \frac{36{,}000 \times 400}{2939.4} + \frac{(979.8)^2 \times 10}{2 \times 2939.4} + \frac{(1959.6)^2 \times 5}{2 \times 2939.4}
$$

$$
TC = 1{,}800{,}000 + 4{,}899.4 + 1{,}633.1 + 3{,}266.3
$$

$$
\boxed{\,TC \approx 1{,}809{,}798.80 \text{ ₹/yr}\,}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — New unit cost** $C' = ₹48$, new holding cost $H' = 0.20 \times 48 = ₹9.6$/unit/yr.

**Step 2 — New EOQ (without shortage) at discounted price:**

$$
Q_{\text{new}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 36{,}000 \times 400}{9.6}} = \sqrt{3{,}000{,}000} \approx 1732.05 \text{ units}
$$

Feasibility check: $Q_{\text{new}} = 1732.05 \not\ge 2000$ → **infeasible** at the discount tier.

**Step 3 — Adjust to break quantity** $Q' = 2000$.

**Step 4 — Compute total cost at adjusted quantity** (assume backordering still allowed, but for simplicity, ignore for comparison):

Using the no-shortage version for cleaner comparison:

$$
TC_{\text{discount}} = 36{,}000(48) + \frac{36{,}000 \times 400}{2000} + \frac{2000 \times 9.6}{2}
$$

$$
TC_{\text{discount}} = 1{,}728{,}000 + 7{,}200 + 9{,}600 = 1{,}744{,}800 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with original total cost (₹1,809,798.80):**

$$
\text{Savings} = 1{,}809{,}798.80 - 1{,}744{,}800 = 64{,}998.80 \text{ ₹/yr}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Yes, the company should accept the discount offer. Annual savings} \approx ₹64{,}999.\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting the feasibility check:** Many students compute EOQ at the cheapest price and declare it optimal without checking if it meets the minimum break quantity. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 2–3 marks for this.
> 2. **Including $D \cdot C$ in the EOQ minimisation:** $D \cdot C$ is constant in $Q$ and **must be excluded** from the derivative. Students who differentiate $D \cdot C$ and set it to zero commit a conceptual error.
> 3. **Mixing up $H$ and $I \cdot C$:** In discount problems, students often use the original $H$ for all tiers. The correct $H_j = I \cdot C_j$ **changes** with each price tier.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** $D$ in units/year and $L$ in days — always convert to a common time base (typically annual) before applying formulas.
> 5. **Confusing the role of shortage cost:** In the EOQ-with-shortage model, the shortage cost $S_h$ is **per unit per year of being short**, not a one-time penalty. Read the question wording carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3 (Deterministic Models)**

- [ ] **EOQ formula** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derived by setting $\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} = 0$ and verified by $\frac{d^2\,TC}{dQ^2} > 0$.
- [ ] At $Q^*$, **ordering cost = holding cost** (a quick consistency check).
- [ ] Total relevant cost = $D \cdot C + \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$; the purchase term drops out during differentiation.
- [ ] $N^* = D/Q^*$ orders/yr, $T^* = Q^*/D$ years/cycle, $ROP = D \cdot L$.
- [ ] **All-units discount** = discounted price applies to *all* units once break is met.
- [ ] **Incremental discount** = each price slab charges its own price to the units in that slab.
- [ ] **Discount algorithm:** Compute EOQ at each tier → check feasibility → adjust to break quantity if infeasible → compare total costs.
- [ ] **Holding cost with discount** is $H_j = I \cdot C_j$ (rate $I$ applied to *each* tier's price).
- [ ] **EOQ with shortages:** multiply basic EOQ by $\sqrt{(H + S_h)/S_h}$; max inventory uses $\sqrt{S_h/(H+S_h)}$.
- [ ] **EPQ (production):** multiply basic EOQ by $\sqrt{p/(p-d)}$; inventory builds at rate $(p-d)$ during production.
- [ ] **Second-order condition** $\frac{d^2\,TC}{dQ^2} > 0$ confirms a minimum — always state it in derivations.
- [ ] Convert all time units to a **common base** (year) before plugging into formulas.
- [ ] **Sketch the sawtooth inventory diagram** for full-method questions — it earns easy 1–2 marks.
- [ ] Common traps: forgetting feasibility checks, mixing $H$ and $I \cdot C$, dropping the second-order test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Forecasting - Types of Forecasting, Demand Patterns
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Demand Forecasting - Types of Forecasting, Demand Patterns

# Demand Forecasting: Types of Forecasting & Demand Patterns

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Demand Forecasting** is the systematic, scientific, and analytical process of estimating the **future demand** of a product or service over a specified time horizon, using historical data, market intelligence, and quantitative/statistical techniques, to facilitate **production planning, inventory control, capacity planning, and strategic decision-making** in an industrial organization.

In the context of **Production Management**, demand forecasting serves as the **primary input** for:
- **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)**
- **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**
- **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**
- **Inventory Management Models (EOQ, ROP, Safety Stock)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *"Forecasting is a process of predicting future events that will have a major impact on the organization. It is a plan of action to meet the demand and supply situation prevailing in the market."*

The forecasting horizon is generally classified into:
- **Short-Range Forecast:** Up to **1 year** (daily/weekly/monthly basis) — used for **production scheduling & inventory**
- **Medium-Range Forecast:** **3 months to 3 years** — used for **sales planning & budgeting**
- **Long-Range Forecast:** **3 years and above** — used for **strategic planning & capacity expansion**

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World "Plain English" Explanation)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Monsoon Prediction for a Kerala Farmer**
>
> Imagine a cardamom farmer in **Munnar, Kerala**. Before sowing seeds, he looks at:
> - Last 5 years of rainfall data (Historical patterns)
> - Current cloud formations and sea-surface temperature (Market intelligence)
> - Government meteorological reports (Statistical models)
>
> He is essentially **forecasting** — predicting how much rain will fall so he can decide **how much to plant**.
>
> If he over-forecasts rain → he plants too much → surplus wither = **Loss**
> If he under-forecasts rain → he plants too little → missed opportunity = **Lost Revenue**
>
> **A factory is identical to this farmer.** The "rain" is **customer demand**, the "seeds" are **raw materials**, and the "harvest" is **finished goods inventory**. Demand forecasting is the *factory's weather report*.

---

### 1.3 Significance & Objectives of Demand Forecasting

The key objectives (high-weight KTU answers) are:

1. **Production Planning:** Determines *what*, *how much*, and *when* to produce.
2. **Inventory Control:** Helps maintain optimum stock — avoiding both **stockouts** and **overstocking**.
3. **Cost Reduction:** Reduces **holding cost**, **ordering cost**, and **shortage cost**.
4. **Capacity Planning:** Helps decide whether to add machinery, labour, or shifts.
5. **Financial Planning:** Forms the basis for **budgeting** and **revenue projections**.
6. **Manpower Planning:** Determines future workforce requirements.
7. **Marketing Decisions:** Guides pricing, promotions, and channel decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Characteristics of a Good Forecast (Chase & Aquilano):**
> 1. Accurate
> 2. Timely
> 3. Reliable
> 4. Easy to understand
> 5. Cost-effective
> 6. Flexible
> 7. Stable

---

### 1.4 Forecast Error Metrics — Foundational Definitions

Before diving into types, we define the four **critical error metrics** universally used in KTU problems:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Forecast Error (eₜ):** Actual Demand − Forecasted Demand → $e_t = D_t - F_t$
> - **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD):** Average of |absolute errors|
> - **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Average of squared errors
> - **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE):** Average of |percentage errors|
> - **Tracking Signal (TS):** Ratio of cumulative error to MAD → detects **bias**

These will be derived exhaustively in **SECTION 3**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Grand Families of Forecasting

KTU frequently frames the first 7-mark answer around this dichotomy:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forecasting Techniques are classified into TWO primary families:**
>
> | **Family** | **Also Known As** | **Basis** | **Data Requirement** |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Qualitative Forecasting** | Judgmental / Subjective Methods | Opinion, judgment, intuition, market research | No historical data required |
> | **Quantitative Forecasting** | Statistical / Mathematical Methods | Numerical historical data + mathematical models | Requires historical time-series data |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Forecasting Methods

### A. Qualitative (Judgmental) Forecasting Methods

| **Method** | **Description** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **Executive Opinion** | Top management pool their judgments collectively | Strategic long-range planning |
| **Sales Force Composite** | Salesmen estimate demand in their territory | Short-range forecasts |
| **Delphi Method** | Iterative, anonymous expert questionnaire rounds | New product launch, technology forecasting |
| **Market Research** | Customer surveys, interviews, focus groups | New markets, product acceptance |
| **Historical Analogy** | Compare new product with similar past product | Genuinely new products |

> [!TIP]
> **The Delphi Method** is a **favorite KTU question**. Key features:
> - Uses a **panel of experts** (typically 5–10).
> - Three Rounds: (i) Open questionnaire → (ii) Feedback to experts → (iii) Final consensus.
> - All communication is **anonymous** to avoid groupthink / dominant-person bias.

---

### B. Quantitative (Statistical) Forecasting Methods

These are divided into **Time-Series Models** (intrinsic patterns) and **Causal / Associative Models** (extrinsic relationships).

#### B.1 Time-Series Models

These assume that **past demand pattern = future demand pattern**, with adjustments for trend & seasonality.

| **Model** | **Key Formula Notation** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Naïve Forecast** | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | Trivial baseline |
| **Simple (Arithmetic) Average** | $F_{t+1} = \frac{\sum D_i}{n}$ | Stable demand, no trend |
| **Moving Average (MA)** | $F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + \cdots + D_{t-n+1}}{n}$ | Stable demand, smoothes random variation |
| **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_i$ where $\sum w_i = 1$ | Recent data weighted more |
| **Exponential Smoothing (ES)** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | Adaptive, low data storage |
| **Trend-Adjusted ES (Holt's Method)** | Uses level $L_t$ and trend $T_t$ | Linear trend present |
| **Seasonal ES (Holt-Winters)** | Level + Trend + Seasonal indices | Trend + Seasonality |

#### B.2 Causal / Associative Models

| **Model** | **Mathematical Form** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Regression** | $y = a + bx$ | One independent variable |
| **Multiple Regression** | $y = a + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \cdots$ | Multiple independent variables |
| **Econometric Models** | System of simultaneous equations | Macroeconomic forecasting |
| **Leading Indicators** | Cross-industry leading variables | Economic turning points |

---

## 2.3 Demand Patterns — The Heart of Forecasting

Demand in a time series exhibits **four fundamental patterns**. Every KTU forecasting problem is essentially identifying which pattern dominates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Demand Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Horizontal (Stationary / Constant)** — Demand fluctuates randomly around a constant mean. Example: Sales of milk, bread.
> 2. **Trend (Upward / Downward)** — Demand systematically increases or decreases over time. Example: Mobile phone sales 2015–2023.
> 3. **Seasonality** — Predictable, recurring pattern within a year (typically). Example: AC sales peak in summer; umbrella in monsoon.
> 4. **Cyclical (Business Cycle)** — Longer-term wave-like swings tied to economic cycles. Example: Real estate boom-bust cycles (5–10 years).
> 5. **Random / Irregular Variation** — Unpredictable, non-recurring noise. Example: COVID-19 demand shock, sudden strikes.

### Mathematical Representation of a Time Series

$$X_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

Where:
- $X_t$ = Actual demand at time $t$
- $T_t$ = Trend component
- $S_t$ = Seasonal component
- $C_t$ = Cyclical component
- $I_t$ = Irregular / Random component

> [!NOTE]
> **Additive Model:** Used when seasonal variation is **constant** over time.
> **Multiplicative Model:** $X_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$ — used when seasonal variation **grows proportionally** with trend.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are derived from first principles in SECTION 3.**

### A. Forecast Error Metrics

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Interpretation** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forecast Error | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | Signed deviation | Units |
| Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) | $MAD = \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$ | Average magnitude of error | Units |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $MSE = \frac{\sum e_t^2}{n}$ | Penalizes large errors | Units² |
| Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) | $MAPE = \frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{\vert e_t \vert}{D_t}\times 100$ | Relative error | % |
| Cumulative Error (CE / RSFE) | $CE = \sum e_t$ | Total bias | Units |
| Tracking Signal (TS) | $TS = \frac{CE}{MAD}$ | Detects bias | Dimensionless |

### B. Forecasting Models

| **Model** | **Formula** | **Smoothing Constant Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Naïve | $F_{t+1} = D_t$ | — |
| Simple Average | $F_{t+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i}{n}$ | — |
| n-Period Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} D_{t-i}}{n}$ | — |
| Weighted Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_i$, $\sum w_i = 1$ | — |
| Simple Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(D_t - F_t)$ | $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$ |
| Linear Trend (Least Squares) | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | — |
| Seasonal Index | $SI = \frac{\text{Average demand for season}}{\text{Overall average demand}}$ | — |

### C. Least Squares Regression Coefficients

| **Coefficient** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| Slope $b$ | $b = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ |
| Intercept $a$ | $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ |
| Correlation Coefficient $r$ | $r = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{[n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2][n\sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2]}}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automobile Industry:** Uses **Delphi Method** for new vehicle concept forecasting.
- **Apparel/FMCG:** Uses **Holt-Winters** for seasonal demand (winter wear, ice cream).
- **Power Sector:** Uses **Multiple Regression** with temperature/humidity as leading variables.
- **Retail (Big-Basket, Amazon):** Uses **ML-driven hybrid models** combining ARIMA + Neural Networks.
- **Aerospace:** Uses **Econometric + Leading Indicator** models for long-range fleet planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forecast Error & MAD

### Step-by-Step Logic

Given a series of actual demands $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n$ and corresponding forecasts $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n$.

**Step 1:** Define forecast error at any period $t$ as the deviation of actual from forecast:

$$e_t = D_t - F_t$$

**Step 2:** Since errors can be positive (over-forecast) or negative (under-forecast), we take absolute values to obtain magnitude:

$$\vert e_t \vert = \vert D_t - F_t \vert$$

**Step 3:** Sum all absolute errors over $n$ periods:

$$E_{abs} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $n$ to obtain the average absolute deviation (MAD):

$$MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MAD and not just average error?** Because positive and negative errors can cancel out, giving a misleadingly small average. MAD uses absolute values to measure *magnitude* of inaccuracy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Squared Error (MSE) and RMSE

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Square each error to eliminate sign and amplify large errors:

$$e_t^2 = (D_t - F_t)^2$$

**Step 2:** Sum all squared errors:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^2$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $n$:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)^2$$

**Step 4:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is often preferred since MSE is in squared units:

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (D_t - F_t)^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **MSE penalizes large errors MORE heavily** than MAD. If one big miss is catastrophic (e.g., blood bank inventory), MSE is preferred. If errors are equally bad regardless of size (e.g., bread stockout), MAD is preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Convert each absolute error into a percentage of actual demand:

$$\text{Percentage Error}_t = \frac{\vert D_t - F_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Sum all percentage errors:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert D_t - F_t \vert}{D_t} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $n$:

$$MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert D_t - F_t \vert}{D_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAPE is dimensionless** and excellent for comparing forecast accuracy **across different products** with different units.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cumulative Error (CE) and Tracking Signal (TS)

### Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Cumulative Error (CE), also called **Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)**:

$$CE_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} e_i = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (D_i - F_i)$$

**Step 2:** A persistent positive CE indicates **systematic under-forecasting** (actual demand consistently exceeds forecast). A persistent negative CE indicates **systematic over-forecasting**.

**Step 3:** Tracking Signal normalizes the cumulative error by the typical magnitude of error (MAD):

$$TS_t = \frac{CE_t}{MAD_t}$$

**Step 4 (Control Limit):** In practice, a forecast is considered "in control" when:

$$\vert TS_t \vert \leq 4$$

(Threshold of ±4 is widely used; some firms use ±5 or ±6).

> [!NOTE]
> If $\vert TS \vert > 4$, the forecasting model has **bias** and must be re-tuned (adjust $\alpha$ or re-fit regression).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Simple Exponential Smoothing — Recursive Form

### Step-by-Step Logic

The idea: blend the **most recent observation** with the **most recent forecast**, using a smoothing constant $\alpha$.

**Step 1:** New forecast = weighted combination of actual demand and old forecast:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange by adding and subtracting $F_t$:

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(D_t - F_t)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $(D_t - F_t)$ is the forecast error $e_t$:

$$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha \cdot e_t$$

**Step 4 (Expanded Form):** Substitute recursively:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) D_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 D_{t-2} + \cdots$$

**Step 5:** Observation: All weights sum to 1 (verify):

$$\alpha [1 + (1-\alpha) + (1-\alpha)^2 + \cdots] = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{1-(1-\alpha)} = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Older observations receive **exponentially decaying weights**, but never zero — making exponential smoothing **memory-efficient** (only $F_t$ and $D_t$ need to be stored, not entire history).

**Choice of $\alpha$:**
- High $\alpha$ (close to 1) → forecast reacts quickly to recent changes (good for **volatile/trending** demand).
- Low $\alpha$ (close to 0) → forecast smoothes out noise (good for **stationary** demand).
- Common practice: try $\alpha = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, \ldots, 0.9$ and pick the one minimizing MSE.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem:** Actual demand for a product for the last 5 months is: 100, 120, 115, 130, 140.
Forecast for month 1 was 110 (assume $\alpha = 0.3$ for exponential smoothing).
Forecast for month 2 onwards was generated using Simple Exponential Smoothing.
Compute MAD, MSE, MAPE, CE, and TS at the end of month 5.

### Solution Table

| Month $t$ | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 110 (given) | $-10$ | 10 | 100 | 10.00% |
| 2 | 120 | $F_2 = 0.3(100) + 0.7(110) = 107$ | $+13$ | 13 | 169 | 10.83% |
| 3 | 115 | $F_3 = 0.3(120) + 0.7(107) = 110.9$ | $+4.1$ | 4.1 | 16.81 | 3.57% |
| 4 | 130 | $F_4 = 0.3(115) + 0.7(110.9) = 112.13$ | $+17.87$ | 17.87 | 319.34 | 13.75% |
| 5 | 140 | $F_5 = 0.3(130) + 0.7(112.13) = 117.491$ | $+22.509$ | 22.509 | 506.65 | 16.08% |
| **Σ** | — | — | **47.479** | **67.479** | **1111.80** | **54.23%** |

### Calculations

**MAD:**

$$MAD = \frac{67.479}{5} = 13.4958 \text{ units}$$

**MSE:**

$$MSE = \frac{1111.80}{5} = 222.36 \text{ units}^2$$

**MAPE:**

$$MAPE = \frac{54.23}{5} = 10.846\%$$

**Cumulative Error (CE):**

$$CE = 47.479 \text{ units}$$

**Tracking Signal:**

$$TS = \frac{CE}{MAD} = \frac{47.479}{13.4958} = 3.518$$

**Interpretation:** $|TS| = 3.52 < 4$, so the forecast is **in control**, but approaching bias. We might try a higher $\alpha$ next cycle.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Choosing the Right Forecasting Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-weight KTU answer.**

| **Demand Pattern** | **Best-Fit Forecasting Model** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal (stable)** | Simple Exponential Smoothing | Adapts to random fluctuations, minimal data needed |
| **Horizontal (stable)** | Naïve / Simple Average | Trivial baseline, works for very stable items |
| **Trend (linear)** | Holt's Method (Double ES) | Explicitly models level + trend |
| **Trend + Seasonality** | Holt-Winters Method | Adds seasonal index to level + trend |
| **Pure Seasonality (no trend)** | Seasonal Index Method (Multiplicative) | Decompose seasonal factors |
| **Cyclical + Causal** | Regression / Econometric Models | Links to macroeconomic indicators |
| **High Noise, No Pattern** | Moving Average | Smoothes random noise |
| **New Product (No History)** | Qualitative (Delphi, Market Research) | No data exists |

---

## 3.8 Forecasting Time Horizon Selection Matrix

| **Time Horizon** | **Planning Purpose** | **Typical Models Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **0–3 months (Short)** | Production scheduling, daily/weekly operations | Naïve, Exponential Smoothing, Moving Average |
| **3–18 months (Medium)** | Sales & operations planning (S&OP), budgeting | Holt's, Holt-Winters, Regression |
| **1–5 years (Long)** | Strategic capacity, location, technology | Multiple Regression, Econometric, Delphi |
| **5+ years** | R&D, new product platforms | Delphi, Scenario Planning, Cross-Impact Analysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Master Taxonomy of Forecasting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNode["Demand Forecasting Methods"]
    rootNode --> qualNode["Qualitative Methods"]
    rootNode --> quantNode["Quantitative Methods"]

    qualNode --> execNode["Executive Opinion"]
    qualNode --> salesNode["Sales Force Composite"]
    qualNode --> delphiNode["Delphi Method"]
    qualNode --> mrNode["Market Research"]
    qualNode --> analogyNode["Historical Analogy"]

    quantNode --> tsNode["Time Series Models"]
    quantNode --> causalNode["Causal Associative Models"]

    tsNode --> naiveNode["Naive Forecast"]
    tsNode --> saNode["Simple Average"]
    tsNode --> maNode["Moving Average"]
    tsNode --> wmaNode["Weighted Moving Average"]
    tsNode --> esNode["Simple Exponential Smoothing"]
    tsNode --> holtNode["Holt Method"]
    tsNode --> hwNode["Holt Winters"]

    causalNode --> lrNode["Linear Regression"]
    causalNode --> mr2Node["Multiple Regression"]
    causalNode --> ecoNode["Econometric Models"]
    causalNode --> liNode["Leading Indicators"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer Conceptual)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]`
> **Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative forecasting methods. Give two examples of each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Qualitative Forecasting** relies on **judgment, intuition, experience, and opinion** of experts rather than numerical data. It is used when historical data is unavailable, unreliable, or when forecasting new products or major technology shifts.

- **Examples:** (i) Delphi Method, (ii) Sales Force Composite.

**Quantitative Forecasting** relies on **mathematical and statistical models** applied to historical numerical data. It assumes that past demand patterns will continue into the future, with adjustments.

- **Examples:** (i) Simple Exponential Smoothing, (ii) Linear Regression.

**Key Difference:** Qualitative = subjective, no data needed; Quantitative = objective, requires time-series data.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`
> **List and briefly explain the four fundamental demand patterns in forecasting.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The four fundamental demand patterns are:

1. **Horizontal (Constant) Demand:** Demand fluctuates randomly around a constant mean over time. *Example:* Daily demand for milk or bread.

2. **Trend Pattern:** Demand systematically increases (upward trend) or decreases (downward trend) over a long period. *Example:* Smartphone sales rising every year.

3. **Seasonal Pattern:** Demand exhibits regular, predictable fluctuations within a year tied to weather, festivals, or calendar. *Example:* AC sales peak in summer, umbrellas in monsoon.

4. **Cyclical Pattern:** Demand moves in longer wave-like swings (typically 3–10 years) tied to economic business cycles. *Example:* Real estate boom-bust cycles.

(Random/Irregular variation is the unpredictable fifth component added on top.)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Write EITHER A OR B)

> **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both carry 14 marks.**

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the various **types of forecasting methods** with a suitable classification chart. Discuss the **Delphi Method** in detail with its key features, steps, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The demand for a product over the last 6 months is given below. Use **3-month Moving Average** and **Simple Exponential Smoothing (α = 0.4, F₁ = 50)** to forecast demand for month 7. Compute **MAD, MSE, and MAPE** for both methods and state which method is better. **(7 Marks)**

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 50 | 54 | 48 | 60 | 58 | 65 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Forecasting methods are classified into two broad families:

**(i) Qualitative Forecasting Methods** (subjective / judgmental): Used when no historical data is available.
- Executive Opinion, Sales Force Composite, Delphi Method, Market Research, Historical Analogy.

**(ii) Quantitative Forecasting Methods** (statistical / mathematical): Require historical time-series data.
- **Time-Series Models:** Naïve, Simple Average, Moving Average, Weighted MA, Simple Exponential Smoothing, Holt's Method, Holt-Winters.
- **Causal Models:** Linear Regression, Multiple Regression, Econometric Models, Leading Indicators.

**Delphi Method (Detailed):**

Developed by RAND Corporation in the 1950s, the Delphi Method is a **structured, iterative group forecasting technique** where a panel of experts is consulted anonymously over multiple rounds.

**Key Features:**
- Uses a **panel of 5–20 experts** from diverse backgrounds.
- **Anonymity** — experts do not meet or know each other → prevents groupthink / dominance bias.
- **Iteration** — multiple rounds (typically 3) of questionnaires.
- **Feedback** — after each round, a coordinator summarizes responses and shares statistics (mean, median, range) with the group.
- **Convergence** — process continues until expert opinions converge to a consensus.

**Steps:**
1. Select panel of experts.
2. Round 1: Open-ended questionnaire to identify key forecasting factors.
3. Round 2: Experts rate each factor; coordinator computes mean, median, and reasons for disagreement.
4. Round 3: Experts re-evaluate given group feedback; majority view converges.
5. Final aggregated forecast is produced.

**Advantages:** Reduces bias, accommodates diverse viewpoints, suitable for long-range and new-product forecasting.

**Limitations:** Time-consuming (weeks/months), expensive, dependent on expert selection quality.

**(b) Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Method 1: 3-Month Moving Average (MA-3)**

Forecast formula: $F_{t+1} = \frac{D_t + D_{t-1} + D_{t-2}}{3}$

| Month $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 54 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 3 | 48 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 4 | 60 | $(50+54+48)/3 = 50.667$ | $+9.333$ | 9.333 | 87.11 | 15.56% |
| 5 | 58 | $(54+48+60)/3 = 54.000$ | $+4.000$ | 4.000 | 16.00 | 6.90% |
| 6 | 65 | $(48+60+58)/3 = 55.333$ | $+9.667$ | 9.667 | 93.45 | 14.87% |

**Forecast for Month 7:** $F_7 = (60+58+65)/3 = 61.00$

**Error metrics (n = 3 forecasts):**

$$MAD = \frac{9.333 + 4.000 + 9.667}{3} = 7.667$$

$$MSE = \frac{87.11 + 16.00 + 93.45}{3} = 65.52$$

$$MAPE = \frac{15.56 + 6.90 + 14.87}{3} = 12.44\%$$

**Method 2: Simple Exponential Smoothing (α = 0.4, F₁ = 50)**

Formula: $F_{t+1} = 0.4 D_t + 0.6 F_t$

| Month $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50.000 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0% |
| 2 | 54 | $0.4(50)+0.6(50) = 50.000$ | $+4.000$ | 4.000 | 16.00 | 7.41% |
| 3 | 48 | $0.4(54)+0.6(50) = 51.600$ | $-3.600$ | 3.600 | 12.96 | 7.50% |
| 4 | 60 | $0.4(48)+0.6(51.6) = 50.160$ | $+9.840$ | 9.840 | 96.83 | 16.40% |
| 5 | 58 | $0.4(60)+0.6(50.16) = 54.096$ | $+3.904$ | 3.904 | 15.24 | 6.73% |
| 6 | 65 | $0.4(58)+0.6(54.096) = 55.658$ | $+9.342$ | 9.342 | 87.27 | 14.37% |

**Forecast for Month 7:** $F_7 = 0.4(65) + 0.6(55.658) = 59.395$

**Error metrics (n = 5 valid forecasts, months 2–6):**

$$MAD = \frac{0 + 4.000 + 3.600 + 9.840 + 3.904 + 9.342}{6} = 5.114$$

(Using all 6 months; or n = 5 if month 1 excluded since error = 0; KTU accepts either with explanation.)

$$MSE = \frac{0 + 16.00 + 12.96 + 96.83 + 15.24 + 87.27}{6} = 38.05$$

$$MAPE = \frac{0 + 7.41 + 7.50 + 16.40 + 6.73 + 14.37}{6} = 8.74\%$$

**Comparison:**

| **Metric** | **Moving Average** | **Exponential Smoothing** | **Better Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| MAD | 7.667 | 5.114 | ES |
| MSE | 65.52 | 38.05 | ES |
| MAPE | 12.44% | 8.74% | ES |

**Conclusion:** **Simple Exponential Smoothing (α = 0.4) is better** because it yields lower MAD, MSE, and MAPE — it adapts more quickly to recent demand changes.

**Final forecasts:**
- 3-MA: $F_7 = 61$
- ES: $F_7 = 59.4$ (preferred)

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Demand Patterns** in forecasting. Discuss the **additive and multiplicative models** of time-series decomposition with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The quarterly sales (in ₹ lakhs) of an AC brand are given below. The data shows **clear seasonality**. Compute the **seasonal indices** using the **ratio-to-moving-average method** and forecast sales for Q1 of next year. **(7 Marks)**

| Year | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2021 | 80 | 120 | 200 | 100 |
| 2022 | 100 | 140 | 220 | 120 |
| 2023 | 120 | 160 | 260 | 140 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Demand Patterns in Forecasting:**

Demand over time exhibits four fundamental patterns (see SECTION 2.3 for definitions). The actual observed demand $X_t$ at any time $t$ can be decomposed into four components:

**Mathematical Decomposition Models:**

$$X_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t \quad \text{(Additive Model)}$$

$$X_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t \quad \text{(Multiplicative Model)}$$

Where:
- $T_t$ = Trend component
- $S_t$ = Seasonal component
- $C_t$ = Cyclical component
- $I_t$ = Irregular / Random component

**Additive Model — When to Use:**
- Seasonal variation is **constant** in absolute magnitude over time.
- Example: Ice-cream sales increase by ~1000 units every summer, irrespective of overall growth.
- All components are in the **same units** as the original series.
- Decomposition can be done by simple subtraction.

**Multiplicative Model — When to Use:**
- Seasonal variation **grows proportionally** with the trend.
- Example: Diwali sales increase by **30%** every year (so the seasonal swing widens as sales grow).
- Components are **dimensionless ratios**.
- Decomposition is done by division; seasonal indices typically sum to **4.00** (for quarterly data) or **12.00** (for monthly).

**Conceptual Diagram (described textually as KTU does not allow physical diagrams in math answers):**

```
Observed Demand (X_t)
        |
        v
  +-------------+        +--------------+
  | TREND  T_t  |        | SEASONAL S_t |
  +-------------+        +--------------+
        |                      |
        +--------+-------------+
                 |
                 v   (Additive: + ; Multiplicative: x)
                 |
        +--------+--------+
        |  COMBINED       |
        +-----------------+
                 |
                 v
        +-----------------+
        |  CYCLICAL  C_t  |
        +-----------------+
                 |
                 v
        +-----------------+
        |  IRREGULAR  I_t |
        +-----------------+
                 |
                 v
        FINAL OBSERVED X_t
```

**(b) Part (b) — 7 Marks — Ratio-to-Moving-Average Method**

**Step 1:** Compute **4-quarter centered moving average** (since 4 quarters/year).

**Step 2:** Compute **ratio of actual demand to moving average** = approximate seasonal index.

**Step 3:** Arrange ratios by quarter, take average → final seasonal index. **Adjust so indices sum to 4.00.**

**Computation Table:**

| Year | Quarter | Sales $D_t$ | 4-Qtr MA | Ratio $(D_t / MA)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2021 | Q1 | 80 | — | — |
| 2021 | Q2 | 120 | $(80+120+200+100)/4 = 125.00$ | $120/125 = 0.960$ |
| 2021 | Q3 | 200 | $(120+200+100+100)/4 = 130.00$ | $200/130 = 1.538$ |
| 2021 | Q4 | 100 | $(200+100+100+140)/4 = 135.00$ | $100/135 = 0.741$ |
| 2022 | Q1 | 100 | $(100+100+140+220)/4 = 140.00$ | $100/140 = 0.714$ |
| 2022 | Q2 | 140 | $(100+140+220+120)/4 = 145.00$ | $140/145 = 0.966$ |
| 2022 | Q3 | 220 | $(140+220+120+120)/4 = 150.00$ | $220/150 = 1.467$ |
| 2022 | Q4 | 120 | $(220+120+120+160)/4 = 155.00$ | $120/155 = 0.774$ |
| 2023 | Q1 | 120 | $(120+120+160+260)/4 = 165.00$ | $120/165 = 0.727$ |
| 2023 | Q2 | 160 | $(120+160+260+140)/4 = 170.00$ | $160/170 = 0.941$ |
| 2023 | Q3 | 260 | $(160+260+140+140)/4 = 175.00$ | $260/175 = 1.486$ |
| 2023 | Q4 | 140 | — | — |

**Step 4:** Compute average ratio per quarter (Modified Mean — drop highest and lowest if any):

- **Q1 average:** $(0.714 + 0.727)/2 = 0.7205$
- **Q2 average:** $(0.960 + 0.966 + 0.941)/3 = 0.9557$
- **Q3 average:** $(1.538 + 1.467 + 1.486)/3 = 1.4970$
- **Q4 average:** $(0.741 + 0.774)/2 = 0.7575$

**Sum of averages:** $0.7205 + 0.9557 + 1.4970 + 0.7575 = 3.9307$

**Adjustment factor:** $4.00 / 3.9307 = 1.01764$

**Adjusted Seasonal Indices:**

- **SI(Q1) =** $0.7205 \times 1.01764 = \mathbf{0.7332}$
- **SI(Q2) =** $0.9557 \times 1.01764 = \mathbf{0.9726}$
- **SI(Q3) =** $1.4970 \times 1.01764 = \mathbf{1.5234}$
- **SI(Q4) =** $0.7575 \times 1.01764 = \mathbf{0.7708}$

**Check:** $0.7332 + 0.9726 + 1.5234 + 0.7708 = 4.000$ ✓

**Step 5: Forecast for Q1 of next year (2024):**

Overall average demand per quarter:

$$\bar{D} = \frac{80+120+200+100+100+140+220+120+120+160+260+140}{12} = \frac{1760}{12} = 146.67$$

Forecast for Q1 2024 = Overall Average × Seasonal Index (Q1):

$$F_{Q1,2024} = 146.67 \times 0.7332 \approx 107.53 \text{ lakhs}$$

(Alternative: Deseasonalize trend and project. For KTU level, the above ratio method is acceptable.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Demand" vs "Forecast"** in the $e_t$ formula — error is always $D_t - F_t$, not the reverse. Reversing the sign **inverts the Tracking Signal interpretation**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up MAD and MSE** — MSE squares the error and is **in squared units** (₹², kg²); MAD is in original units.
>
> 3. **Moving Average Sample Size:** Forecast at month $t+1$ using MA-3 uses months $t, t-1, t-2$. Many students **omit the earliest period**, leading to off-by-one errors.
>
> 4. **Seasonal Index Adjustment:** The unadjusted seasonal indices **must be normalized to sum to the number of periods** (4 for quarterly, 12 for monthly). Skipping this normalization step is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Exponential Smoothing Initialization:** $F_1$ must be explicitly **given or assumed** ($F_1 = D_1$ is a common valid assumption). Students often forget to handle the first period.
>
> 6. **Choosing the Wrong Method:** Forecasting seasonal data with simple ES (without seasonal adjustment) is a conceptual mistake. Examiners look for **pattern-matching** ability.
>
> 7. **Tracking Signal Limits:** Using the wrong control limit (e.g., ±3 instead of ±4) loses a mark. **Standard KTU answer: ±4.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Demand Forecasting**

- **Definition:** Forecasting = predicting future demand using historical data and judgment.
- **Two Grand Families:** Qualitative (subjective) vs Quantitative (statistical).
- **Qualitative Methods:** Executive Opinion, Sales Force Composite, **Delphi Method**, Market Research, Historical Analogy.
- **Delphi Method:** Anonymous, iterative, expert panel, 3 rounds, converges to consensus.
- **Quantitative Sub-families:** Time-Series (intrinsic) vs Causal / Associative (extrinsic).
- **Time-Series Methods:** Naïve, Simple Average, MA, WMA, Simple ES, Holt's, Holt-Winters.
- **Causal Methods:** Linear Regression, Multiple Regression, Econometric Models, Leading Indicators.
- **Four Demand Patterns:** Horizontal, Trend, Seasonality, Cyclical (+ Irregular).
- **Time-Series Decomposition:** Additive ($T+S+C+I$) vs Multiplicative ($T \times S \times C \times I$).
- **Key Error Metrics:**
  - MAD = $\frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$
  - MSE = $\frac{\sum e_t^2}{n}$
  - MAPE = $\frac{100}{n}\sum\frac{\vert e_t \vert}{D_t}$
  - TS = $\frac{CE}{MAD}$, control limit $\pm 4$.
- **Exponential Smoothing:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)F_t = F_t + \alpha e_t$, with $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$.
- **Seasonal Index Normalization:** Adjusted indices must sum to **4** (quarterly) or **12** (monthly).
- **High $\alpha$** → reactive forecast (good for trends/volatility).
- **Low $\alpha$** → smooth forecast (good for stable demand).
- **Forecasting Horizons:** Short (≤1 yr) → MA/ES; Medium (1–3 yr) → Holt/Winters; Long (>3 yr) → Regression/Delphi.

---

topics.title: Forecasting Models Including Moving Average (Simple and Weighted), Exponential Smoothing and Regression Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forecasting Models Including Moving Average (Simple and Weighted), Exponential Smoothing and Regression Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Forecasting?

> [!NOTE]
> **Forecasting** is the process of estimating future events or trends based on the analysis of **past and present data**, using systematic mathematical or judgmental models. In Industrial Engineering, it is the backbone of **production planning, capacity planning, inventory control, and workforce scheduling**.

Formally, forecasting is a **statistical learning procedure** in which historical demand (or any time-series variable) $D_t$ is used to generate a future estimate $\hat{F}_{t+1}$ such that the expected deviation from the true value is minimized.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Weather Memory" Approach

Imagine you are a **farmer in Kerala planning tomorrow's irrigation**:

- **Naïve approach**: Assume tomorrow will be exactly like today.
- **Simple Moving Average**: Look at the rainfall of the **last 7 days** and take their *average* — short-term storms wash out, long-term pattern emerges.
- **Weighted Moving Average**: The **most recent 2 days matter more** than the older 5 days — you trust fresh memory more.
- **Exponential Smoothing**: Continuously update your "mental model" of rainfall by giving **exponentially decreasing weight** to older days — yesterday matters most, day-before-yesterday a bit less, and so on.
- **Linear Regression (Trend)**: Fit a straight **trend line** through the data so that you can extrapolate even *years* ahead.

In every case, you are using a model of the past to peek into the future.

### 1.3 Why Forecasting Matters in Industrial Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of KTU-Exam Relevance:**
> 1. **Demand Forecasting** → Drives MRP, EOQ, and safety stock.
> 2. **Sales Forecasting** → Drives aggregate production planning.
> 3. **Technology / Capacity Forecasting** → Drives long-term capital investment.

### 1.4 Classification of Forecasting Models

Forecasting models fall broadly into two categories:

| Category | Sub-type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative** | Judgmental | Delphi method, Market survey, Sales-force composite |
| **Quantitative** | Time-series | Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing |
| **Quantitative** | Associative / Causal | Linear Regression, Multiple Regression |

This module focuses exclusively on the **Quantitative Time-Series Models** and **Linear Regression**.

### 1.5 Components of a Time Series

Any demand series $D_t$ can be decomposed as:

$$D_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$

Where:
- $T_t$ = **Trend** (long-term upward/downward movement)
- $S_t$ = **Seasonality** (repeating periodic pattern)
- $C_t$ = **Cyclical variation** (multi-year economic cycles)
- $I_t$ = **Irregular / Random noise**

> [!NOTE]
> The models studied in this module (MA, WMA, ES) handle **stationary series** (no trend). Linear regression is needed when **trend** is present.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The **n-period Simple Moving Average** forecast for the next period is the arithmetic mean of the last $n$ actual demands.

**Logic steps:**
1. Choose a window length $n$ (e.g., 3 months, 5 weeks).
2. Sum the last $n$ observed demands.
3. Divide by $n$.
4. That becomes the forecast for the next period.

**Advantages**: Smooths out random noise; simple to compute.
**Disadvantages**: Lags behind trends; equal weight to old and recent data; needs storage of $n$ past values.

### 2.2 Weighted Moving Average (WMA)

An improvement over SMA where **recent observations are given higher importance** than older ones.

**Logic steps:**
1. Assign weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ to the past $n$ periods such that $\sum w_i = 1$.
2. Most recent period gets the **largest weight** $w_1$.
3. Multiply each demand by its weight, sum, and that becomes the forecast.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb**: For a 3-period WMA, a common choice is $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$.

### 2.3 Exponential Smoothing (Simple / Single)

The most elegant and widely-used time-series forecasting method. It **does not require storing past data** — only the previous forecast and the previous actual demand.

**Logic steps:**
1. Initialize $F_1 = D_1$ (or use average of first few periods).
2. Choose smoothing constant $\alpha \in (0, 1)$.
3. Update forecast recursively.

**Three equivalent forms:**
- Form A: $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$
- Form B: $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (D_t - F_t)$
- Form C (Expanded): $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) D_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 D_{t-2} + \dots$

**Interpretation of α:**
- **α → 1**: Forecast reacts strongly to recent changes (responsive but noisy).
- **α → 0**: Forecast is very stable, slow to react.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The choice of α is critical.** KTU board questions frequently ask: *"Which α is best?"* The answer is the one that **minimizes MAD/MSE/MAPE** on validation data.

### 2.4 Linear Regression for Trend Forecasting

When the data exhibits a **clear upward or downward trend**, moving averages and exponential smoothing (without trend adjustment) will systematically lag. The remedy is **Linear Regression**.

We fit a straight line to the data:

$$\hat{y} = a + bx$$

Where:
- $x$ = Time period (independent variable)
- $\hat{y}$ = Forecasted demand
- $b$ = **Slope** (change in demand per unit time)
- $a$ = **Intercept** (baseline demand at $x = 0$)

**The least-squares estimates** minimize $\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorize these for the KTU University Exam.**

| # | Model | Formula | Notes / Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Moving Average | $\hat{F}_{t+1} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i}{n}$ | $n$ = window length |
| 2 | Weighted Moving Average | $\hat{F}_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| 3 | Exp. Smoothing (Recursive) | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 4 | Exp. Smoothing (Error form) | $F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha (D_t - F_t)$ | $(D_t - F_t)$ = forecast error |
| 5 | Slope (Regression) | $b = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | Least squares |
| 6 | Intercept (Regression) | $a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x}$ | Where $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$, $\bar{y} = \sum y_i / n$ |
| 7 | Forecast (Regression) | $\hat{y} = a + bx$ | $x$ = future time period |
| 8 | MAD | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{\sum \mid D_t - F_t \mid}{n}$ | Mean Absolute Deviation |
| 9 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{\sum (D_t - F_t)^2}{n}$ | Mean Squared Error |
| 10 | MAPE | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum \dfrac{\mid D_t - F_t \mid}{\mid D_t \mid} \times 100$ | Mean Absolute % Error |
| 11 | Bias (Tracking Signal input) | $\text{Bias} = \sum (D_t - F_t)$ | Sum of forecast errors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Engineering Use:** In **capacity planning**, a manufacturing firm uses exponential smoothing for short-term (1–3 month) demand. For **annual capacity expansion**, linear regression on the last 5–10 years is preferred. **MAPE** is the most industry-friendly metric because it is dimensionless (a percentage).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Least-Squares Regression Coefficients

We wish to find $a$ and $b$ that minimize the sum of squared errors (SSE):

$$\text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - a - bx_i)^2$$

**Step 1** — Take the partial derivative of SSE with respect to $a$ and set it to zero.

$$\frac{\partial (\text{SSE})}{\partial a} = -2 \sum (y_i - a - bx_i) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \sum y_i = na + b \sum x_i \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

**Step 2** — Take the partial derivative of SSE with respect to $b$ and set it to zero.

$$\frac{\partial (\text{SSE})}{\partial b} = -2 \sum x_i (y_i - a - bx_i) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \sum x_i y_i = a \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2 \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

**Step 3** — Solving Eq. A for $a$:

$$a = \frac{\sum y_i - b \sum x_i}{n} = \bar{y} - b \bar{x}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute into Eq. B:

$$\sum x_i y_i = (\bar{y} - b\bar{x}) \sum x_i + b \sum x_i^2$$

Expanding:

$$\sum x_i y_i = \bar{y} \sum x_i - b\bar{x}\sum x_i + b\sum x_i^2$$

Since $\bar{x}\sum x_i = n\bar{x}^2$ and $\bar{y}\sum x_i = n\bar{x}\bar{y}$:

$$\sum x_i y_i = n\bar{x}\bar{y} - bn\bar{x}^2 + b\sum x_i^2$$

$$b \left( \sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2 \right) = \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}$$

$$b = \frac{\sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}}{\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2}$$

**Step 5** — Multiply numerator and denominator by $n$ to get the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}\,}$$

and

$$\boxed{\,a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = \frac{\sum y_i - b\sum x_i}{n}\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Simple Moving Average (3-period)

**Problem**: Past 6 months' demand (in hundreds): 17, 21, 19, 23, 18, 22. Compute a **3-period SMA** forecast for month 7 and the **MAD**.

**Solution:**

$$\hat{F}_4 = \frac{17 + 21 + 19}{3} = 19.00$$

$$\hat{F}_5 = \frac{21 + 19 + 23}{3} = 21.00$$

$$\hat{F}_6 = \frac{19 + 23 + 18}{3} = 20.00$$

$$\hat{F}_7 = \frac{23 + 18 + 22}{3} = \frac{63}{3} = \mathbf{21.00}$$

**MAD computation:**

| Month | $D_t$ | $\hat{F}_t$ | $\vert D_t - \hat{F}_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 23 | 19.00 | 4.00 |
| 5 | 18 | 21.00 | 3.00 |
| 6 | 22 | 20.00 | 2.00 |
| **Sum** | | | **9.00** |

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{9}{3} = \mathbf{3.00}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Weighted Moving Average

**Problem**: Same data; use weights $w_1 = 0.5$ (most recent), $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$. Forecast month 7.

**Solution:**

$$\hat{F}_7 = 0.5 \times 22 + 0.3 \times 18 + 0.2 \times 23$$

$$\hat{F}_7 = 11.00 + 5.40 + 4.60 = \mathbf{21.00}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Exponential Smoothing (α = 0.3)

**Problem**: Same demand series. Initialize $F_1 = D_1 = 17$. Compute forecasts and MAD.

**Recursive Update Formula:**

$$F_{t+1} = 0.3 \cdot D_t + 0.7 \cdot F_t$$

| $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ (formula) | $F_t$ (value) | $\vert D_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 17 | $F_1 = D_1$ | 17.00 | — |
| 2 | 21 | $0.3(17) + 0.7(17)$ | 17.00 | 4.00 |
| 3 | 19 | $0.3(21) + 0.7(17)$ | 18.20 | 0.80 |
| 4 | 23 | $0.3(19) + 0.7(18.20)$ | 18.44 | 4.56 |
| 5 | 18 | $0.3(23) + 0.7(18.44)$ | 19.81 | 1.81 |
| 6 | 22 | $0.3(18) + 0.7(19.81)$ | 19.47 | 2.53 |

$$\hat{F}_7 = 0.3(22) + 0.7(19.47) = 6.60 + 13.63 = \mathbf{20.23}$$

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{4.00 + 0.80 + 4.56 + 1.81 + 2.53}{5} = \frac{13.70}{5} = \mathbf{2.74}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Linear Regression for Trend

**Problem**: 5 years of demand data:

| Year ($x$) | Demand ($y$) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 55 |
| 3 | 58 |
| 4 | 60 |
| 5 | 65 |

Fit a linear trend $\hat{y} = a + bx$ and forecast Year 7.

**Step 1 — Build the auxiliary table:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $x_i y_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 1 | 50 |
| 2 | 55 | 4 | 110 |
| 3 | 58 | 9 | 174 |
| 4 | 60 | 16 | 240 |
| 5 | 65 | 25 | 325 |
| **Σ** = **15** | **Σ** = **288** | **Σ** = **55** | **Σ** = **899** |

So $n = 5$.

**Step 2 — Compute slope $b$:**

$$b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{5(899) - (15)(288)}{5(55) - (15)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{4495 - 4320}{275 - 225} = \frac{175}{50} = \mathbf{3.5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept $a$:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{15}{5} = 3, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{288}{5} = 57.6$$

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 57.6 - (3.5)(3) = 57.6 - 10.5 = \mathbf{47.1}$$

**Step 4 — Trend equation:**

$$\hat{y} = 47.1 + 3.5x$$

**Step 5 — Forecast for Year 7:**

$$\hat{y}_7 = 47.1 + 3.5 \times 7 = 47.1 + 24.5 = \mathbf{71.6}$$

### 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Comparing Forecast Accuracy

Using the regression line $\hat{y} = 47.1 + 3.5x$, compute forecast errors for each year:

| $x$ | $D_t$ | $\hat{F}_t = 47.1 + 3.5x$ | $(D_t - \hat{F}_t)$ | $(D_t - \hat{F}_t)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 50.6 | -0.6 | 0.36 |
| 2 | 55 | 54.1 | 0.9 | 0.81 |
| 3 | 58 | 57.6 | 0.4 | 0.16 |
| 4 | 60 | 61.1 | -1.1 | 1.21 |
| 5 | 65 | 64.6 | 0.4 | 0.16 |

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{0.6 + 0.9 + 0.4 + 1.1 + 0.4}{5} = \frac{3.4}{5} = \mathbf{0.68}$$

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{0.36 + 0.81 + 0.16 + 1.21 + 0.16}{5} = \frac{2.70}{5} = \mathbf{0.54}$$

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{5}\left(\frac{0.6}{50} + \frac{0.9}{55} + \frac{0.4}{58} + \frac{1.1}{60} + \frac{0.4}{65}\right) \times 100$$

$$= \frac{1}{5}(0.012 + 0.01636 + 0.00690 + 0.01833 + 0.00615) \times 100$$

$$= \frac{1}{5}(0.05974) \times 100 = \mathbf{1.195\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation**: A **MAPE of 1.195%** indicates an excellent forecasting model — typical industry thresholds are: < 10% = highly accurate, 10–20% = good, 20–50% = reasonable, > 50% = inaccurate.

### 3.7 Selecting the Best α — Optimization Procedure

Given a series, the optimal $\alpha$ is found by:

1. Try $\alpha \in \{0.1, 0.2, 0.3, \dots, 0.9\}$.
2. For each $\alpha$, compute MAD (or MSE) across all periods.
3. Plot MAD vs α.
4. Pick the α that **minimizes** MAD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often present a pre-computed table of MAD for different α values and ask the student to **identify the best α** — this is a guaranteed 3-mark question almost every semester.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forecasting Model Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Have Demand Data] --> nodeB{Is there a Trend?}
    nodeB -- No Trend --> nodeC{Is data noisy?}
    nodeB -- Yes Trend --> nodeD[Use Linear Regression]
    nodeC -- Yes Noisy --> nodeE{Is recent data more important?}
    nodeC -- Low Noise --> nodeF[Use Simple Moving Average]
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG[Use Weighted Moving Average]
    nodeE -- Continuous Update --> nodeH[Use Exponential Smoothing]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Compute slope b and intercept a]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Forecast y = a + bx]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Choose alpha to minimize MAD]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Choose window n]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Assign weights summing to 1]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Evaluate MAD or MAPE]
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeK --> nodeN
    nodeJ --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Select Best Model]
```

### 4.2 Exponential Smoothing — Recursive Update Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Previous Forecast Ft] --> nodeC[Weighted Combination]
    nodeB[Current Demand Dt] --> nodeC
    nodeC -- alpha weight to Dt --> nodeD[New Forecast F t+1]
    nodeC -- 1 minus alpha weight to Ft --> nodeD
    nodeD -- feeds into next period --> nodeA
```

**Caption**: Exponential smoothing is a **feedback loop** — every new forecast depends on the previous forecast and the most recent actual demand, scaled by α.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Linear Regression Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Demand Data y1 y2 ... yn] --> nodeB[Pair with Time Index x1 x2 ... xn]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Summations]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Calculate Slope b]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Calculate Intercept a]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Build Equation y hat = a + bx]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Plug future x for Forecast]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Output Forecast Value]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture — When to Use What

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Forecasting Need] --> nodeB{Data Volume}
    nodeB -- Small Less than 10 points --> nodeC[Linear Regression]
    nodeB -- Medium 10 to 50 --> nodeD{Stationary?}
    nodeB -- Large More than 50 --> nodeE[Advanced Models]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF[SMA or Exponential Smoothing]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeG[Trend Adjusted ES or Regression]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Minimize MAD or MAPE]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define forecasting. Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative forecasting methods.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Forecasting is the process of estimating future demand or trends based on historical data using systematic mathematical or judgmental methods.
>
> | Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative |
> |---|---|---|
> | Basis | Judgment, opinion, intuition | Numerical historical data |
> | Data type | Subjective | Objective, measurable |
> | Examples | Delphi, Market survey | Moving Average, Regression |
> | Use case | New products, no history | Mature products, available data |

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison table)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the significance of the smoothing constant α in exponential smoothing. What happens when α = 0 and α = 1?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The smoothing constant **α ∈ (0, 1)** controls how quickly the forecast reacts to recent demand changes.

- When **α → 0**: The forecast is **very stable**; almost no weight is given to recent demand. $F_{t+1} \approx F_t$.
- When **α → 1**: The forecast **reacts fully** to the most recent demand. $F_{t+1} \approx D_t$ (essentially naïve forecasting).
- The optimal α is chosen by minimizing MAD or MSE on historical data.

*(1 mark for α role + 1 mark each for α=0 and α=1 cases)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** The monthly demand for a product over the last 8 months is:

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 200 | 220 | 210 | 240 | 230 | 250 | 245 | 260 |

Compute the **3-month Simple Moving Average** forecast for month 9 and the **MAD**.

**(b)** For the same data, compute the **Weighted Moving Average** forecast for month 9 using weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (most recent to oldest). Which model — SMA or WMA — gives a **lower MAD**?

---

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Simple Moving Average:**

$$\hat{F}_4 = \frac{200 + 220 + 210}{3} = \frac{630}{3} = 210$$

$$\hat{F}_5 = \frac{220 + 210 + 240}{3} = \frac{670}{3} \approx 223.33$$

$$\hat{F}_6 = \frac{210 + 240 + 230}{3} = \frac{680}{3} \approx 226.67$$

$$\hat{F}_7 = \frac{240 + 230 + 250}{3} = \frac{720}{3} = 240$$

$$\hat{F}_8 = \frac{230 + 250 + 245}{3} = \frac{725}{3} \approx 241.67$$

$$\boxed{\hat{F}_9 = \frac{250 + 245 + 260}{3} = \frac{755}{3} \approx 251.67}$$

**MAD computation for SMA:**

| Month | $D_t$ | $\hat{F}_t$ | $\vert D_t - \hat{F}_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 240 | 210.00 | 30.00 |
| 5 | 230 | 223.33 | 6.67 |
| 6 | 250 | 226.67 | 23.33 |
| 7 | 245 | 240.00 | 5.00 |
| 8 | 260 | 241.67 | 18.33 |
| **Σ** | | | **83.33** |

$$\text{MAD}_{\text{SMA}} = \frac{83.33}{5} = \mathbf{16.67}$$

**(7 marks: 4 for forecast computation, 3 for MAD)**

---

**Part (b) — Weighted Moving Average:**

$$\hat{F}_9 = 0.5 \times 260 + 0.3 \times 245 + 0.2 \times 250$$

$$\hat{F}_9 = 130 + 73.5 + 50 = \mathbf{253.50}$$

**Computing WMA for months 4–8 to find MAD:**

$$\hat{F}_4 = 0.5(210) + 0.3(220) + 0.2(200) = 105 + 66 + 40 = 211$$

$$\hat{F}_5 = 0.5(240) + 0.3(210) + 0.2(220) = 120 + 63 + 44 = 227$$

$$\hat{F}_6 = 0.5(230) + 0.3(240) + 0.2(210) = 115 + 72 + 42 = 229$$

$$\hat{F}_7 = 0.5(250) + 0.3(230) + 0.2(240) = 125 + 69 + 48 = 242$$

$$\hat{F}_8 = 0.5(245) + 0.3(250) + 0.2(230) = 122.5 + 75 + 46 = 243.5$$

**MAD computation for WMA:**

| Month | $D_t$ | $\hat{F}_t$ | $\vert D_t - \hat{F}_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 240 | 211 | 29.0 |
| 5 | 230 | 227 | 3.0 |
| 6 | 250 | 229 | 21.0 |
| 7 | 245 | 242 | 3.0 |
| 8 | 260 | 243.5 | 16.5 |
| **Σ** | | | **72.5** |

$$\text{MAD}_{\text{WMA}} = \frac{72.5}{5} = \mathbf{14.50}$$

**Comparison:**

Since $\text{MAD}_{\text{WMA}} = 14.50 < \text{MAD}_{\text{SMA}} = 16.67$, the **Weighted Moving Average** is more accurate for this dataset.

**(7 marks: 4 for WMA forecasts, 3 for MAD + comparison)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Part B Long Answer:**
> - **Forgetting to initialize**: In exponential smoothing, students often write $F_1 = 0$ instead of $F_1 = D_1$ — *lose 1 mark*.
> - **MAD period mismatch**: MAD should be computed over $n - k$ periods, where $k$ is the smallest period for which a forecast exists. Counting all $n$ periods is a common error — *lose 1 mark*.
> - **Weight validation**: In WMA, students frequently write weights that do **not sum to 1**. Always verify $\sum w_i = 1$ before computing — *lose 1 mark*.
> - **Trend in stationary models**: If the data shows a clear trend, applying only SMA/ES will give biased forecasts. Recognize and mention this in your answer — *lose 1 mark*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **exponential smoothing** method of forecasting. Use $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = D_1$ to forecast demand for periods 2–6 given: $D_1 = 100, D_2 = 110, D_3 = 105, D_4 = 115, D_5 = 112$. Compute the **MAD**.

**(b)** A 6-year sales record of a product is given below. Fit a **linear trend equation** using the **least-squares method** and forecast sales for Year 7.

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales | 120 | 135 | 142 | 156 | 168 | 180 |

---

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Exponential Smoothing:**

The exponential smoothing formula is:

$$F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$

With $\alpha = 0.4$, $F_1 = D_1 = 100$.

**Iterative computation:**

$$F_2 = 0.4(100) + 0.6(100) = 40 + 60 = 100.00$$

$$F_3 = 0.4(110) + 0.6(100) = 44 + 60 = 104.00$$

$$F_4 = 0.4(105) + 0.6(104) = 42 + 62.4 = 104.40$$

$$F_5 = 0.4(115) + 0.6(104.4) = 46 + 62.64 = 108.64$$

$$F_6 = 0.4(112) + 0.6(108.64) = 44.8 + 65.184 = 109.98$$

**MAD computation:**

| Period | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert D_t - F_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 110 | 100.00 | 10.00 |
| 3 | 105 | 104.00 | 1.00 |
| 4 | 115 | 104.40 | 10.60 |
| 5 | 112 | 108.64 | 3.36 |
| **Σ** | | | **24.96** |

$$\text{MAD} = \frac{24.96}{4} = \mathbf{6.24}$$

Forecast for Period 7: $F_7 = 0.4(112) + 0.6(109.98) = 44.8 + 65.99 = \mathbf{110.79}$

**(7 marks: 4 for forecasts, 3 for MAD)**

---

**Part (b) — Linear Regression:**

**Auxiliary Table:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $x_i y_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 120 | 1 | 120 |
| 2 | 135 | 4 | 270 |
| 3 | 142 | 9 | 426 |
| 4 | 156 | 16 | 624 |
| 5 | 168 | 25 | 840 |
| 6 | 180 | 36 | 1080 |
| **Σ** = **21** | **Σ** = **901** | **Σ** = **91** | **Σ** = **3360** |

$n = 6$

**Compute slope $b$:**

$$b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{6(3360) - (21)(901)}{6(91) - (21)^2} = \frac{20160 - 18921}{546 - 441} = \frac{1239}{105}$$

$$\boxed{b = 11.80}$$

**Compute intercept $a$:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{901}{6} \approx 150.17$$

$$a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x} = 150.17 - (11.80)(3.5) = 150.17 - 41.30 = \mathbf{108.87}$$

**Trend Equation:**

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 108.87 + 11.80x}$$

**Forecast for Year 7:**

$$\hat{y}_7 = 108.87 + 11.80(7) = 108.87 + 82.60 = \mathbf{191.47}$$

**(7 marks: 3 for slope, 2 for intercept, 2 for forecast)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Forecasting**: Estimating future values from past data; qualitative (judgmental) vs quantitative (statistical).
- ☐ **Simple Moving Average (SMA)**: $\hat{F}_{t+1} = \frac{\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i}{n}$; lag-prone but smooths noise.
- ☐ **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)**: $\hat{F}_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_{t-i+1}$; weights **must sum to 1**; recent data weighted more.
- ☐ **Exponential Smoothing**: $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$; only **two values** to remember ($F_t$ and $D_t$).
- ☐ **Smoothing Constant α**: Higher α → more responsive; lower α → more stable. **Optimal α minimizes MAD**.
- ☐ **Linear Regression Equation**: $\hat{y} = a + bx$; **a = intercept, b = slope**.
- ☐ **Slope Formula**: $b = \frac{n\sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n\sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ — verify calculation of $\sum x$, $\sum y$, $\sum xy$, $\sum x^2$ first.
- ☐ **Intercept**: $a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$ — easier than computing via the alternate formula.
- ☐ **MAD**: $\frac{\sum \vert D_t - F_t \vert}{n}$ — most commonly used accuracy metric.
- ☐ **MSE**: $\frac{\sum (D_t - F_t)^2}{n}$ — penalizes large errors more.
- ☐ **MAPE**: percentage form, scale-independent — preferred for cross-product comparisons.
- ☐ **When to use which model**:
  - **No trend, noisy** → SMA or Exponential Smoothing.
  - **No trend, recent data more important** → WMA or Exponential Smoothing.
  - **Trend present** → Linear Regression.
- ☐ **Initialization**: For exponential smoothing, $F_1 = D_1$ or use the average of the first few periods.
- ☐ **Validation**: Always evaluate forecast accuracy on **out-of-sample** data, not the same data used to build the model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition: Forecast Accuracy Measures**
> **Forecast Accuracy Measures** are quantitative statistical metrics used to evaluate the deviation between the *actual demand (or output)* observed in a real process and the *forecasted demand* generated by a chosen forecasting model. They form the **backbone of forecast monitoring and control** within the production planning subsystem of Industrial Engineering and Production Management. The universally accepted measures in the KTU 2024 scheme include the **Forecast Error ($e_t$)**, **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**, **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**, **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)**, **Mean Error (ME) / Bias**, **Cumulative Sum of Forecast Errors (CSE / RSFE)**, and the **Tracking Signal (TS)**.

The fundamental forecast error at any period $t$ is mathematically defined as:

$$e_t = A_t - F_t$$

Where:
- $A_t$ = **Actual demand** in period $t$
- $F_t$ = **Forecasted demand** in period $t$ (as generated by the model in period $t-1$)
- $e_t$ = **Forecast error** in period $t$ (positive indicates under-forecasting, negative indicates over-forecasting)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The "Weather Forecaster" Analogy**
> Imagine you are the **Indian Meteorological Department (IMD)** issuing daily rainfall forecasts for Kerala. You predicted **20 mm** of rain for Tuesday, but the actual gauge at the station recorded **35 mm**. You "missed" by 15 mm. Now suppose you predicted **20 mm** for Wednesday, and it rained exactly **5 mm**. You "overshot" by 15 mm.
>
> If you only collected these two errors, you might naively average them: $(+15 + (-15))/2 = 0$ mm — which falsely suggests your forecast was **perfect**! This is precisely why simple averaging fails: positive and negative errors *cancel out*.
>
> To truly measure how *off* you were (regardless of direction), you need to take the **absolute magnitude** of each error — giving you **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)**. If you want to **punish large misses more severely** (because predicting 5 mm when 500 mm actually falls is catastrophic for farmers), you square each error and average them — yielding **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**.
>
> **The industrial engineer’s job is exactly the IMD’s job**: continuously measure how *far* their demand forecasts (used to plan raw materials, manpower, machine capacity) deviate from reality, so that the forecasting model can be re-tuned or replaced before it ruins the production schedule.

### 1.3 Classification of Forecast Error Measures

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight: Two Broad Families**
> KTU 2024 Module 4 groups all forecast accuracy measures into two families:
>
> 1. **Scale-Dependent Measures** — expressed in the *same units* as the data (e.g., units produced, kWh, ₹). Best for comparing forecasts of the *same* product across different models. Examples: **MAD, MSE, RMSE, ME, RSFE**.
>
> 2. **Scale-Independent (Relative) Measures** — expressed as *percentages* or *ratios*, allowing comparison across products with different units, scales, or magnitudes. Examples: **MAPE**.
