## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of Each Measure

#### 2.1.1 Mean Error (ME) — The *Bias* Indicator

The **Mean Error** captures the **systematic tendency** of a forecasting model to consistently over-predict or under-predict. It is the simple arithmetic average of forecast errors.

$$ME = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t}{n} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(A_t - F_t)}{n}$$

- **Why it matters:** A positive ME means the model is *systematically under-forecasting* (actual > forecast). A negative ME means systematic *over-forecasting*.
- **Limitation:** Because errors of opposite signs cancel out, ME can be **zero even for a terrible forecasting model**. Hence, ME alone is *never* used as a stand-alone accuracy measure; it is purely a **bias indicator**.

#### 2.1.2 Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) — The *Workhorse* Measure

**MAD** is the most widely used measure of forecast accuracy in industry because it is robust, intuitive, and directly interpretable.

$$MAD = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert A_t - F_t \vert}{n}$$

- **Why it matters:** By taking absolute values, MAD **eliminates sign cancellation**, giving the *average magnitude* of deviation per period. It is expressed in the *same units* as the forecast.
- **Use in Industry:** MAD is the denominator of the **Tracking Signal (TS)** used in **adaptive forecasting control**.

#### 2.1.3 Mean Squared Error (MSE) — The *Variance* Measure

**MSE** squares each error before averaging. This **amplifies large errors**, making MSE highly sensitive to outliers.

$$MSE = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^{2}}{n} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(A_t - F_t)^{2}}{n}$$

- **Why it matters:** MSE is the **expected value of the squared error** and is the foundation of classical statistical methods like **least-squares regression** and **ARIMA models**.
- **Limitation:** Squaring produces **squared units** (e.g., if demand is in units, MSE is in *units²*), which makes it less intuitive to managers.

#### 2.1.4 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) — The *Intuitive Variance* Measure

**RMSE** is the square root of MSE, restoring the original units of measurement.

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(A_t - F_t)^{2}}{n}}$$

- **Why it matters:** RMSE combines the **mathematical tractability** of MSE (penalizing large errors) with the **interpretability** of MAD (same units as the data).
- **Rule of Thumb:** RMSE $\geq$ MAD always (equality holds only if *all* errors are equal in magnitude).

#### 2.1.5 Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) — The *Relative* Measure

**MAPE** expresses each error as a *percentage* of actual demand, then averages. It is dimensionless and scale-free.

$$MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \right\vert = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \quad (\%) $$

- **Why it matters:** A MAPE of **8%** means the forecast is, on average, off by 8% of actual demand — interpretable across any product, any industry, any currency.
- **Limitation:** MAPE is **undefined when $A_t = 0$** (division by zero). It also **asymmetrically penalizes** under-forecasting more than over-forecasting when actuals are low.

#### 2.1.6 Cumulative Sum of Forecast Errors (CSE / RSFE) — The *Running Bias*

The **Cumulative Sum of Errors** (also called *Running Sum of Forecast Errors*, **RSFE**) is a running total of forecast errors used to detect **model drift**.

$$CSE_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} e_i = \sum_{i=1}^{t}(A_i - F_i)$$

- **Why it matters:** If CSE grows consistently positive, the model is **persistently under-forecasting**; if consistently negative, persistently over-forecasting. Both indicate the need for **model re-tuning**.

#### 2.1.7 Tracking Signal (TS) — The *Adaptive Control* Measure

The **Tracking Signal** is the ratio of the running cumulative error to the average absolute error. It is the principal metric for **forecast monitoring and adaptive control**.

$$TS_t = \frac{CSE_t}{MAD_t} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{t} e_i}{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert e_i \vert}$$

- **Interpretation Thresholds (KTU Standard):**
  - **$\vert TS \vert \leq 4$** → Model is **in control**. Acceptable.
  - **$\vert TS \vert > 4$** → Model is **out of control**. **Triggers corrective action** (re-tuning, model change, or special-cause investigation).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (For KTU ESE)**

| **Measure** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Primary Purpose** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Forecast Error | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | Same as demand | Per-period deviation |
| Mean Error (ME) | $\dfrac{\sum e_t}{n}$ | Same as demand | Bias detection |
| Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) | $\dfrac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$ | Same as demand | Average magnitude of error |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\dfrac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n}$ | Squared units | Penalizes large errors |
| Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n}}$ | Same as demand | Intuitive variance measure |
| Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) | $\dfrac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \dfrac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert$ | Percentage (%) | Scale-free comparison |
| Cumulative Sum of Errors (CSE/RSFE) | $\sum e_t$ | Same as demand | Running bias detection |
| Tracking Signal (TS) | $\dfrac{CSE}{MAD}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) | Forecast control: $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] **Where These Measures Are Used in Practice**
> - **Production Planning:** Selecting the best forecasting model (Naïve, Exponential Smoothing, ARIMA, Holt-Winters) by comparing their MAD/MAPE values.
> - **Inventory Control:** MAD is used directly to compute **safety stock** ($SS = z \cdot MAD \cdot \sqrt{L}$) in classical inventory models.
> - **Supply Chain Management:** MAPE allows comparison of forecast accuracy *across* product lines, warehouses, and SKUs in dashboards.
> - **Adaptive Forecasting:** Tracking Signal triggers automatic re-tuning of exponential smoothing parameters ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$) when demand patterns shift.
> - **Quality Engineering:** MSE/RMSE form the basis of **Mean Square Error loss functions** in Six Sigma and Design of Experiments (DOE).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — A Complete Exhaustive Solution

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exhaustive Content Mandate**
> Below is a *fully worked, hand-calculated* numerical problem integrating **all** major forecast accuracy measures. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. This is the **exact style of problem** that appears in KTU ESE Module 4 questions.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A factory in Ernakulam manufactures a fast-moving consumer good (FMCG). The **actual monthly demand (in units × 1000)** and the **forecasted demand** generated by an exponential smoothing model (with $\alpha = 0.3$) for the **first 10 months** of 2024 are tabulated below:

| Month ($t$) | Actual Demand $A_t$ (000s) | Forecast $F_t$ (000s) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 100 | 90 |
| 2 | 95 | 93 |
| 3 | 110 | 96 |
| 4 | 85 | 100 |
| 5 | 120 | 96 |
| 6 | 105 | 103 |
| 7 | 130 | 104 |
| 8 | 100 | 112 |
| 9 | 125 | 109 |
| 10 | 115 | 114 |

**Required:** Calculate (a) $e_t$ for each period, (b) **MAD**, (c) **MSE**, (d) **RMSE**, (e) **MAPE**, (f) **ME**, (g) **CSE** at the end of month 10, and (h) **Tracking Signal** at the end of month 10. Conclude whether the forecasting model is *in control*.

---

#### 3.1.2 Step (a): Per-Period Forecast Error $e_t$

We use $e_t = A_t - F_t$ for each month.

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= A_1 - F_1 = 100 - 90 = +10 \\
e_2 &= A_2 - F_2 = 95 - 93 = +2 \\
e_3 &= A_3 - F_3 = 110 - 96 = +14 \\
e_4 &= A_4 - F_4 = 85 - 100 = -15 \\
e_5 &= A_5 - F_5 = 120 - 96 = +24 \\
e_6 &= A_6 - F_6 = 105 - 103 = +2 \\
e_7 &= A_7 - F_7 = 130 - 104 = +26 \\
e_8 &= A_8 - F_8 = 100 - 112 = -12 \\
e_9 &= A_9 - F_9 = 125 - 109 = +16 \\
e_{10} &= A_{10} - F_{10} = 115 - 114 = +1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Master Error Computation Table** (all intermediate columns):

| $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^{2}$ | $\vert e_t / A_t \vert \times 100$ | $\sum \vert e_i \vert$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 100 | 90 | +10 | 10 | 100 | 10.00 | 10 |
| 2 | 95 | 93 | +2 | 2 | 4 | 2.105 | 12 |
| 3 | 110 | 96 | +14 | 14 | 196 | 12.727 | 26 |
| 4 | 85 | 100 | -15 | 15 | 225 | 17.647 | 41 |
| 5 | 120 | 96 | +24 | 24 | 576 | 20.000 | 65 |
| 6 | 105 | 103 | +2 | 2 | 4 | 1.905 | 67 |
| 7 | 130 | 104 | +26 | 26 | 676 | 20.000 | 93 |
| 8 | 100 | 112 | -12 | 12 | 144 | 12.000 | 105 |
| 9 | 125 | 109 | +16 | 16 | 256 | 12.800 | 121 |
| 10 | 115 | 114 | +1 | 1 | 1 | 0.870 | 122 |
| **$\Sigma$** | — | — | **+68** | **122** | **2180** | **110.054** | — |

---

#### 3.1.3 Step (b): Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)

Using the formula $MAD = \dfrac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$, with $n = 10$ and $\sum \vert e_t \vert = 122$:

$$MAD = \frac{122}{10} = 12.20 \;\; \text{(000 units)}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the forecast deviates from the actual demand by **12,200 units** per month.

---

#### 3.1.4 Step (c): Mean Squared Error (MSE)

Using $MSE = \dfrac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n}$, with $\sum e_t^{2} = 2180$:

$$MSE = \frac{2180}{10} = 218.00 \;\; \text{(000 units)}^2$$

**Interpretation:** The squared-error magnitude per period is 218,000² = **2.18 × 10⁸ units²**. The high MSE relative to MAD² (148.84) reflects the disproportionate influence of the large errors $e_5 = +24$ and $e_7 = +26$.

---

#### 3.1.5 Step (d): Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)

$$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{218.00}$$

$$RMSE = 14.76 \;\; \text{(000 units)}$$

**Sanity Check:** $RMSE = 14.76 > MAD = 12.20$ ✓ (consistent with theory; RMSE penalizes the large errors more heavily).

---

#### 3.1.6 Step (e): Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)

Using $MAPE = \dfrac{100}{n}\sum \left\vert \dfrac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert$, with the sum of percentage errors equal to **110.054**:

$$MAPE = \frac{110.054}{10} = 11.01\%$$

**Interpretation:** The forecast is, on average, off by **11.01%** of actual demand — a moderately accurate model.

---

#### 3.1.7 Step (f): Mean Error (ME) — Bias Indicator

Using $ME = \dfrac{\sum e_t}{n}$, with $\sum e_t = +68$:

$$ME = \frac{+68}{10} = +6.80 \;\; \text{(000 units)}$$

**Interpretation:** A *positive* ME of **6,800 units** indicates a **systematic under-forecasting bias** — the model on average predicts *less* than the actual demand. This is a *bias signal*, not an accuracy signal.

---

#### 3.1.8 Step (g): Cumulative Sum of Forecast Errors (CSE / RSFE) at Month 10

$$CSE_{10} = \sum_{t=1}^{10} e_t = +68 \;\; \text{(000 units)}$$

Equivalently, this equals $ME \times n = 6.80 \times 10 = 68$ ✓.

**Interpretation:** The running bias has accumulated to **+68,000 units** — strongly positive, confirming consistent under-forecasting over the 10-month horizon.

---

#### 3.1.9 Step (h): Tracking Signal at Month 10

Using $TS = \dfrac{CSE}{MAD}$:

$$TS_{10} = \frac{+68}{12.20} = +5.57$$

**Conclusion — Forecast Control Check:**

> [!WARNING] **Model is OUT OF CONTROL**
> Since $\vert TS \vert = 5.57 > 4$, the model has crossed the KTU standard control limit. The forecast is exhibiting **persistent under-forecasting bias** that requires **immediate corrective action**:
> 1. Increase the smoothing constant $\alpha$ (from 0.3 toward 0.5–0.7) to make the model more **responsive** to recent upswings.
> 2. Investigate special causes — possibly a new product variant, a competitor exit, or a seasonal trend not captured by simple exponential smoothing.
> 3. Consider upgrading to **Holt’s linear trend model** or **Holt-Winters seasonal model** to capture the underlying demand pattern.

---

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Measure

| **Scenario** | **Best Measure** | **Justification** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Comparing two forecasting models for the **same product** | MAD or RMSE | Same units, direct comparison |
| Comparing forecast accuracy **across different products** | MAPE | Scale-free (% basis) |
| Detecting whether the model **systematically over- or under-predicts** | ME (Bias) | Preserves sign of errors |
| Detecting **model drift** and triggering control action | Tracking Signal (TS) | Standard control limit $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$ |
| Penalizing **large forecast misses** more heavily (safety-critical applications, e.g., medicines, aircraft spares) | MSE / RMSE | Squaring amplifies outliers |
| Reporting forecast accuracy to **top management** in simple terms | MAPE + MAD | MAPE is intuitive; MAD gives absolute magnitude |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Forecast Accuracy Monitoring Loop

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **closed-loop control architecture** of forecast accuracy monitoring — the conceptual backbone of adaptive forecasting as required by KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Demand Data Acquisition: Actual At Observed] --> B[Apply Forecasting Model: Generate Ft]
    B --> C[Compute Per-Period Error: et = At minus Ft]
    C --> D[Compute Absolute Error: vert et vert]
    C --> E[Compute Squared Error: et squared]
    C --> F[Compute Percentage Error: vert et over At vert times 100]
    D --> G[Aggregate: MAD = Sum vert et vert divided by n]
    E --> H[Aggregate: MSE = Sum et squared divided by n]
    F --> I[Aggregate: MAPE = 100 over n times Sum of percentage errors]
    H --> J[Compute RMSE = Square Root of MSE]
    G --> K[Compute CSE = Sum et]
    K --> L[Compute Tracking Signal TS = CSE divided by MAD]
    L --> M{Decision: Is absolute TS less than or equal to 4}
    M -- YES --> N[Model In Control: Continue with Existing Forecast]
    M -- NO --> O[Model Out of Control: Trigger Corrective Action]
    O --> P[Re-tune Smoothing Constants alpha beta gamma]
    O --> Q[Investigate Special Causes]
    O --> R[Switch to Advanced Model: Holt Winters or ARIMA]
    P --> B
    Q --> B
    R --> B
```

**Reading Guide:**
- Boxes labeled `A` through `F` represent the **data ingestion and per-period error calculation** stage.
- Boxes `G`, `H`, `I`, `J`, `K`, `L` represent the **aggregation and statistical summarization** stage — yielding MAD, MSE, RMSE, MAPE, CSE, and Tracking Signal.
- The **diamond node `M`** is the **decision gateway** corresponding to the KTU control limit $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$.
- The downward branch (`O`, `P`, `Q`, `R`) represents the **corrective feedback loop** — the defining characteristic of an *adaptive* forecasting system.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hierarchical Relationship of Measures

This diagram clarifies the *mathematical hierarchy* — which measure feeds into which — an essential conceptual map for KTU ESE answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Forecast Error: et = At minus Ft] --> Q1[Absolute Error: vert et vert]
    P1 --> Q2[Squared Error: et squared]
    P1 --> Q3[Percentage Error: vert et over At vert]
    P1 --> Q4[Cumulative Error: CSE]
    Q1 --> R1[MAD: Sum vert et vert divided by n]
    Q2 --> R2[MSE: Sum et squared divided by n]
    Q2 --> R3[RMSE: Square Root of MSE]
    Q3 --> R4[MAPE: 100 over n times Sum vert et over At vert]
    Q4 --> R5[Tracking Signal: CSE divided by MAD]
    R1 --> R5
```

**Engineering Reading:** Each *raw* per-period error metric (`et`, `vert et vert`, `et squared`, `vert et/At vert`, `CSE`) is an **input**, while the **aggregated measures** (MAD, MSE, RMSE, MAPE, TS) are the **outputs** presented to the production planner. Notice that **MAD** serves as the *foundation* of the **Tracking Signal**, while **MSE** serves as the *foundation* of **RMSE** — illustrating a strict dependency hierarchy.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**Define the following forecast accuracy measures: (i) MAD, (ii) MSE, (iii) MAPE. State one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation):** It is the average of the absolute values of forecast errors over $n$ periods: $MAD = \dfrac{\sum \vert A_t - F_t \vert}{n}$. It is expressed in the same units as the data.
>
> *Limitation:* It is scale-dependent, so it cannot be used to compare forecast accuracy across products with different units or magnitudes.

> **(ii) MSE (Mean Squared Error):** It is the average of the squared forecast errors: $MSE = \dfrac{\sum (A_t - F_t)^{2}}{n}$.
>
> *Limitation:* Squaring produces *squared units*, which is not intuitive for managerial interpretation, and the metric is overly sensitive to outliers.

> **(iii) MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error):** It is the average of the absolute percentage errors: $MAPE = \dfrac{100}{n} \sum \vert \frac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \vert$.
>
> *Limitation:* It is undefined when actual demand $A_t = 0$ (division by zero) and asymmetrically penalizes under-forecasting more than over-forecasting at low actuals.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**Explain the concept of Tracking Signal in forecast control. What is the standard control limit and the corrective action when it is breached?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Tracking Signal (TS)** is a dimensionless ratio that detects whether a forecasting model is *in control* or *out of control*. It is defined as:
>
> $$TS = \frac{CSE}{MAD} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t \vert}$$
>
> where CSE is the **Cumulative Sum of Forecast Errors** and MAD is the **Mean Absolute Deviation**.
>
> **Standard Control Limit:** $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$.
>
> **Corrective Action when breached ($\vert TS \vert > 4$):** The forecaster must investigate the cause of bias — typically by (a) re-tuning the smoothing constants $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, (b) checking for special causes such as demand shifts or seasonal effects, or (c) replacing the model with a more sophisticated one (e.g., upgrading from simple exponential smoothing to Holt-Winters or ARIMA).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`**

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *A manufacturing firm has recorded the following quarterly demand for a product over the past 8 quarters. Forecasts were generated using a moving-average model. Compute: (i) the Mean Error (ME), (ii) Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD), (iii) Mean Squared Error (MSE), and (iv) the Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). Comment on the bias of the model.*

| Quarter | Actual ($A_t$) | Forecast ($F_t$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 200 | 210 |
| 2 | 220 | 215 |
| 3 | 240 | 225 |
| 4 | 260 | 240 |
| 5 | 230 | 250 |
| 6 | 250 | 240 |
| 7 | 270 | 255 |
| 8 | 290 | 270 |

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Per-period forecast error $e_t = A_t - F_t$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 200 - 210 = -10 \\
e_2 &= 220 - 215 = +5 \\
e_3 &= 240 - 225 = +15 \\
e_4 &= 260 - 240 = +20 \\
e_5 &= 230 - 250 = -20 \\
e_6 &= 250 - 240 = +10 \\
e_7 &= 270 - 255 = +15 \\
e_8 &= 290 - 270 = +20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Build the master table** (full computation):

| $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^{2}$ | $\vert e_t / A_t \vert \times 100$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 200 | 210 | -10 | 10 | 100 | 5.000 |
| 2 | 220 | 215 | +5 | 5 | 25 | 2.273 |
| 3 | 240 | 225 | +15 | 15 | 225 | 6.250 |
| 4 | 260 | 240 | +20 | 20 | 400 | 7.692 |
| 5 | 230 | 250 | -20 | 20 | 400 | 8.696 |
| 6 | 250 | 240 | +10 | 10 | 100 | 4.000 |
| 7 | 270 | 255 | +15 | 15 | 225 | 5.556 |
| 8 | 290 | 270 | +20 | 20 | 400 | 6.897 |
| **$\Sigma$** | — | — | **+55** | **115** | **1875** | **46.364** |

**Step 3: Compute each metric with $n = 8$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ME &= \frac{\sum e_t}{n} = \frac{+55}{8} = +6.875 \\
MAD &= \frac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n} = \frac{115}{8} = 14.375 \\
MSE &= \frac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n} = \frac{1875}{8} = 234.375 \\
MAPE &= \frac{100}{n} \sum \left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert = \frac{46.364}{8} = 5.795\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Bias Commentary**

Since $ME = +6.875 > 0$, the model exhibits a **systematic under-forecasting bias** — it consistently predicts *less* than actual demand. This is corroborated by 6 of 8 errors being positive. The model should be **upward-adjusted** by either re-tuning the smoothing constant or upgrading to a trend-aware model (e.g., Holt's linear trend method).

**(b) [7 Marks]** *What is the Tracking Signal? Compute the Tracking Signal at the end of Quarter 8 using the data above. Is the model in control? Justify with KTU's standard control criterion.*

**Model Solution:**

The Tracking Signal is defined as:

$$TS = \frac{CSE}{MAD} = \frac{\sum e_t}{\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t \vert}$$

**Step 1: CSE** (Cumulative Sum of Errors) at Quarter 8:

$$CSE_8 = \sum_{t=1}^{8} e_t = +55$$

**Step 2: MAD** (already computed) = 14.375

**Step 3: Tracking Signal computation:**

$$TS_8 = \frac{CSE_8}{MAD} = \frac{+55}{14.375} = +3.826$$

**Step 4: Control Decision**

The KTU standard control limit is $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$.

Since $\vert TS_8 \vert = 3.826 < 4$, **the model is currently IN CONTROL**. However, the positive bias is approaching the limit, so the forecaster should remain alert and consider proactive model re-tuning before the Tracking Signal breaches the limit in subsequent periods.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Define MSE, RMSE, and MAPE. The actual and forecasted sales (in lakhs ₹) for a 6-month period are given. Calculate MSE, RMSE, and MAPE. Comment on which model is best suited if management prefers a single percentage-based metric.*

| Month | Actual Sales | Forecasted Sales |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 50 | 45 |
| 2 | 55 | 50 |
| 3 | 60 | 65 |
| 4 | 45 | 50 |
| 5 | 70 | 60 |
| 6 | 65 | 70 |

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **MSE:** $MSE = \dfrac{\sum (A_t - F_t)^2}{n}$ — penalizes large errors quadratically.
- **RMSE:** $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ — restores original units.
- **MAPE:** $MAPE = \dfrac{100}{n} \sum \vert \frac{A_t - F_t}{A_t} \vert$ — scale-free percentage measure.

**Step 1: Compute errors**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 50 - 45 = +5, \quad \vert e_1 \vert = 5, \quad e_1^2 = 25, \quad \%E_1 = 10.000 \\
e_2 &= 55 - 50 = +5, \quad \vert e_2 \vert = 5, \quad e_2^2 = 25, \quad \%E_2 = 9.091 \\
e_3 &= 60 - 65 = -5, \quad \vert e_3 \vert = 5, \quad e_3^2 = 25, \quad \%E_3 = 8.333 \\
e_4 &= 45 - 50 = -5, \quad \vert e_4 \vert = 5, \quad e_4^2 = 25, \quad \%E_4 = 11.111 \\
e_5 &= 70 - 60 = +10, \quad \vert e_5 \vert = 10, \quad e_5^2 = 100, \quad \%E_5 = 14.286 \\
e_6 &= 65 - 70 = -5, \quad \vert e_6 \vert = 5, \quad e_6^2 = 25, \quad \%E_6 = 7.692
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Aggregations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum e_t^2 &= 25+25+25+25+100+25 = 225 \\
\sum \vert e_t \vert &= 5+5+5+5+10+5 = 35 \\
\sum \%E_t &= 10.000+9.091+8.333+11.111+14.286+7.692 = 60.513
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply formulas with $n = 6$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MSE &= \frac{225}{6} = 37.50 \;\; (\text{₹ lakhs})^2 \\
RMSE &= \sqrt{37.50} = 6.124 \;\; \text{₹ lakhs} \\
MAPE &= \frac{60.513}{6} = 10.086\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best Metric for Management:** Since management prefers a **single percentage-based metric** that is intuitive and comparable across product lines, **MAPE is the most suitable metric** — it directly conveys that the forecast is, on average, off by ~10% from actual sales, in a language that non-technical executives understand immediately.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain why MAD alone is insufficient for forecast control. With reference to the Tracking Signal, describe how MAD and CSE together enable early detection of model bias. What corrective actions should be triggered when $\vert TS \vert > 4$?*

**Model Solution:**

**Why MAD Alone Is Insufficient:** MAD only captures the *average magnitude* of forecast error — it ignores whether errors are systematically positive or negative. A model with MAD = 10 could be equally off in both directions (well-balanced) or consistently under-forecasting by 10 every period (highly biased). MAD cannot distinguish between these two scenarios.

**How MAD and CSE Together Enable Bias Detection:** The **Cumulative Sum of Errors (CSE)** accumulates the *signed* errors over time. When the model is unbiased, errors cancel out and CSE hovers near zero. When the model is consistently biased (e.g., persistent under-forecasting), CSE grows steadily in one direction. By dividing CSE by MAD, the **Tracking Signal (TS)** normalizes this cumulative bias by the typical error magnitude, yielding a unitless control statistic with well-defined limits.

**Corrective Actions when $\vert TS \vert > 4$:**

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall**
> Examiners repeatedly observe students losing 2–3 marks on this sub-question because they list *generic* fixes. The KTU-marker wants **specific, model-tiered** corrective actions:
>
> 1. **First-tier fix — Re-tune smoothing constants:** Increase $\alpha$ (for simple exponential smoothing), or adjust $\beta$ and $\gamma$ (for Holt-Winters) to make the model more responsive to recent demand shifts.
>
> 2. **Second-tier fix — Investigate special causes:** Use **fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams** or **Pareto analysis** to identify whether demand shift is due to a known event (festivals, competitor exit, price change, weather).
>
> 3. **Third-tier fix — Model upgrade:** Migrate from simple exponential smoothing to **Holt's linear trend** (if trend exists), **Holt-Winters** (if seasonality exists), or **ARIMA/SARIMA** (if autocorrelation is significant).
>
> 4. **Fourth-tier fix — Add judgmental override:** Apply **executive judgment adjustment** to model forecasts if domain knowledge suggests a structural shift (e.g., a new product launch, regulatory change).
>
> 5. **Reset Tracking Signal baseline:** Once corrective action is applied, reset CSE to zero and re-compute TS over the new control period.

A complete answer covering all five tiers will fetch **full 7 marks**; missing the *specificity* of fixes typically costs 2 marks even when the logic is otherwise correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Measures of Forecast Accuracy**

- **Forecast Error:** $e_t = A_t - F_t$; positive = under-forecasting, negative = over-forecasting.
- **ME (Mean Error):** $\dfrac{\sum e_t}{n}$ — a *bias* indicator, **not** an accuracy measure; can be zero for terrible forecasts due to cancellation.
- **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation):** $\dfrac{\sum \vert e_t \vert}{n}$ — the *workhorse* scale-dependent measure; same units as data.
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** $\dfrac{\sum e_t^{2}}{n}$ — penalizes large errors quadratically; foundation of least-squares and ARIMA.
- **RMSE (Root MSE):** $\sqrt{MSE}$ — same units as data; always $\geq$ MAD.
- **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error):** $\dfrac{100}{n}\sum \vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \vert$ — scale-free, ideal for executive dashboards; **undefined when $A_t = 0$**.
- **CSE / RSFE:** Running sum of errors; detects persistent bias direction.
- **Tracking Signal:** $TS = \dfrac{CSE}{MAD}$; dimensionless; **control limit $\vert TS \vert \leq 4$**.
- **Out-of-control signal** ($\vert TS \vert > 4$) triggers **five-tier corrective action**: re-tune → investigate causes → upgrade model → add judgment → reset baseline.
- **Comparative Logic:** Use **MAD/RMSE** for same-product model comparison; use **MAPE** for cross-product comparison; use **ME/TS** for bias detection and control.
- **Worked Numerical Mastery:** KTU ESE consistently awards 7+ marks for hand-calculation of at least 4 measures with proper tabulation — never skip the master table.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aggregate Planning and Master Production Scheduling: Basic Strategies for Meeting Uneven Demand, Techniques for Aggregate Planning, Master Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET612 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING AND PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aggregate Planning and Master Production Scheduling: Basic Strategies for Meeting Uneven Demand, Techniques for Aggregate Planning, Master Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aggregate Planning (AP) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a *medium-range* (typically 3 to 18 months) operational planning activity that determines the overall levels of **production output, inventory, workforce, and subcontracting** in order to meet forecast demand while **minimizing total cost** across the planning horizon. It deals with aggregated product families (e.g., "family A refrigerators" rather than individual SKUs) and uses a single, common unit of measure such as units, hours, or tons.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme hierarchy of production planning, Aggregate Planning sits precisely between **Long-Range Planning** (capacity, facilities, capital) and **Short-Range Planning** (daily scheduling, dispatching). It is the **bridging layer** that translates strategic business decisions into actionable production plans.

## 1.2 Master Production Schedule (MPS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a *short-to-medium range* time-phased plan (typically weeks, but extending 3–12 months) that specifies the **quantity and timing of finished products (end items)** to be produced. The MPS is the *dis-aggregated output* of the Aggregate Plan — it breaks the family-level plan into specific finished goods and serves as the **direct input to the Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** system.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bus Route Manager"

Imagine you are the manager of a **Kerala State Road Transport (KSRTC) bus depot** that runs services between **Thiruvananthapuram and Kochi**:

- **Aggregate Planning** is like deciding, *6 months in advance*, the *total number of buses* you will operate, the *total driver hours* you will book, and the *fuel budget* for the entire fleet during the Onam rush, the Sabarimala season, and the lean summer months. You are not assigning *which specific bus* goes *at which exact minute* — you are matching **aggregate capacity to aggregate demand**.

- **Master Production Schedule** is like the *weekly timetable* — bus #KL-15-4521 departs Trivandrum at 06:15 AM, bus #KL-15-7832 departs at 06:30 AM, and so on. It is the *detailed execution* of the aggregate plan.

- **Uneven demand** in this analogy is the difference between the **3 buses needed in May** versus the **12 buses needed during Onam week**. The aggregate plan decides *how* to handle this gap — do we hire more drivers, run more trips, lease buses from other depots, or accept some passengers being left behind (backorders)?

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams

- **Planning Horizon:** **3 to 18 months** (commonly 6 or 12 months in problems)
- **Period Granularity:** **Months** (sometimes weeks/quarters)
- **Unit of Measure:** units, labor-hours, machine-hours, or tons
- **Objective:** **Minimize Total Relevant Cost** (Production + Inventory + Workforce Change + Overtime/Undertime + Backorder + Subcontracting)
- **Demand Variability:** measured using **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)** and **forecast error**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Aggregate"?** Because the plan groups individual products into **product families** that share similar production processes, labor requirements, and material needs. This simplifies decision-making and reduces computational complexity. For example, a TV manufacturer may aggregate all 32-inch LED TVs into "Family A" rather than planning each model variant separately.

## 1.5 The Three Fundamental Planning Decisions

Every aggregate plan must answer three questions:

| Decision Area | Options / Levers |
|---|---|
| **Production Volume** | Make-to-stock, make-to-order, subcontract |
| **Workforce Level** | Hire, lay off, maintain steady workforce |
| **Inventory & Backorders** | Build inventory, allow backorders/lost sales |

The interaction of these three levers gives rise to the **basic strategies** we will study in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Planning Framework (KTU 2024)

Aggregate planning is part of a **4-tier hierarchy**:

$$
\text{Long-Range (Capacity, Facilities)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Aggregate Plan} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Master Schedule} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Short-Range (Daily Schedules)}
$$

The aggregate plan *receives* demand forecasts and capacity constraints and *outputs* production rates, workforce levels, and inventory targets.

## 2.2 Basic Strategies for Meeting Uneven Demand

### Strategy 1 — Level Strategy (Constant Workload)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Produce at a *constant rate* throughout the planning horizon. Demand fluctuations are absorbed entirely by **inventory build-up** (when demand is low) and **inventory depletion / backorders** (when demand is high). The workforce and production rate remain unchanged.

**How it works:**
- Compute the **average demand** across all periods.
- Produce exactly that average every period.
- Carry the **surplus as inventory**; absorb the **deficit as backorder**.

**Why use it:** Stable workforce, predictable quality, lower hiring/training costs, smoother supplier schedules.

**Why avoid it:** High carrying costs during low-demand months, customer service risk during peak.

### Strategy 2 — Chase Strategy (Variable Workload)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Match the *production rate exactly to the demand in each period* by **hiring and laying off workers** as needed. Inventory is theoretically zero (or minimal). The workforce fluctuates in direct proportion to demand.

**How it works:**
- In each period, the production rate = period demand.
- Workforce is adjusted to match: hire when demand rises, lay off when demand falls.
- No inventory carries, but heavy workforce churn.

**Why use it:** Zero inventory cost, no backorders, ideal for high variety / make-to-order.

**Why avoid it:** Very high hiring/training/layoff costs, morale issues, skill loss.

### Strategy 3 — Mixed / Hybrid Strategy

**Definition:** Combine elements of both level and chase strategies. Use a combination of **overtime, undertime, subcontracting, part-time workers, and modest inventory build-up** to fine-tune the plan. **This is the most commonly used strategy in practice** and is the most frequent answer in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 Decision Variables and Cost Levers

The aggregate planner manipulates **six cost categories**:

| Cost Lever | Symbol | When Incurred |
|---|---|---|
| **Regular-time production cost** | $C_{RT}$ | Every unit produced in regular hours |
| **Overtime production cost** | $C_{OT}$ | Units produced beyond regular capacity |
| **Inventory holding cost** | $C_{H}$ | Units carried from one period to next |
| **Backorder / shortage cost** | $C_{B}$ | Demand not met on time |
| **Hiring cost** | $C_{Hire}$ | Workers added |
| **Layoff / firing cost** | $C_{Fire}$ | Workers removed |
| **Subcontracting cost** | $C_{Sub}$ | Outsourced production |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula for KTU Aggregate Planning Problems**

$$
\boxed{\;TC = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\Big[ P_t \cdot C_{RT} + OT_t \cdot C_{OT} + I_t \cdot C_{H} + B_t \cdot C_{B} + \Delta W_t^{+} \cdot C_{Hire} + \Delta W_t^{-} \cdot C_{Fire} + S_t \cdot C_{Sub}\Big]\;}
$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Regular production in period $t$
- $OT_t$ = Overtime production in period $t$
- $I_t$ = Ending inventory in period $t$
- $B_t$ = Backorders in period $t$
- $\Delta W_t^{+}$ = Workers hired in period $t$
- $\Delta W_t^{-}$ = Workers laid off in period $t$
- $S_t$ = Units subcontracted in period $t$

### Core Operational Equations

**Inventory Balance Equation** (the most-tested KTU formula):

$$
\boxed{\;I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t + OT_t + S_t - D_t - B_{t-1} + B_t\;}
$$

**Workforce Balance Equation:**

$$
W_t = W_{t-1} + \Delta W_t^{+} - \Delta W_t^{-}
$$

**Production per Worker per Period:**

$$
P_t = W_t \cdot H \cdot p
$$

where $H$ = hours worked per worker per period, $p$ = production rate (units/hour).

**Average Production Rate for Level Strategy:**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} D_t}{n} = \dfrac{\text{Total Demand}}{\text{Number of Periods}}
$$

**Required Workforce for Level Strategy:**

$$
W_{req} = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{H \cdot p}
$$

**Cumulative Production & Inventory Check:**

$$
I_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (P_i - D_i) + I_0
$$

### Master Production Schedule Formulae

**Available-to-Promise (ATP):**

$$
ATP_t = \text{On-hand inventory at } t + \text{Scheduled receipts at } t - \text{Committed orders before next MPS receipt}
$$

**Projected On-Hand Inventory (POHI):**

$$
POHI_t = POHI_{t-1} + MPS_t - \text{Forecast}_t
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki):** Aggregate plans balance production across SUVs, sedans, and EVs over 12 months.
- **Apparel Industry (e.g., Kitex Garments, Kerala):** Aggregate plans match seasonal festival demand (Onam, Vishu, Christmas) with workforce flexibility.
- **Electronics (e.g., Samsung, Apple):** MPS drives the global supply chain — each iPhone model's weekly production is on the MPS.
- **Steel & Cement (e.g., SAIL, UltraTech):** Aggregate plans are typically in tons — capacity, inventory silos, and dispatch.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Problems & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** Numerical aggregate planning problems are the *highest-weightage* question in this module. The standard 14-mark question presents demand for 6 months and 4–5 cost parameters, and requires evaluation of **Level, Chase, and Mixed strategies** with a cost comparison table.

## 3.1 Solved Problem 1 — Level vs. Chase Strategy (Full Solution)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern Question]**
>
> A manufacturing firm forecasts the following demand for its product over the next **6 months**: 500, 700, 900, 800, 600, 400 units respectively. The firm currently has **5 workers**, each producing **50 units per month**. Holding cost = **Rs. 5/unit/month**, backorder cost = **Rs. 10/unit/month**, hiring cost = **Rs. 200/worker**, layoff cost = **Rs. 250/worker**, regular-time production cost = **Rs. 20/unit**. The current inventory is **100 units** and there are **no backorders**. Compare the **Level Strategy** and the **Chase Strategy**. Which is cheaper?

### Step 1 — Compute Total Demand and Average

$$
D_{total} = 500 + 700 + 900 + 800 + 600 + 400 = 3900 \text{ units}
$$

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{3900}{6} = 650 \text{ units/month}
$$

### Step 2 — Required Workforce for Level Strategy

$$
W_{req} = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{p} = \dfrac{650}{50} = 13 \text{ workers}
$$

Since current workforce = 5, we must **hire 8 workers** at the start.

$$
\text{Hiring Cost} = 8 \times 200 = \text{Rs. } 1600
$$

### Step 3 — Build the Level Strategy Inventory Table

Use $I_t = I_{t-1} + P - D_t$:

| Month | Demand $D_t$ | Production $P$ | Begin Inv. $I_{t-1}$ | End Inv. $I_t$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 500 | 650 | 100 | 250 |
| 2 | 700 | 650 | 250 | 200 |
| 3 | 900 | 650 | 200 | **−50** (Backorder) |
| 4 | 800 | 650 | −50 | −200 (Backorder) |
| 5 | 600 | 650 | −200 | 0 |
| 6 | 400 | 650 | 0 | **+250** |

Wait — at the *end* of month 6 we still carry 250 units. We must convert to a closed-loop: the ending inventory of month 6 = beginning of month 1's prior 100 plus cumulative 3900–3900 = 0. But the table above gives a different answer because production 650 × 6 = 3900 and demand = 3900, so *net* inventory = 0. Let me recheck the cumulative sum.

### Step 3 (Corrected) — Closed-Loop Level Strategy

Production each month = 650. Starting inventory = 100. Use **cumulative net** $(I_0 + \Sigma(P-D))$:

$$
\text{Cum}_t = 100 + \sum_{i=1}^{t}(650 - D_i)
$$

| Month | $D_t$ | $P$ | Cum $I_t$ | Notes |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|---|
| 1 | 500 | 650 | 100 + 150 = **250** | Inventory |
| 2 | 700 | 650 | 250 − 50 = **200** | Inventory |
| 3 | 900 | 650 | 200 − 250 = **−50** | Backorder |
| 4 | 800 | 650 | −50 − 150 = **−200** | Backorder |
| 5 | 600 | 650 | −200 + 50 = **−150** | Backorder |
| 6 | 400 | 650 | −150 + 250 = **100** | Closes to 100 |

### Step 4 — Total Cost Calculation for Level Strategy

**Production cost (regular):** Total production = 650 × 6 = 3900 units

$$
C_{prod} = 3900 \times 20 = \text{Rs. } 78{,}000
$$

**Inventory holding cost** (sum of *positive* inventory months):

$$
C_H = (250 + 200) \times 5 = \text{Rs. } 2250
$$

**Backorder cost** (sum of *absolute* backorder levels):

$$
C_B = (50 + 200 + 150) \times 10 = \text{Rs. } 4000
$$

**Hiring cost (one-time at start):**

$$
C_{Hire} = 8 \times 200 = \text{Rs. } 1600
$$

**Layoff cost:** Rs. 0 (workforce is steady at 13).

**Total Cost (Level Strategy):**

$$
TC_{Level} = 78{,}000 + 2250 + 4000 + 1600 = \boxed{\text{Rs. } 85{,}850}
$$

### Step 5 — Chase Strategy

Workforce = $D_t / 50$ each month, hiring/firing to track demand.

| Month | $D_t$ | Workers $W_t$ | Change | Hire/Fire |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 500 | 10 | +5 | Hire 5 |
| 2 | 700 | 14 | +4 | Hire 4 |
| 3 | 900 | 18 | +4 | Hire 4 |
| 4 | 800 | 16 | −2 | Fire 2 |
| 5 | 600 | 12 | −4 | Fire 4 |
| 6 | 400 | 8 | −4 | Fire 4 |

### Step 6 — Total Cost for Chase Strategy

**Production cost:** Same 3900 × 20 = Rs. 78,000

**Inventory & Backorder:** Zero (by definition of chase).

**Hiring cost:** $(5+4+4) \times 200 = 13 \times 200 = \text{Rs. } 2600$

**Layoff cost:** $(2+4+4) \times 250 = 10 \times 250 = \text{Rs. } 2500$

$$
TC_{Chase} = 78{,}000 + 2600 + 2500 = \boxed{\text{Rs. } 83{,}100}
$$

### Step 7 — Comparison and Decision

| Cost Component | Level | Chase |
|---|:-:|:-:|
| Production | 78,000 | 78,000 |
| Inventory | 2,250 | 0 |
| Backorder | 4,000 | 0 |
| Hiring | 1,600 | 2,600 |
| Layoff | 0 | 2,500 |
| **Total** | **85,850** | **83,100** |

**Decision:** **Chase strategy is cheaper by Rs. 2,750** in this scenario. However, the Mixed Strategy is recommended if overtime/subcontracting is allowed — it would push cost even lower.

## 3.2 Solved Problem 2 — Mixed Strategy with Overtime

> **[Modified KTU Board Pattern]**
>
> Given the same problem, allow **overtime up to 100 units/month** at **Rs. 30/unit**. Build a mixed strategy that uses **overtime in months 3 and 4** to avoid backorders, then recompute total cost.

### Step 1 — Maintain Level Workforce (13 workers = 650/period)

### Step 2 — Use Overtime to Cover Peaks

Months 3 and 4 need 900 and 800. With regular 650:
- Month 3: 650 + 250 OT (but cap is 100) → use 100 OT + accept 150 backorder OR subcontract.
- Let's allow 100 OT max and use subcontract for the rest.

**Revised plan:** Month 3 — Produce 650 regular + 100 OT + 150 subcontract; Month 4 — 650 + 100 OT + 50 subcontract.

### Step 3 — Cost Recalculation

$$
C_{OT} = (100 + 100) \times 30 = \text{Rs. } 6000
$$

$$
C_{Sub} = (150 + 50) \times 25 \text{ (assume)} = \text{Rs. } 5000
$$

All inventory/backorder → zero.

$$
TC_{Mixed} = 78{,}000 + 1600 + 6000 + 5000 = \boxed{\text{Rs. } 90{,}600}
$$

In this case, Mixed is *more expensive* than Chase. **The optimal strategy depends entirely on the cost parameters** — a key insight KTU examiners test.

## 3.3 Master Production Schedule Construction

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern — 7 Mark Sub-Question]**
>
> A company has forecasted weekly demand for product **X** as: Week 1 = 100, Week 2 = 150, Week 3 = 200, Week 4 = 150. Current on-hand inventory = 80 units. Lot-size rule: **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)**. Safety stock = 0. Build the MPS.

### Step 1 — Compute Projected On-Hand Inventory

$$
POHI_t = POHI_{t-1} + MPS_t - \text{Forecast}_t
$$

| Week | Forecast | Beginning Inv. | MPS (Planned Order Receipts) | Ending POHI |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 (start) | — | — | — | 80 |
| 1 | 100 | 80 | 100 (L4L) | 80 |
| 2 | 150 | 80 | 150 | 80 |
| 3 | 200 | 80 | 200 | 80 |
| 4 | 150 | 80 | 150 | 80 |

### Step 2 — Available-to-Promise (ATP)

If a customer orders 60 units in Week 1:

$$
ATP_1 = 80 + 100 - 60 = 120 \text{ units available}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key MPS Insight:** The MPS is **time-phased** (each week has a firm quantity), **dis-aggregated** (specific end items, not families), and acts as the **interface between demand and supply**. It is the *only* system that can truly commit to customers.

## 3.4 Time Fences in MPS (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Time Fences:**
> 1. **Demand Time Fence (DTF):** The period within which customer orders drive the schedule. *No changes* allowed to MPS quantities in this zone.
> 2. **Planning Time Fence (PTF):** Inside this zone, the MPS quantities are *firm* but the *timing* of orders can shift slightly.
> 3. **Frozen Zone:** Beyond PTF — orders can be added, deleted, or rescheduled freely.

$$
\text{Frozen Zone} \;\supset\; \text{Planning Time Fence} \;\supset\; \text{Demand Time Fence}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aggregate Planning Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Long Range Business Plan] --> B[Demand Forecast + Capacity]
    B --> C[Aggregate Planning]
    C --> D{Choose Strategy}
    D --> E1[Level Strategy]
    D --> E2[Chase Strategy]
    D --> E3[Mixed Hybrid Strategy]
    E1 --> F[Cost Computation]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Compare Total Cost]
    G --> H[Select Minimum Cost Plan]
    H --> I[Master Production Schedule MPS]
    I --> J[Material Requirements Planning MRP]
    J --> K[Shop Floor Scheduling]
    K --> L[Production Execution]
    L --> M[Feedback to Forecast]
```

## 4.2 Master Production Schedule Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Aggregate Plan Families] --> B[MPS End Items]
    B --> C[Time Phased Weekly Buckets]
    C --> D[Frozen Zone DTF]
    C --> E[Slushy Zone PTF]
    C --> F[Fluid Zone]
    D --> G[Customer Orders Drive]
    E --> H[Quantity Firm Timing Adjustable]
    F --> I[Free Rescheduling]
    G --> J[MRP Explosion]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Work Orders to Shop Floor]
```

## 4.3 Strategy Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uneven Demand] --> B{Strategy Selection}
    B -->|Low HireFire Cost| C1[Chase Strategy]
    B -->|High Inventory Cost| C1
    B -->|High HireFire Cost| C2[Level Strategy]
    B -->|Low Inventory Cost| C2
    B -->|Balanced Costs| C3[Mixed Strategy]
    C1 --> D1[Variable Workforce]
    C2 --> D2[Constant Workforce + Inventory]
    C3 --> D3[OT Subcontracting PartTime]
    D1 --> E[Optimal Plan Selected]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define Aggregate Planning. List the three basic strategies used to meet uneven demand.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Aggregate Planning (AP)** is a medium-range (3–18 month) planning process that determines the overall production quantity, workforce level, and inventory to meet forecast demand at minimum total cost, treating products as **aggregated families** (1 mark).
>
> The three basic strategies are:
> 1. **Level Strategy** — constant production rate, inventory absorbs demand swings (1 mark).
> 2. **Chase Strategy** — production matches demand period-by-period, workforce varies (½ mark).
> 3. **Mixed/Hybrid Strategy** — combines overtime, subcontracting, part-time workers, and inventory (½ mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**What is a Master Production Schedule (MPS)? State any two differences between an Aggregate Plan and an MPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is a time-phased plan that specifies the *quantity* and *timing* of specific **finished end-items** to be produced, typically in weekly buckets, and serves as the direct input to MRP (1½ marks).
>
> **Differences:**
> | Aspect | Aggregate Plan | MPS |
> |---|---|---|
> | Granularity | Product *families* | Specific *end items* (1 mark) |
> | Time bucket | Months | Weeks (½ mark) |
> | Purpose | Capacity planning | Drives MRP & shop floor (½ mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in aggregate planning numerical problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert backorders properly** — students add backorder cost to inventory cost in the same period, instead of using the absolute backorder level × backorder rate.
> 2. **Confusing "ending inventory" with "average inventory"** — KTU problems use *ending inventory* unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Miscalculating the chase workforce** — remember $W_t = D_t / \text{units per worker}$ rounded appropriately.
> 4. **Not including the opening inventory adjustment** in the cumulative balance.
> 5. **Forgetting that production cost is constant** across strategies when total units are the same — only *relevant* costs differ. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for redundant production-cost columns.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Aggregate Planning Numerical**

> **[KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Question]**
>
> **(a)** A company manufactures a single product. The demand forecast for the next 6 months is: **1200, 900, 1000, 1400, 1500, 1100 units**. The company has **40 workers** at the start, each producing **20 units/month**. Other data: Regular production cost = **Rs. 15/unit**, Overtime cost = **Rs. 22/unit** (max 200 units/month overtime), Holding cost = **Rs. 4/unit/month**, Backorder cost = **Rs. 8/unit/month**, Hiring cost = **Rs. 300/worker**, Layoff cost = **Rs. 400/worker**, Starting inventory = **200 units**, No backorders initially.
>
> **Develop the Level Strategy and Chase Strategy, compute total cost for each, and recommend the better option.** **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Total Demand**

$$
D_{total} = 1200 + 900 + 1000 + 1400 + 1500 + 1100 = 7100 \text{ units}
$$

$$
P_{avg} = 7100/6 \approx 1183 \text{ units/month}
$$

**Step 2 — Required Workforce (Level)**

$$
W_{req} = 1183/20 \approx 59.16 \rightarrow 60 \text{ workers}
$$

*Hire 20 workers at start* → Cost = $20 \times 300 = \text{Rs. } 6000$

**Step 3 — Level Strategy Inventory Table** (using $I_t = I_{t-1} + P - D_t$, $P = 1200$):

| Month | $D_t$ | $P$ | $I_{t-1}$ | $I_t$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1200 | 1200 | 200 | 200 |
| 2 | 900 | 1200 | 200 | 500 |
| 3 | 1000 | 1200 | 500 | 700 |
| 4 | 1400 | 1200 | 700 | 500 |
| 5 | 1500 | 1200 | 500 | 200 |
| 6 | 1100 | 1200 | 200 | 300 |

**Total positive inventory = 200+500+700+500+200+300 = 2400 unit-months**

**Step 4 — Level Strategy Cost**

$$
C_{prod} = 7100 \times 15 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}06{,}500
$$

$$
C_H = 2400 \times 4 = \text{Rs. } 9600
$$

$$
C_B = 0
$$

$$
C_{Hire} = 6000
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{Level} = 1{,}06{,}500 + 9600 + 0 + 6000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}22{,}100}
$$

**Step 5 — Chase Strategy** ($W_t = D_t/20$):

| Month | $D_t$ | $W_t$ | $\Delta W$ | Hire/Fire |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1200 | 60 | +20 | Hire 20 |
| 2 | 900 | 45 | −15 | Fire 15 |
| 3 | 1000 | 50 | +5 | Hire 5 |
| 4 | 1400 | 70 | +20 | Hire 20 |
| 5 | 1500 | 75 | +5 | Hire 5 |
| 6 | 1100 | 55 | −20 | Fire 20 |

**Step 6 — Chase Strategy Cost**

$$
C_{prod} = 1{,}06{,}500
$$

$$
C_{Hire} = (20+5+20+5) \times 300 = 50 \times 300 = \text{Rs. } 15{,}000
$$

$$
C_{Fire} = (15+20) \times 400 = 35 \times 400 = \text{Rs. } 14{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{TC_{Chase} = 1{,}06{,}500 + 15{,}000 + 14{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 1{,}35{,}500}
$$

**Recommendation:** **Level Strategy is better** (saves Rs. 13,400). For this problem, the **inventory holding cost is low** and **workforce change costs are high**, so a steady workforce with inventory build-up wins. ✅

---

> **(b)** Explain any **three techniques** used for aggregate planning. Compare **graphical methods** with **mathematical (optimization) methods**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

The major techniques for aggregate planning are:

**1. Graphical / Charting Methods (Heuristic):**
- Plot cumulative demand vs. cumulative production on a graph.
- Adjust production rate, workforce, and inventory visually.
- Simple, intuitive, suitable for small problems; **does not guarantee optimal solution** (1 mark).
- Example: *Chase* line vs. *Level* line on a cumulative chart (½ mark).

**2. Mathematical Optimization Methods:**
- **Linear Programming (LP):** Minimizes $TC = \sum(C_{RT}P_t + C_{OT}OT_t + C_H I_t + C_B B_t + C_{Hire}\Delta W^+ + C_{Fire}\Delta W^-)$ subject to inventory balance and capacity constraints (1 mark).
- **Transportation Method:** Formulates AP as a transportation problem with sources = production levels and destinations = demand periods; unit costs include production, holding, and backorder (1 mark).
- **Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)** and **Dynamic Programming** handle more complex real-world cases (½ mark).

**3. Simulation Methods:**
- Models the production system as a stochastic simulation (e.g., Monte Carlo).
- Useful when demand is highly variable and relationships are non-linear.
- Computationally expensive (½ mark).

**4. Spreadsheet / Trial-and-Error Heuristics:**
- Manager constructs and tests alternative plans in Excel.
- Practical and widely used in small/medium firms (½ mark).

**Comparison Table (Mandatory for full marks):**

| Aspect | Graphical Method | Mathematical Method |
|---|---|---|
| Optimality | Approximate / heuristic | Guaranteed optimum (for LP) |
| Data requirement | Low | High — all costs needed |
| Complexity | Simple, manual | Requires solver software |
| Real-world factors | Hard to incorporate | Easy to add constraints |
| Use case | Quick decision-making | Strategic, large-scale planning | (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Master Production Scheduling & Mixed Strategy**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**. With a suitable example, describe the procedure to construct an MPS for a product with **Lot-for-Lot (L4L)** and **Minimum 500 lot-size** rules. The forecast for 4 weeks is: 350, 400, 500, 450 units. Beginning inventory = 250 units. **Compare the inventory levels** under both lot-sizing rules. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**MPS Concept (2 marks):**
- MPS is a *detailed, time-phased* plan listing the *quantity* of each *finished product* to be produced in each period.
- It is derived from the Aggregate Plan (family-level) and feeds the MRP system.
- The MPS *commits* to customers — orders are firm in the demand time fence.

**Construction Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Convert the aggregate family plan into end-item forecasts.
2. Apply the chosen lot-sizing rule (L4L, EOQ, min lot size, etc.).
3. Compute POHI each period: $POHI_t = POHI_{t-1} + MPS_t - Forecast_t$.
4. Verify POHI ≥ 0 (or ≥ safety stock) at every period.
5. Compute ATP for customer order promising.

**L4L Rule (1 mark):** $MPS_t = Forecast_t$.

**Min 500 Rule (1 mark):** $MPS_t = \text{Max}(Forecast_t, 500)$ — produce in multiples of 500 or 500 minimum.

**MPS Table (1 mark):**

| Week | Forecast | L4L MPS | Min-500 MPS | L4L POHI | Min-500 POHI |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | — | — | — | 250 | 250 |
| 1 | 350 | 350 | 500 | 250 | 400 |
| 2 | 400 | 400 | 500 | 250 | 500 |
| 3 | 500 | 500 | 500 | 250 | 500 |
| 4 | 450 | 450 | 500 | 250 | 550 |

**Comparison (½ mark):**
- **L4L** maintains exactly 250 units POHI throughout — zero extra inventory.
- **Min-500** builds inventory (550 units at end of week 4) — better production efficiency but higher holding cost.

---

> **(b)** Discuss the **basic strategies for meeting uneven demand**. Develop a **Mixed Strategy** for the following problem: Demand = 1000, 800, 1200, 1500, 1300, 900 over 6 months. Workforce = 30 workers producing 30 units/month each. Overtime = 10 units/worker/month max at 1.5× regular rate. Regular cost = Rs. 10/unit. Hiring cost = Rs. 500/worker. Subcontracting = Rs. 18/unit (any amount). Starting inventory = 200 units. Holding cost = Rs. 3/unit/month. Show that the mixed strategy may not be the cheapest if subcontracting is expensive. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Three Basic Strategies (2 marks):**

1. **Level:** Constant production, inventory absorbs variation.
2. **Chase:** Production = demand; workforce varies.
3. **Mixed:** Combination of OT, subcontract, part-time, inventory.

**Mixed Strategy Design (3 marks):**
- Base production = 30 workers × 30 units = 900/month (level).
- Total demand = 6700; base 5400 → deficit 1300.
- Use OT + subcontract to cover peak demand.

| Month | Demand | Regular | OT | Subcontract | End Inv. |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | 1000 | 900 | 100 | 0 | 200 (from 200+100) |
| 2 | 800 | 900 | 0 | 0 | 300 |
| 3 | 1200 | 900 | 100 | 0 | 100 |
| 4 | 1500 | 900 | 100 | 200 | −100 → Sub |
| 5 | 1300 | 900 | 100 | 200 | −200 → Sub |
| 6 | 900 | 900 | 0 | 0 | 100 |

**Cost Calculation (2 marks):**
- Regular: $5400 \times 10 = 54{,}000$
- OT: $400 \times 15 = 6{,}000$
- Sub: $400 \times 18 = 7{,}200$
- Holding: $\sim 1000 \times 3 = 3{,}000$
- **Total ≈ Rs. 70,200**

**Key Insight (½ mark):** When **subcontracting/unit cost > regular cost + holding cost**, mixed strategy loses to chase. The student must conclude that **strategy selection is data-driven**, not a universal rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Quick Recall — Read this 10 minutes before the exam**

- ✅ **Aggregate Plan** is **medium-range, family-level, monthly**; uses **single unit of measure**.
- ✅ **MPS** is **short-range, end-item, weekly**; drives **MRP** and **shop floor**.
- ✅ **Three strategies:** Level (steady work, inventory), Chase (variable work, no inventory), Mixed (OT + sub + part-time + inventory).
- ✅ **Inventory balance equation (most important):** $I_t = I_{t-1} + P_t + OT_t + S_t - D_t - B_{t-1} + B_t$
- ✅ **Workforce balance:** $W_t = W_{t-1} + \text{Hire}_t - \text{Fire}_t$
- ✅ **Total Cost =** Production + Holding + Backorder + Hiring + Firing + OT + Subcontracting.
- ✅ **Level production rate** = $\Sigma D_t / n$ periods.
- ✅ **Chase workforce** = $D_t / (\text{units per worker per period})$, rounded to whole number.
- ✅ **Strategy selection rule of thumb:** *Low hire/fire cost + high holding cost → Chase*; *High hire/fire cost + low holding cost → Level*; *Otherwise → Mixed*.
- ✅ **MPS** uses **POHI** = POHI(prev) + MPS − Forecast; must be ≥ safety stock.
- ✅ **ATP** = On-hand + Scheduled receipts − Committed customer orders.
- ✅ **Time Fences:** DTF (frozen) → PTF (slushy) → Fluid (free).
- ✅ **Lot-sizing rules:** L4L, EOQ, Min lot size, Period Order Quantity (POQ).
- ✅ **Aggregate planning techniques:** Graphical, Linear Programming, Transportation Method, Simulation, Spreadsheet Heuristic.
- ✅ **Transportation Method** is a *special LP structure*; rows = production levels, columns = demand periods, cell costs = production + holding or backorder.
- ✅ **Capacity** must equal or exceed demand in each period for the chosen strategy.
- ✅ **Backorder** is a *negative inventory* and incurs cost every period it persists.
- ✅ **Inventory carrying cost** is computed on *ending inventory* unless the problem says *average*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always draw a **cumulative net inventory graph** for level strategy problems — it instantly reveals the *minimum safety stock* required and the *maximum backorder* exposure. Examiners award **1 bonus mark** for a clear, labeled graph even when not explicitly asked.
